question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 1
A 26-year-old man comes to the office because of a 1-week history of
increased urinary frequency accompanied by excessive thirst. He says he
has been urinating hourly. Physical examination shows no abnormalities.
Serum chemistry studies are within the reference ranges. Urine osmolality
is 50 mOsmol/kg H2O. After administration of ADH (vasopressin), his urine
osmolality is within the reference range. The most likely cause of this
patient's symptoms is dysfunction of which of the following structures?
explanation:
Supraoptic nucleus
Findings are classic for central diabetes insipidus (CDI): hypotonic polyuria (urine
osmolality 50 mOsm/kg H2O) with prompt normalization of urine osmolality
after vasopressin/desmopressin, indicating intact renal responsiveness and
deficient endogenous AVP production/secretion.[1][2][3][4][5][6][7] AVP is
synthesized in magnocellular neurons of the supraoptic (and paraventricular)
nuclei, transported via the infundibulum, and released from the posterior
pituitary; dysfunction of the supraoptic nucleus impairs AVP synthesis,
producing CDI.[5][6]
Other options are inconsistent:
Anterior pituitary and hypophysial portal system: not involved in AVP
synthesis/release (posterior/neurohypophyseal function).[5][6]
Bowman capsule, glomerulus: do not explain selective correction with
exogenous AVP.
Loop of Henle: defects would impair medullary gradient; nephrogenic DI
would not correct with vasopressin.[1][2][3][4]
references: Neuroimaging of Central Diabetes Insipidus-When, How and Findings.

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 1
A 19-year-old man who is in the US Army is brought to the emergency
department 45 minutes after he sustained a knife wound to the right side
of his chest during an altercation. He has no history of major medical
illness and takes no medications. His temperature is 36.9°C (98.4°F), pulse
is 110/min, respirations are 24/min, and blood pressure is 114/76 mm Hg.
Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 94%. On
physical examination, the trachea appears to be shifted to the left.
Pulmonary examination of the right chest is most likely to show which of
the following findings?

options.A: Decreased Fremitus, dull Percussion, decreased breath sounds
options.C: Decreased Fremitus, hyperresonant Percussion, dull breath sounds
options.D: Increased Fremitus, dull Percussion, bronchial breath sounds
options.E: Increased Fremitus, dull Percussion, decreased breath sounds

explanation:
Decreased tactile fremitus, hyperresonant percussion, decreased breath
sounds[1][2][3][4][5]
Penetrating chest trauma with tachycardia, tachypnea, hypoxemia, and
contralateral tracheal deviation indicates tension pneumothorax on the right. On
exam, air in the pleural space impedes transmission of low-frequency vocal
vibrations (decreased tactile fremitus) and increases percussion resonance; the
collapsed lung transmits little airflow (decreased/absent breath sounds).[2][4]
The American College of Cardiology/American Heart Association guideline
section on physical examination notes unilateral absence of breath sounds in
pneumothorax, and clinical literature describes diminished breath sounds with
hyperresonance in large or tension pneumothorax.[1][3][5]
These findings contrast with:
Pleural effusion: decreased fremitus with dullness to percussion and
decreased breath sounds.[4]
Consolidation: increased fremitus with bronchial breath sounds and
dullness.[4]
Thus, the best answer is (B).
references: Clinical Presentation of Patients With Tension Pneumothorax: A Systematic

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 1
A 45-year-old man comes to the office for a follow-up examination 1 month
after routine urinalysis showed an increased protein concentration. He has
a 15-year history of type 2 diabetes mellitus and a 10-year history of
hypertension. Current medications include hydrochlorothiazide, insulin,
and metformin. His blood pressure is 150/80 mm Hg. Physical examination
shows no other abnormalities. Daily lisinopril therapy is started. Which of
the following sets of changes in glomerular hemodynamics is most likely to
occur in this patient as a result of this pharmacotherapy?

options.A: increased renal blood flow, increased ultrafiltration pressure, decreased
options.B: increased renal blood flow, decreased ultrafiltration pressure, increased
options.C: increased renal blood flow, decreased ultrafiltration pressure, decreased
options.D: decreased renal blood flow, increased ultrafiltration pressure,
options.E: decreased renal blood flow, decreased ultrafiltration pressure,

explanation:
(C) increased renal blood flow, decreased ultrafiltration pressure, decreased
glomerular filtration rate
Mechanism: ACE inhibition (lisinopril) preferentially dilates the efferent arteriole,
lowering glomerular capillary hydrostatic (ultrafiltration) pressure. Total renal
vascular resistance falls, so renal blood flow rises, but the drop in
intraglomerular pressure typically leads to a small early decline in GFR and
filtration fraction—an expected, functional change that underlies the
antiproteinuric effect in diabetic kidney disease with albuminuria.[1][2][3][4][5]
[6][7]
Clinical implications:
Antiproteinuric/renoprotective benefit is mediated by reduced
intraglomerular pressure and shear at the filtration barrier.[3][6][7]
A mild increase in serum creatinine (often ≤10–20%) may occur initially;
monitoring creatinine and potassium within 2–4 weeks (or 1–2 weeks in
higher-risk CKD) after initiation or dose uptitration is recommended. The
Kidney Disease: Improving Global Outcomes (KDIGO) guideline advises
continuing therapy unless creatinine rises >30% within 4 weeks or if
hyperkalemia/hypotension is uncontrolled.[8][9]
Summary of hemodynamics with lisinopril:
Efferent arteriolar dilation → decreased glomerular capillary pressure
(ultrafiltration pressure).[1][3]
Decreased renal vascular resistance → increased renal blood flow.[1][2][4]
Slight early decline or no change in GFR, commonly a small decline in
clinical practice.[1][4][5][10]
references: in Cardiovascular Disease and the Council for High Blood Pressure
Acute and Chronic Effects of Lisinopril on Renal and Systemic
Lisinopril. A Review of Its Pharmacology and Use in the Management of the
Meta-Analysis: Effect of Monotherapy and Combination Therapy With
Use of Renin-Angiotensin System Blockade in Advanced CKD: An NKF-

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 1
A 3438-g (7-lb 9-oz) female newborn is delivered in the hospital at term to
a 24-year-old primigravid woman after an uncomplicated pregnancy. Apgar
scores are 8 and 9 at 1 and 5 minutes, respectively. Examination of the
newborn shows ambiguous genitalia. Results of a blood analysis show
markedly increased serum concentrations of 17- hydroxyprogesterone.
Genetic analysis shows a karyotype of 46,XX. Further testing of this
newborn is most likely to show a deficiency of which of the following
enzymes?
explanation:
The clinical picture of a 46,XX neonate with ambiguous genitalia and markedly
elevated 17-hydroxyprogesterone is most consistent with classic congenital
adrenal hyperplasia due to 21-hydroxylase deficiency. Newborn screening and
diagnostic algorithms hinge on the marked elevation of 17-hydroxyprogesterone
in 21-hydroxylase deficiency; 11β-hydroxylase deficiency typically elevates 11-
deoxycortisol with mineralocorticoid hypertension, and 3β-hydroxysteroid
dehydrogenase deficiency shows a different steroid profile. In classic 21-
hydroxylase deficiency, impaired cortisol (± aldosterone) synthesis increases
ACTH, driving excess adrenal androgens and virilization in 46,XX infants.[1][2][3]
[4][5][6][7]
Answer: 21α-Hydroxylase
Contextual figure: The NEJM table below summarizes diagnostic features across
ages in 21-hydroxylase CAH, including infancy findings of markedly increased 17-
OHP and androgen excess with atypical genitalia in 46,XX, aligning with this
case.[1] The Endocrine Society (United States) also recommends cosyntropin
testing and comprehensive steroid profiling when needed to distinguish from
rarer enzyme defects.[2]
Table 1. Diagnosis and Treatment of Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia Due to 21-Hydroxylase Deficiency
(CAH) According to Age.*
Used under license from The New England Journal of Medicine.
Key points supporting 21-hydroxylase deficiency:
Markedly increased 17-hydroxyprogesterone is the biochemical hallmark of
21-hydroxylase CAH; most affected infants have levels well above 5000 ng/
dL, and diagnosis is confirmed with ACTH stimulation when needed.[1][2][3]
[5][6]
• 
46,XX infants present with virilized external genitalia due to prenatal
adrenal androgen excess; internal Müllerian structures are normal.[1][4][8]
21-hydroxylase deficiency accounts for >90% of CAH, distinguishing it from
rarer forms such as 11β-hydroxylase or 3β-HSD deficiencies with different
hormonal patterns.[1][4][6][7]
references: Diagnosis and Treatment of Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia Due to 21-Hydroxylase Deficiency
Recent Advances in the Diagnosis and Management of Congenital Adrenal
Quantitation of 17-Oh-Progesterone (OHPG) for Diagnosis of Congenital

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 1
A 27-year-old woman comes to the emergency department because of a 1-
hour history of severe shortness of breath. She has just returned from a
cross-country flight. She has a history of borderline hypertension. Her
temperature is 36.9°C (98.5°F), pulse is 113/min, respirations are 28/min,
and blood pressure is 138/85 mm Hg. Physical examination shows that the
right calf has an increased circumference compared with the left calf, and
there is tenderness behind the right knee. Which of the following is the
most likely underlying cause of this patient's condition?
explanation:
The presentation (acute dyspnea, tachycardia/tachypnea after a long-haul flight,
unilateral calf swelling/tenderness) is classic for VTE from DVT with possible PE,
precipitated by prolonged immobility and venous stasis during air travel.[1][2]
Among inherited thrombophilias, the most common in young adults is resistance
to activated protein C due to a factor V variant, which amplifies thrombin
generation and raises VTE risk; this risk is further potentiated by travel-related
stasis.[2][3][4][5][6] In contrast, Glanzmann thrombasthenia and von Willebrand
disease confer bleeding, not thrombosis.[7][8] Antithrombin and protein C
deficiencies are strong but less prevalent thrombophilias than factor V Leiden in
the general population.[3][5][9]
Answer: Factor V Leiden mutation.
The table below (from JAMA) summarizes established VTE risk factors, including
inherited thrombophilias such as factor V Leiden, and contextualizes travel-
related immobility as a provoking factor relevant to this case.[2]
Key supporting points:
Air-travel–associated VTE reflects Virchow’s triad, with venous stasis
playing a major role.[1][10][11]
Factor V Leiden is the most common inherited thrombophilia identified in
clinical practice; testing often begins with an APCR assay followed by
confirmatory genotyping when indicated.[2][3][4]
Compared with antithrombin or protein C deficiency, factor V Leiden
carries a more modest per-carrier risk but greater population-attributable
risk due to higher prevalence, making it the most likely underlying cause in
an otherwise healthy young traveler with suspected VTE.[2][3][5][6]
references: Prevention of VTE in Nonsurgical Patients: Antithrombotic Therapy and
Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guidelines. Kahn SR, Lim W, Dunn AS, et
Epidemiology, Etiology and Diagnosis of Venous Thrombosis. Gathof BS,
Hereditary Thrombophilia and Venous Thromboembolism. Murin S,

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 1
A 17-year-old boy is brought to the clinic for a follow-up examination. He
has been evaluated for three episodes of full-body weakness at the ages of
13, 16, and 17 years. Each episode occurred when he lay down after playing
in a football scrimmage. The weakness improved spontaneously during the
next 6 hours; he was asymptomatic by the time he was evaluated by
medical personnel. The patient attributes the episodes to eating "a lot of
pasta and salty foods" prior to playing football. Results of a complete blood
count and comprehensive metabolic profile following each episode have
been within the reference ranges. He has no history of serious illness and
takes no medications. Vital signs are within normal limits. Physical and
neurologic examinations disclose no abnormalities. Which of the following
serum concentrations is most likely to be abnormal if measured during one
of this patient's episodes?
explanation:
Potassium.
The clinical picture is most consistent with hypokalemic periodic paralysis:
adolescent onset; attacks precipitated by rest after exertion and carbohydrate-
or salt-rich intake; normal interictal exam and labs; and spontaneous resolution
within hours. During attacks, insulin- and catecholamine-driven transcellular
shift of K+ into skeletal muscle lowers serum potassium without total-body K+
deficit, producing flaccid weakness; interictal potassium is normal.[1][2][3] Other
electrolytes (sodium, chloride, calcium, magnesium) are typically normal during
attacks and are not diagnostic.[1][2]
Measuring serum potassium during an episode would reveal hypokalemia,
distinguishing this from hyperkalemic periodic paralysis (often earlier onset;
attacks precipitated by rest after exercise, cold exposure, fasting, or K+
ingestion; K+ normal or high during attacks).[1][2] Management during attacks
emphasizes cautious potassium repletion given redistribution physiology;
chronic prevention may include carbonic anhydrase inhibitors (e.g.,
acetazolamide, dichlorphenamide) in appropriate genotypes.[1][2] Case literature
further illustrates profound episodic hypokalemia with typical triggers and the
risk of rebound hyperkalemia if over-repleted.[4]
references: Hypokalemic periodic paralysis. National Library of Medicine

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 1
A 20-year-old woman is brought to the urgent care center because of a 2-
month history of progressive weakness of her arms. She also has a 1-week
history of moderate back pain and headache. Her only medication is
ibuprofen as needed for pain. Muscle strength is 3/5 in the upper
extremities. Sensation to pinprick is decreased over the upper extremities.
MRI of the spine shows a central syrinx in the cervical spinal cord. It is
most appropriate to obtain specific additional history regarding which of
the following in this patient?
explanation:
Trauma
A focused history of prior neck or spinal trauma is most appropriate.
Posttraumatic syringomyelia is a well-recognized cause of central cervical syrinx
and can present months to years after injury with progressive weakness, sensory
loss, pain, and spasticity; latency can be as short as weeks to as long as decades,
so remote injuries are relevant.[1][2][3] Case reports and series also highlight
presentations with upper limb symptoms and cervical syrinx after trauma,
including in the setting of Chiari I malformation, underscoring the importance of
eliciting any history of head, neck, or spinal injury—even if initially minor or not
associated with immediate deficits.[3][4][5][6] Compared with alternatives (diet,
family illness, travel, weight loss), trauma history most directly informs
pathogenesis and management for syringomyelia in this context.[1][2][6]
references: Rhythmic Neck Muscle Spasms and Upper Limb Muscle Myoclonic Jerks as

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 1
A 15-year-old girl is brought to the rehabilitation facility for physical
therapy. Two months ago, she was involved in a motor vehicle collision in
which she sustained a crush injury to the ulnar nerve at the right elbow.
Neurologic examination shows severe weakness and atrophy of the
intrinsic muscles of the right hand. When the patient is asked to grasp the
therapist’s fingers, right handgrip is noticeably weaker than left handgrip.
Sensation to pinprick and fine touch is decreased at the palmar aspect of
the ring and small fingers of the right hand. No other deficits are noted.
Which of the following cell types is critical for recovery of neurologic
function in this patient?
explanation:
Schwann cells
Rationale: This presentation is a peripheral ulnar neuropathy from a crush injury.
Functional recovery after peripheral nerve injury depends on Schwann cell
plasticity. After axonal injury, Schwann cells dedifferentiate into a “repair”
phenotype, clear myelin and axonal debris with macrophage collaboration
(Wallerian degeneration), form bands of Büngner to guide regenerating axons to
targets, and then remyelinate regenerated axons—processes essential for
restoration of conduction and function in the PNS.[1][2][3][4][5] In contrast:
Astrocytes and oligodendrocytes are CNS glia; oligodendrocytes myelinate
CNS axons and, along with astrocyte-associated scar and inhibitors, do not
support regeneration in the same way.[6][7]
Satellite cells in peripheral ganglia support neuronal somata but are not the
key effectors of distal nerve regeneration across an injured peripheral
nerve segment.[2][7]
Tanycytes are specialized ependymal cells in the CNS and are unrelated to
peripheral nerve repair.[7][8]
Thus, recovery after an ulnar nerve crush critically relies on Schwann cell–
mediated axonal regrowth and remyelination.[1][2][3][4][5]

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 1
A 3-year-old boy is brought to the office because of a 2-day history of
bulging of his left eye. He says his eye hurts. He has no history of major
medical illness or recent trauma to the area, and he receives no
medications. Vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination
shows exophthalmos of the left eye. MRI of the brain shows a 2-cm mass
involving the ocular muscles of the left eye. A biopsy specimen of the mass
shows malignant cells, some of which have striations. Which of the
following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
Rhabdomyosarcoma
Rationale:
Rapid-onset unilateral proptosis/exophthalmos with pain in a young child
and an orbital mass centered on the extraocular muscles is classic for
primary orbital rhabdomyosarcoma, the most common primary malignant
orbital tumor in children.[1][2][3][4][5][6][7][8]
Histopathology showing malignant cells with cross-striations indicates
skeletal muscle differentiation (rhabdomyoblasts), which is characteristic of
rhabdomyosarcoma and distinguishes it from other pediatric orbital
tumors.[2][3][9][10]
Alternatives are less consistent with the vignette:
Neuroblastoma typically involves the orbit as metastasis, often with bony
erosion, calcifications, periorbital ecchymoses; histology lacks skeletal
muscle striations.[6][11][12][13]
Retinoblastoma is intraocular (retinal) rather than an orbital/extraocular
muscle mass.[1][4][6][7]
Pheochromocytoma and thyroid cancer do not present as primary pediatric
extraocular muscle orbital masses with muscle striations; thyroid
ophthalmopathy is not a malignant mass and is atypical at age 3.[1][4]
references: Pediatric Tumors of the Eye and Orbit. Castillo BV, Kaufman L.. Pediatric
Rhabdomyosarcoma of the Orbit. Evaluation With MR Imaging and CT.
Orbital Rhabdomyosarcoma: Clinicodemographic Features and Outcomes
Alveolar Rhabdomyosarcoma Presenting as an Acute Orbital Mass in the
Orbital Neuroblastoma Metastasis: A Case Report and Literature Review.

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 1
An 11-year-old girl is brought to the emergency department by her parents
because of a 1-week history of breast enlargement. She has not had pain or
nipple discharge. She has asthma treated with inhaled albuterol as needed.
She does not smoke cigarettes, drink alcoholic beverages, or use illicit
drugs. She is at the 50th percentile for height and weight. Vital signs are
within normal limits. Examination of the breasts shows minimal
enlargement under the areolae and mild enlargement of the diameter of
the areolae without nipple discharge; no masses are palpated. There is
scant pubic hair. Which of the following best describes the sexual maturity
rating for this patient?
explanation:
The patient’s physical findings best fit Tanner stage 2, as she demonstrates early
glandular breast development and scant pubic hair, both of which define this
stage of sexual maturity.[1][2][3][4][5][6][7][8]
Minimal subareolar breast enlargement with slight areolar diameter
increase is classic thelarche (Tanner breast stage 2), reflecting palpable
glandular tissue elevating the areola/papilla without contour separation of
the breast and areola (which characterizes later stages). Lack of masses or
discharge is typical for normal pubertal development.[1][2][3][4][9]
Scant pubic hair corresponds to Tanner pubic hair stage 2 (sparse, long,
slightly pigmented hair mainly along the labia).[4][5][6]
At age 11, entry into stage 2 for breast and pubic hair is well within
contemporary norms across populations, with median thelarche around
10–11 years and PH2 around 10–13 years, acknowledging variability by BMI
and ethnicity.[1][3][4][5][6]
Inhaled albuterol has no recognized effect on pubertal timing or breast
development; systemic beta-agonist effects are limited with inhaled
delivery and primarily cardiovascular/metabolic when present.[7][8]
Correct answer: (B) 2.
references: Worldwide Secular Trends in Age at Pubertal Onset Assessed by Breast
Characterization of Puberty in an Australian Population-Based Cohort
Pharmacokinetics, Pharmacodynamics, and the Delivery of Pediatric

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 1
A 5-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department by his mother
because of an episode of bloody stool 3 hours ago. The mother says the
stool was hard "like pebbles" and she noted bright red blood on the tissue
when the patient cleaned himself. His previous bowel movement was 5
days ago. The patient has no abdominal or rectal pain now, but he did have
abdominal pain during his bowel movement 5 days ago. He has no history
of major medical illness and receives no medications. Vaccinations are up-
to-date. The patient has no recent history of travel. He is at the 5th
percentile for height and the 10th percentile for weight; BMI is at the 50th
percentile. Vital signs are within normal limits. Abdominal examination
shows hypoactive bowel sounds and a soft, slightly distended abdomen
that is not tender to palpation. Rectal examination shows 1 cm of bright red
rectal mucosa protruding from the right side of the anus; there is no rectal
bleeding. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities.
Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's physical
findings?
explanation:
A) Constipation
This child has rectal prolapse (bright red rectal mucosa protruding) in the setting
of classic functional constipation: infrequent hard “pebble-like” stools, painful
BM with straining, mild distension with hypoactive bowel sounds, and minor
bright red bleeding on wiping—most consistent with a fissure/irritation from
hard stools. Constipation is the leading cause of pediatric rectal prolapse and
best explains the current findings.[1][2][3] The North American Society for
Pediatric Gastroenterology, Hepatology and Nutrition (NASPGHAN) guideline
emphasizes painful, hard stools and stool withholding as the predominant
pattern in functional constipation, and reviews note constipation as the most
common driver in this age group.[4][5]
Alternatives are less likely:
Cystic fibrosis: historically associated with prolapse, but now uncommon
due to newborn screening; typically accompanied by failure to thrive,
pancreatic insufficiency, or recurrent pulmonary disease—none present
here.[1][2][6]
Hirschsprung disease: usually presents in infancy with delayed meconium,
explosive stool on DRE, and significant distension; not supported by this
history/exam.[4][7]
Hookworm: causes iron-deficiency anemia and occult/overt GI blood loss;
rectal prolapse is classically linked to heavy whipworm (Trichuris), not
hookworm, and this child lacks travel/exposure history.[8][9]
Intussusception: presents with intermittent severe colicky pain, vomiting,
and bloody “currant jelly” stools; absent here, and rectal prolapse is not a
feature.[10][11][12]
Collectively, the presentation most strongly supports constipation as the cause
of the rectal prolapse.[1][2][3][4][5]
• 
• 
• 
•
references: Constipation in Childhood. Mugie SM, Di Lorenzo C, Benninga MA.. Nature
Rectal Prolapse in Children: An Update to Causes, Clinical Presentation,
Helminths, Soil-Transmitted. Mary Kamb, Sharon Roy. CDC Yellow Book
Rectal Prolapse in Children: Significance and Management. Cares K, El-

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 1
An 80-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department (ED) 30
minutes after she fell out of her wheelchair at home. This is the second
visit to the ED for a fall during the past 3 months. She lives with her
daughter and son-in- law, who say they “do the best we can.” The patient
has dementia, Alzheimer type, coronary artery disease, type 2 diabetes
mellitus, and hypertension. Current medications are amlodipine, aspirin,
atorvastatin, donepezil, long-acting insulin, and lisinopril. Five years ago,
she underwent bilateral below-the-knee amputations because of infected
ulcers of the feet. She uses a wheelchair for ambulation. Ten years ago, she
underwent three-vessel coronary artery bypass grafting. She has smoked
one-half pack of cigarettes daily for 60 years. She drinks one shot of
whiskey nightly. She is thin and appears ill and disheveled. Her temperature
is 37.2°C (99.0°F), pulse is 80/min, respirations are 20/min, and blood
pressure is 120/80 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on 2 L/min of oxygen by nasal
cannula shows an oxygen saturation of 95%. Physical examination shows
temporal wasting. There are scattered ecchymoses over the abdomen and
all extremities. No other abnormalities are noted. Which of the following is
the most appropriate initial history to obtain from this patient?

options.C: Relationship with her family.[1][2] This should be done privately when

explanation:
The patient’s recurrent falls, dementia, temporal wasting, scattered ecchymoses
in multiple body regions, disheveled appearance, and caregiver statement raise
immediate concern for possible elder abuse or neglect. The most appropriate
first step in history is to assess safety through a focused inquiry into caregiver
dynamics and support. Therefore, the most appropriate initial history to obtain
is: (C) Relationship with her family.[1][2] This should be done privately when
possible because cognitive impairment and caregiver presence can hinder
disclosure.[1] While malnutrition, medications (e.g., aspirin), and comorbidities
are relevant contributors to bruising and falls, potential abuse/neglect warrants
prioritization in the ED workflow.[3][4] Fall evaluation elements (American
Geriatrics Society Geriatric ED Guidelines) follow after safety screening, and
broader geriatric assessment for unmet needs is important.[5][6]
The following table from NEJM outlines forms of elder abuse and core clinical
procedures for assessment, reinforcing why initial history must focus on
caregiver relationship, safety, and neglect/abuse indicators before other
domains.
Table 1. Forms of Elder Abuse and Clinical Procedures for Assessment by the Physician.
Used under license from The New England Journal of Medicine.
references: 2°C (99.0°F), pulse is 80/min, respirations are 20/min, and blood
Forms of Elder Abuse and Clinical Procedures for Assessment by the Physician.

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 1
An investigator is studying the impact of glucocorticoids on leukocyte
dynamics in an experimental animal model. After dexamethasone
administration, the total number of circulating neutrophils increases. Most
of the additional neutrophils in circulation are detached; these neutrophils
were previously loosely adherent and rolling along the endothelium. The
expression of which of the following cell adhesion molecules is most likely
decreased in this animal model after dexamethasone administration?

options.A: mediate intercellular junctions, not leukocyte rolling.
options.B: is an endothelial ligand for β2 integrins; while glucocorticoids
options.C: Glucocorticoids cause neutrophilia primarily via demargination. A key

explanation:
Bold answer: L-selectin (C)
Glucocorticoids cause neutrophilia primarily via demargination. A key
mechanism is reduced L-selectin (CD62L) on neutrophils, which diminishes their
rolling interactions with endothelium and releases previously marginated cells
into the circulating pool. Multiple animal and human studies demonstrate that
dexamethasone decreases neutrophil L-selectin through transcriptional
downregulation and enhanced shedding (including annexin A1–mediated
sheddase activity), temporally paralleling steroid-induced neutrophilia.[1][2][3][4]
[5][6]
ICAM-1 (B) is an endothelial ligand for β2 integrins; while glucocorticoids
can reduce some endothelial activation markers, the demargination of
rolling neutrophils is best explained by loss of leukocyte L-selectin rather
than endothelial ICAM-1 modulation.
Mac-1 (D; CD11b/CD18) and VLA-4 (E; α4β1) mediate firm adhesion;
decreased expression would impair arrest but is not the principal
mechanism for rapid demargination of rolling neutrophils.
Cadherins (A) mediate intercellular junctions, not leukocyte rolling.
Therefore, the molecule most likely decreased in this model is L-selectin,
consistent with steroid-induced downregulation/shedding and resultant
neutrophil demargination.[1][2][3][4][5][6][7][8]
references: A Potential Role for Annexin 1 as a Physiologic Mediator of Glucocorticoid-
de Coupade C, Solito E, Levine JD.. British Journal of Pharmacology.

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 1
A 67-year-old man comes to the office because he is concerned about
memory loss. He says he sometimes forgets the names of acquaintances he
sees while he is out shopping. He also has occasional word-finding
difficulty and forgets to buy some items when he goes shopping unless he
makes a list. He lives alone and is able to manage his finances, cook, and
shop without help. He works part-time as an accountant. He has
gastroesophageal reflux disease and hypertension. Current medications are
hydrochlorothiazide and omeprazole. Vital signs are within normal limits.
Physical and neurologic examinations show no abnormalities. On mental
status examination, he is fully oriented. His speech is normal, and thoughts
are organized. His mood is euthymic, and he has a full range of affect. His
concentration is intact, and he is able to perform calculations quickly and
accurately. He can name objects accurately and follow written and verbal
commands. He recalls three of four objects after 5 minutes. Which of the
following is the most appropriate physician response to this patient’s
concern?

options.A: “I am concerned about your memory loss. Let’s discuss how to further
options.B: “There's no need to worry right now, but let's meet again in 6 months.”
options.C: “Unfortunately, your memory loss will likely increase significantly
options.D: “Your episodes of forgetfulness are likely just ‘Senior Moments,’ but we
options.E: “Your examination findings indicate that your memory loss is likely

explanation:
(E) “Your examination findings indicate that your memory loss is likely
consistent with the normal aging process.”
This patient reports subjective, mild forgetfulness (names, word-finding, reliance
on lists) with preserved instrumental activities of daily living and normal
cognitive screening except 3/4 delayed recall—features most consistent with
normal age-related cognitive changes rather than mild cognitive impairment
(MCI) or dementia. By consensus definitions, MCI requires objective impairment
on testing with preserved functional independence, and dementia requires
impairment that interferes with daily function—neither is supported here.[1][2]
The American Heart Association’s statement and the American Academy of
Family Physicians’ shared decision-making tool emphasize normalizing common
age-related lapses, assessing impact on function, and educating about red flags
that should prompt reevaluation rather than initiating extensive testing or
conveying undue alarm in functionally intact patients with normal examination.
[1][3][4]
Practical next steps:
Reassure and normalize; briefly review warning signs (progressive decline,
medication mismanagement, lost bills, safety issues, getting lost, repeated
questions) that would trigger earlier assessment.[3]
Screen for potentially reversible contributors if symptoms evolve (sleep
disturbance, depression, sensory loss, anticholinergics/polypharmacy,
vascular risk) and continue cardiovascular risk optimization.[1][2]
• 
• 
Encourage lifestyle measures associated with brain health (aerobic activity,
cognitive/social engagement) and routine follow-up if concerns progress.
[1][2]

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 1
A 30-year-old woman comes to the office because of a 4-day history of an
increasingly severe, painful rash over her body and in her mouth. The rash
began over her trunk area but spread within a day to her face and
extremities. Two days before development of the rash, she had flu-like
symptoms with muscle aches and fatigue as well as a nonproductive cough,
sore throat, and runny nose. Ten days ago, she began treatment with
trimethoprim- sulfamethoxazole for a urinary tract infection; she takes no
other medications. Temperature is 39.0°C (102.2°F), pulse is 120/min,
respirations are 25/min, and blood pressure is 165/105 mm Hg. Physical
examination shows diffuse brownish red macular exanthema with bullous
lesions. Epidermis at an uninvolved site can be removed with mild
tangential pressure. Examination of a biopsy specimen of one of the lesions
shows necrosis of keratinocytes throughout the epidermis. There is
minimal lymphocytic infiltration within the superficial dermis. Which of the
following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
Toxic epidermal necrolysis
The presentation—4 days of a rapidly progressive, painful truncal exanthem
spreading to face/extremities with mucosal involvement, fever, tachycardia,
positive Nikolsky sign, and exposure to trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole 10 days
prior—is classic for SJS/TEN spectrum; the histology of full-thickness
keratinocyte necrosis with scant dermal inflammation points to TEN rather than
mimickers. Sulfonamide antibiotics are among the highest-risk culprit drugs, and
onset 1–3 weeks after initiation is typical.[1][2][3] Histopathology in SJS/TEN
shows widespread keratinocyte apoptosis/necrosis with minimal interface/
dermal inflammation, distinguishing it from erythema multiforme (interface
dermatitis with target lesions), pemphigus vulgaris (intraepidermal acantholysis
with intercellular IgG), and staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome (superficial
intraepidermal split, usually no mucosal involvement).[2][4][5][6] TEN is favored
here given extensive bullae and systemic toxicity; SJS/TEN are differentiated by
%BSA detached (<10% SJS; 10–30% overlap; ≥30% TEN).[1]
Immediate actions (beyond the scope of the question but critical): stop TMP-
SMX and initiate burn/ICU-level supportive care; assess severity with SCORTEN;
early multidisciplinary management including mucosal (especially ocular) care.[1]
[2][7][8]
references: Guo C, Yang G.. International Journal of Antimicrobial Agents.
Multidisciplinary Treatment in Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis. Surowiecka A,

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 1
A 25-year-old woman comes to the emergency department because of a 3-
hour history of fever, severe headache, light-headedness, dizziness,
shaking chills, and muscle aches. Five hours ago, she was diagnosed with
Lyme disease and began doxycycline therapy. She has no other history of
serious illness and takes no other medications. Menses occur at regular 28-
day intervals. She is currently menstruating and using a tampon. She
appears anxious. Temperature is 37.0°C (98.6°F), pulse is 120/min,
respirations are 30/min, and blood pressure is 90/60 mm Hg. Pulse
oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 94%. Physical
examination shows flushing and diaphoresis. Cardiopulmonary
examination shows no other abnormalities. Which of the following is the
most likely mechanism of this patient’s current condition?

options.A: Exacerbation of infection by Borrelia burgdorferi
options.D: Release of bacterial products producing acute inflammation

explanation:
The presentation within hours of initiating doxycycline for Lyme disease, with
rigors, myalgias, flushing/diaphoresis, tachycardia, hypotension, and anxiety, is
most consistent with a Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction. The pathophysiology is
cytokine-mediated inflammation triggered by rapid killing of spirochetes with
liberation of non-endotoxin pyrogens—particularly spirochetal lipoproteins—
which stimulate innate immune receptors and induce TNF, IL-6, and IL-8,
leading to transient systemic symptoms that typically resolve within hours.
Therefore, the most likely mechanism is Release of bacterial products
producing acute inflammation.[1][2][3][4][5][6]
Key distinctions:
Not IgE-mediated anaphylaxis to doxycycline (no urticaria/angioedema/
bronchospasm; timing and symptom complex typical for JHR).[3]
Not sepsis or endotoxin-mediated shock; Borrelia lacks classical LPS
endotoxin, and JHR occurs characteristically within the first 24 hours of
therapy initiation in spirochetal infections.[1][3][6]
Menstrual toxic shock syndrome is a consideration with tampon use but
typically features high fever, diffuse rash with later desquamation, and
multiorgan involvement due to staphylococcal superantigen toxins, which
is not the typical immediate post-antibiotic course seen here.[7]
The Infectious Diseases Society of America/AAN/ACR guideline notes a minority
of patients with early Lyme experience a transient symptom intensification
within 24 hours of starting antibiotics, consistent with JHR; antibiotic therapy is
generally continued with supportive care.[3]
references: Clinical Practice Guidelines by the Infectious Diseases Society of America
Lyme Disease Complicated by the Jarisch-Herxheimer Reaction. Maloy AL,

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 1
A 2-week-old male newborn is brought to the office for a well-child
examination. He was delivered following an uncomplicated, spontaneous
vaginal delivery at 41 weeks' gestation. The mother has no history of
serious illness and did not receive prenatal care. Her only medication is a
prenatal vitamin. She has consumed large amounts of vodka nightly for 10
years. Which of the following examination findings is most likely to be
present in this patient?
explanation:
The most likely finding is Short palpebral fissures.
Chronic heavy maternal alcohol use is strongly associated with fetal alcohol
spectrum disorders (FASD), which have characteristic craniofacial features: short
palpebral fissures, smooth philtrum, and thin upper lip vermilion, often with
microcephaly and growth restriction. The Updated Clinical Guidelines for
Diagnosing FASD from the Collaboration on FASD Prevalence and the
Collaborative Initiative on FASD emphasize short palpebral fissures as a cardinal
feature, incorporated with high weighting in dysmorphology scoring systems
and consistently observed across cohorts.[1] Population-based and
morphometric studies similarly identify small palpebral fissures among the most
discriminating facial markers of prenatal alcohol exposure, and contemporary
reviews reaffirm these as sentinel findings within FASD.[2][3][4] Prospective data
also link alcohol exposure timing in early to mid–first trimester with core
craniofacial features (philtrum/vermilion), growth restriction, and microcephaly,
consistent with the FASD phenotype that includes shortened palpebral fissures
in diagnostic frameworks.[5][6]
Alternative options are less consistent with prenatal alcohol exposure:
Hypospadias: associated with growth restriction and placental factors, not
specifically with alcohol exposure.[7][8][9]
Limb hypoplasia: classically linked to thalidomide or vascular disruptions,
not alcohol.
Neck webbing: associated with Turner syndrome and lymphatic dysgenesis.
[10]
Spasticity: not a defining neonatal exam feature of FASD;
neurodevelopmental deficits emerge later.[4][11]
Therefore, short palpebral fissures best fit the infant of a heavy-drinking mother.
[1][2][3][4][5][6]

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 1
A 15-year-old boy is brought to the office by his parents to discuss results
of a biopsy done 3 days ago for a rapidly enlarging neck mass. He first
noted the mass 1 month ago. Two weeks ago, he was evaluated for an
episode of prolonged epistaxis and was found to have a right ear effusion,
which was treated with amoxicillin-clavulanic acid. He has no other history
of serious illness and takes no medications. Height is at the 10th percentile,
weight is at the 50th percentile, and BMI is at the 75th percentile. Vital
signs are within normal limits. Physical examination shows a right ear
effusion and a 4-cm, firm mass in the right posterior triangle of the neck.
Results of a biopsy specimen show squamous epithelium with indistinct
cell margins, enlarged atypical nuclei, and absent keratin formation. Which
of the following infectious agents is the most likely underlying cause of this
patient's current condition?
explanation:
Epstein-Barr virus
The presentation is classic for EBV-associated nasopharyngeal carcinoma (NPC):
adolescent male with rapidly enlarging neck mass from nodal metastases,
recurrent epistaxis, and unilateral serous otitis media from eustachian tube
obstruction. Histology of undifferentiated/nonkeratinizing squamous epithelium
with indistinct borders and enlarged atypical nuclei without keratinization
matches WHO type III NPC, which is tightly linked to EBV. EBV is present in
practically all NPCs on EBER in situ hybridization, distinguishing it from CMV,
HIV, and HHV-8, which are not etiologically linked to NPC.[1][2][3][4][5] IDSA/
ASM guidance discusses EBV diagnostics but, in this context, EBER-ISH on
tumor tissue is the key ancillary test to confirm EBV-driven NPC.[6]
references: DNA Viruses (CMV, EBV, and the Herpesviruses). Zamora MR.. Seminars in
Guide to Utilization of the Microbiology Laboratory for Diagnosis of

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 1
A 14-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department by his parents
because of a 1-month history of intermittent right knee pain that has
worsened during the past day. He rates his current pain as a 6 on a 10-point
scale and says that it worsens when he walks and lessens when he sits.
During the past 2 weeks, he has been walking 1 mile daily in preparation for
participation in the school marching band. He has not taken any
medications for his pain. He sustained a right tibia and fibula fracture at the
age of 8 years after a skateboarding accident, which was treated with
internal fixation and casting. He has asthma treated with inhaled
budesonide daily and inhaled albuterol as needed. His mother has type 2
diabetes mellitus, and his maternal grandmother has osteoporosis. The
patient is 170 cm (5 ft 7 in; 77th percentile) tall and weighs 88 kg (195 lb;
>95th percentile); BMI is 31 kg/m² (98th percentile). Temperature is 37.0°C
(98.6°F), pulse is 95/min, and blood pressure is 130/80 mm Hg. Physical
examination shows hyperpigmented, thickened skin at the nape of the
neck. There is tenderness to palpation of the anterior aspect of the right
hip and limited range of motion on abduction, internal rotation, and flexion
of the right hip. The left hip and knees are nontender; range of motion is
full in all directions. The remainder of the examination discloses no
abnormalities. Which of the following factors in this patient’s history most
increased his risk for developing this condition?
explanation:
Body mass index (BMI)
Elevated BMI (overweight/obesity) is the most robustly supported risk factor for
pediatric fractures across upper and lower extremities, including forearm, with
consistent associations from population-based cohorts and mechanistic data.
Higher adiposity alters bone geometry and increases fall forces, yielding greater
fracture risk independent of activity levels or other covariates; higher BMI Z-
scores show strong, independent associations with fractures.[1][2][3][4][5] In
contrast, recent physical activity generally improves bone strength and is not
associated with higher fracture risk in healthy youth.[6][7] Prior fracture history
signals susceptibility but, in typical adolescents without primary bone disease or
chronic glucocorticoid exposure, the epidemiologic signal linking prior fractures
to subsequent fractures is less consistent and smaller than the effect of BMI in
large datasets.[2][4][5] Family history and medication use can matter in specific
contexts (e.g., chronic steroids), but in otherwise healthy adolescents they are
less strongly and consistently linked to fracture risk than BMI.[5][8][9]
Key evidence:
Overweight/obese youth have increased forearm fracture risk and site-
specific bone weaknesses; adiposity is overrepresented among those with
repeated forearm fractures.[1][2]
Large EMR cohort shows dose-response increase in lower-extremity
fractures with higher BMI class; effect strongest in school-aged children
but relevant through adolescence.[3]
BMI Z-score independently associates with fractures (multivariable models)
and with more complete/physeal upper-extremity fractures after low-
energy mechanisms.[4][5]
• 
• 
•
references: 6°F), pulse is 95/min, and blood pressure is 130/80 mm Hg. Physical
Associations of Childhood Overweight and Obesity With Upper-Extremity
A, Lührmann P.. International Journal of Environmental Research and Public

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 1
A 38-year-old woman comes to the clinic to discuss results of a biopsy
specimen of a mass in the right breast obtained 1 week ago. She has no
history of serious illness and takes no medications. Vital signs are within
normal limits. Examination of the right breast shows a well-healing biopsy
incision. The remainder of the examination discloses no abnormalities.
Results of the biopsy specimen disclose ductal carcinoma in situ. The
physician informs the patient he has some bad news to share and tells her
the diagnosis. He explains her treatment options, including mastectomy,
breast-conserving surgery, radiation therapy, chemotherapy, and any
combination of these options. The patient appears afraid but listens
attentively before responding, "You're the doctor. My children are still little.
Tell me what I should do." In addition to expressing empathy, which of the
following is the most appropriate physician response?

options.A: Risks abandonment; (C) premature directive without values elicitation;
options.B: "Before I make recommendations, can you tell me what you understand
options.C: "Given the stage of the cancer, I recommend mastectomy."
options.D: , (E), (F), (G) may be relevant later, but first ensuring understanding is
options.E: "Would you like to discuss these options further with your family?"

explanation:
The most appropriate next step is to elicit the patient’s understanding to ground
shared decision-making and tailor subsequent guidance. The best response is: 
(B) "Before I make recommendations, can you tell me what you understand
about your options?"
DCIS management involves preference-sensitive choices (e.g., lumpectomy
plus radiation vs mastectomy; optional endocrine therapy when ER+), with
comparable survival but differing trade-offs in local control, toxicity,
cosmesis, logistics, and alignment with values; shared decision-making is
essential. [1][2][3][4][5]
When patients express fear and ask the clinician to decide, best practice is
to acknowledge emotion and then use an ask–tell–ask/teach-back
approach to assess understanding and preferences before offering a
recommendation, avoiding both paternalism and abandonment. [4][5][6][7]
[8][9][10]
Parental responsibilities often shape choices (e.g., logistics of radiotherapy),
so eliciting understanding and values is foundational before addressing
concerns like childcare or proposing specific options. [10][11][12][13]
Why alternatives are less appropriate now:
(A) Risks abandonment; (C) premature directive without values elicitation;
(D), (E), (F), (G) may be relevant later, but first ensuring understanding is
recommended to support informed, value-congruent decisions.[3][6][7][8]
• 
• 
• 
•
references: Breast Conservation Therapy Versus Mastectomy: Shared Decision-Making
Doctor, What Would You Do? An ANSWER for Patients Requesting Advice

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 1
A 36-year-old woman with hypertension comes to the office because she
thinks she may be “going through early menopause.” She has not had a
menstrual period since her most recent office visit 6 months ago. During
this time, she also has been “gaining weight around the middle” despite
increased exercise; she has had a 6.3-kg (14-lb) weight gain. She has no
other history of major medical illness. Her only medication is lisinopril. She
does not smoke, drink alcohol, or use illicit drugs. She is 168 cm (5 ft 6 in)
tall and weighs 107 kg (236 lb); BMI is 38 kg/m². Vital signs are within
normal limits. Examination shows a uterus consistent in size with a 24-
week gestation. Pelvic ultrasonography shows oligohydramnios and a fetus
with a misshapen cranium, pericardial effusion, small bladder, and
echogenic bowel. The most likely cause of the fetal abnormalities in this
patient’s pregnancy is interference with which of the following?
explanation:
Fetal renal hemodynamics.
The constellation of oligohydramnios with small/absent bladder, hypocalvaria/
misshapen cranium, and associated cardiothoracic/abdominal findings in a mid-
to-late second trimester fetus is classic for renin–angiotensin system (RAS)
blockade fetopathy (ACE inhibitors/ARBs). RAS inhibition impairs fetal renal
perfusion and nephrogenesis, causing fetal hypotension, decreased urine output,
and oligohydramnios, with secondary sequelae (pulmonary hypoplasia,
contractures, craniofacial abnormalities/Potter sequence) and potential
neonatal renal failure.[1][2][3] Observational and guideline data consistently
contraindicate ACE inhibitors/ARBs in pregnancy—particularly after 20 weeks—
because blockade of the fetal RAS “clearly interferes with kidney development
and function” and is fetotoxic in the second/third trimesters.[4][5][6][7] ARBs
appear to carry higher fetopathy risk than ACE inhibitors when exposure
persists beyond mid-gestation.[1][2] The timing (uterus ≈24 weeks) and
ultrasound findings point to interference with fetal renal hemodynamics rather
than primary pulmonary epithelial differentiation or surfactant synthesis,
placental perfusion, or maternal prostaglandin pathways.
The American Heart Association and the American College of Cardiology
emphasize avoiding all RAS blockers in pregnancy and using alternatives such as
labetalol, methyldopa, or long-acting nifedipine for chronic hypertension.[4][5]
[7] Discontinuation of RAS blockers before 30 weeks may allow partial recovery
of amniotic fluid if fetal status remains reassuring.[1]
references: Cardiovascular Disease and Risk Management: Standards of Care in

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 1
A 25-year-old woman, gravida 1, para 1, comes to the office because of a 2-
week history of palpitations and heat intolerance. She delivered her child 3
months ago following an uncomplicated pregnancy and delivery. She is
breast- feeding. She has no history of serious illness and takes no
medications. She is 163 cm (5 ft 4 in) tall and weighs 54 kg (120 lb); BMI is 21
kg/m². Temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), pulse is 106/min, respirations are 20/
min, and blood pressure is 124/68 mm Hg. Examination shows moist palms
and bilateral lid lag. No exophthalmos is noted. The thyroid gland is
enlarged and nontender. No murmurs are heard on cardiac examination.
Deep tendon reflexes are 3+. Serum studies show an undetectable TSH
concentration, thyroxine (T₄) concentration of 20 μg/dL, and
triiodothyronine (T₃) concentration of 275 ng/dL. Which of the following is
the most likely mechanism of this patient's symptoms?

options.A: Activation of mutations of TSH receptors

explanation:
The most likely mechanism is lymphocytic infiltration of the thyroid.
This presentation is most consistent with the thyrotoxic phase of postpartum
thyroiditis: painless, nontender goiter; onset 3 months postpartum; suppressed
TSH with elevated T4/T3; absence of ophthalmopathy or bruit. Postpartum
thyroiditis is an autoimmune destructive thyroiditis triggered by postpartum
immune rebound; the thyrotoxicosis reflects release of preformed hormone
rather than increased synthesis, and many patients later transit to
hypothyroidism before recovery.[1][2][3][4] This timing helps distinguish it from
postpartum Graves disease, which more often presents after 3–4 months
postpartum and is mediated by TSH receptor–stimulating antibodies with
increased glandular synthesis and blood flow.[1][2][3] Increased serum
thyroglobulin can be seen but is not the mechanism (it is a marker of follicular
disruption). TSHR-activating mutations cause toxic adenoma/multinodular
disease; hCG stimulation applies to early pregnancy; hypothalamic ischemia does
not explain isolated thyrotoxicosis.
To underscore the distinction between destructive thyroiditis and
hyperthyroidism due to increased synthesis, the following summarizes etiologies
of thyrotoxicosis:
This table situates postpartum thyroiditis among causes of thyrotoxicosis due to
destructive release (not increased synthesis), supporting lymphocytic infiltration
as the mechanism in this case.[5]
Table 1. Epidemiology of Different Causes of Thyrotoxicosis
Used under license from JAMA Network.
Citations: The American Thyroid Association (2016, 2017) emphasizes
distinguishing postpartum destructive thyroiditis from postpartum Graves
disease based on timing, exam, TRAb, and uptake/blood flow patterns.[1][3]
Reviews concur on autoimmune lymphocytic infiltration with destructive release
as the pathophysiology and typical onset 1–6 months postpartum.[2][4][5]
references: Epidemiology of Different Causes of Thyrotoxicosis

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 68-year-old man is brought to the emergency department because of a
2-day history of progressive lethargy and tremors. He has alcoholism and
opiate, cocaine, and amphetamine use disorder, but he has abstained from
alcohol and illicit substances during the past 2 weeks. He also has alcoholic
cirrhosis. His only medication is a daily multivitamin. He previously drank
six 12-oz beers daily. He appears chronically ill and is somnolent.
Temperature is 36.8°C (98.2°F), pulse is 100/min, respirations are 18/min,
and blood pressure is 100/60 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an
oxygen saturation of 94%. Examination shows conjunctival icterus, ascites,
and 2+ edema of the lower extremities. Cranial nerves are intact. Muscle
strength is 4/5 throughout. When he is awake, there is a postural and
action tremor of the forearms and hands that is accompanied by a sudden,
brief loss of muscle strength. His speech is normal, and he is able to follow
simple commands. Serum total bilirubin concentration is 3 mg/dL. Blood
and urine cultures grow no pathogens. Which of the following is most likely
to improve this patient’s cognition and movement disorder?
explanation:
The clinical picture—cirrhosis with somnolence, icterus, ascites, edema, and
asterixis (sudden, brief loss of posture during action)—is most consistent with
overt hepatic encephalopathy (HE). Nonabsorbable disaccharides are first-line
for overt HE; lactulose reduces intestinal ammonia and improves cognition and
motor findings (including asterixis) and is associated with reductions in HE
episodes and serious liver-related events.[1][2] Therefore, the best option is: Oral
lactulose therapy.
Why not the others:
Vancomycin/cefepime: no infectious source; unnecessary broad-spectrum
antibiotics increase AKI/CDI risks.[3][4]
Thiamine: essential if Wernicke encephalopathy suspected, but the triad
(ocular findings, ataxia, confusion) is not suggested here; thiamine would
not target HE-related asterixis.[5]
Lorazepam: indicated for alcohol withdrawal; timing (2 weeks abstinent)
and exam do not fit withdrawal; benzodiazepines can precipitate/worsen
HE in cirrhosis.[6][7][8][9]
Practical HE management points:
Lactulose titration to 2–3 soft stools/day; typical start 20–30 g (30–45 mL)
orally every 1–2 hours until first bowel movement, then 15–30 mL two to
four times daily, adjusted to stool target.[1][2]
Add rifaximin for secondary prophylaxis after an overt HE episode per
hepatology practice guidance.[2][10]
To contrast with alcohol withdrawal management (not the issue here),
benzodiazepines are standard but can worsen HE; short-acting agents
(lorazepam/oxazepam) are preferred if withdrawal coexists in cirrhotics.[6][7][8]
[9] The following table summarizes benzodiazepine-based protocols for delirium
tremens, underscoring that these regimens apply to withdrawal, not HE:
• 
• 
• 
• 
• 
The NEJM table below outlines benzodiazepine regimens for alcohol withdrawal
delirium (delirium tremens), which are inappropriate for isolated HE and can
exacerbate encephalopathy in cirrhosis.
Table 3. Suggested Treatment of Alcohol Withdrawal Delirium (Delirium Tremens).
Used under license from The New England Journal of Medicine.
Supporting evidence: The Society of Critical Care Medicine guideline
conditionally recommends lactulose (and related nonabsorbable disaccharides)
as first-line therapy for overt HE with reductions in HE and serious
complications.[1] Narrative and practice guidance literature corroborate
lactulose as first-line and highlight benefit on admissions and outcomes, with
rifaximin as adjunct for recurrence prevention.[2][10] Empiric broad-spectrum
antibiotics without infection carry harm.[3][4]
references: Suggested Treatment of Alcohol Withdrawal Delirium (Delirium Tremens).
Disease, and Gastroenterology Considerations. Nanchal R, Subramanian R,
Overt Hepatic Encephalopathy: Current Pharmacologic Treatments and
Recognition and Management of Withdrawal Delirium (Delirium Tremens).

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 45-year-old woman comes to the emergency department because of a 3-
day history of progressive shortness of breath, cough productive of blood-
tinged sputum, and fever. She also has a 3-month history of numbness of
her left leg and tingling in her right arm. She has not had chills. She was
diagnosed with asthma 6 months ago; it is well controlled with inhaled
budesonide and albuterol as needed. She has no other history of serious
illness and takes no other medications. She appears to be in mild
respiratory distress. Temperature is 38.1°C (100.6°F), pulse is 105/min and
regular, respirations are 24/min, and blood pressure is 145/95 mm Hg.
Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 92%. Crackles
are heard at the right lung base, and there are occasional diffuse wheezes.
Cardiac examination shows no abnormalities. On neurologic examination,
sensation to pinprick is decreased over the lateral aspect of the left lower
extremity and right upper extremity. Muscle strength is 5/5 throughout.
Babinski sign is absent. Deep tendon reflexes are 2+ and symmetric. Which
of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?

options.B: Determination of serum angiotensin-converting enzyme activity

explanation:
Serum antineutrophil cytoplasmic autoantibody assay
The triad of adult-onset asthma, pulmonary hemorrhage/infiltrates (hemoptysis,
crackles), and peripheral neuropathy (mononeuritis multiplex pattern) strongly
suggests eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis (EGPA). In this context,
antigen-specific ANCA testing—particularly MPO-ANCA—is the appropriate
initial serologic test to support EGPA and distinguish it from other small-vessel
vasculitides; a negative result does not exclude EGPA.[1][2][3] Contemporary
guidance recommends high-quality MPO/PR3 antigen-specific immunoassays
for suspected ANCA-associated vasculitis presentations, including pulmonary-
renal or vasculitic syndromes with neuropathy, as emphasized by Kidney
Disease: Improving Global Outcomes (KDIGO) 2024.[4][5]
Why other options are less appropriate:
CT angiography: evaluates pulmonary embolism; does not explain
neuropathy and asthma clustering.[6]
Serum ACE: limited sensitivity/specificity for sarcoidosis and not
appropriate for this vasculitic pattern.[7]
Serum IgE: may be elevated in EGPA but is nonspecific and not the
recommended next step.[3]
Mesenteric angiography: not indicated without GI ischemia signs.
Anti-GBM assay: considered in pulmonary-renal syndrome; neuropathy and
asthma favor EGPA over anti-GBM disease.[4][5]
Sweat chloride: not relevant.
If ANCA is positive (often MPO), it supports a vasculitic EGPA phenotype;
proceed with eosinophil count, chest imaging for infiltrates/DAH, urinalysis/
renal function, and consider tissue biopsy when feasible.[1][2][4]
• 
• 
• 
• 
• 
•
references: International Consensus on ANCA Testing in Eosinophilic Granulomatosis
Pulmonary Embolism. Kahn SR, de Wit K.. The New England Journal of

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
An 87-year-old woman, gravida 6, para 6, is brought to the emergency
department because of a 24-hour history of severe abdominal pain and an
inability to void. She notes that for the past 2 months, she has had difficulty
emptying her bladder, especially at the end of the day. Twenty years ago,
she underwent mastectomy for breast cancer; she has been cancer-free
since then. She has a 10-year history of osteoporosis. All of her children
were delivered vaginally without complications. Her only medication is
alendronate. Examination shows the cervix protruding 7.5 cm from the
vagina. After the cervix is replaced into the vagina, a urinary catheter
drains 700 mL of urine. Urinalysis shows no abnormalities. Which of the
following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
(D) Urinary retention
The presentation is classic for acute urinary retention with a large postvoid
residual (700 mL) relieved by catheterization, precipitated by mechanical bladder
outlet obstruction from advanced pelvic organ prolapse (cervix protruding 7.5 cm
beyond the introitus). Urinary retention is defined as the inability to voluntarily
void adequately and may be acute on chronic; evaluation emphasizes PVR
measurement and prompt decompression, as done here.[1][2] In women, pelvic
organ prolapse is a recognized extrinsic anatomic cause of bladder outlet
obstruction leading to retention, particularly in elderly, multiparous patients
with prior vaginal deliveries.[3][4]
Overflow incontinence typically presents with continuous dribbling due to
chronic incomplete emptying rather than an acute inability to void; the key
finding here is overt retention relieved by catheterization, not leakage.[4][5]
Overactive bladder (urgency, frequency, urgency incontinence) and stress
incontinence (leakage with cough/exertion) are not supported by the history or
exam.[5] Normal urinalysis makes urinary tract infection unlikely as the primary
diagnosis.[5]
The most likely diagnosis is urinary retention due to bladder outlet obstruction
from advanced uterine prolapse, as evidenced by the acute inability to void, large
postvoid residual, and physical exam findings, distinguishing it from other forms
of incontinence or infection.[1][2][3][5]
references: Urinary Retention in Adults: Evaluation and Initial Management. Serlin DC,
Pelvic Organ Prolapse. Iglesia CB, Smithling KR.. American Family
Urinary Incontinence in Women: A Review. Lukacz ES, Santiago-Lastra Y,

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 25-year-old woman is admitted to the hospital because of a 2-day history
of fever, dizziness, light-headedness, chills, and shaking. She has
hyperthyroidism, gastroesophageal reflux disease, and asthma. Her
medications are oral methimazole and omeprazole, and inhaled fluticasone,
and she has been on the same dosages for 1 year. She is in the military
reserve and will be deployed overseas next week. She began atovaquone-
proguanil therapy 1 week ago and received the hepatitis A and yellow fever
vaccines 2 weeks ago. She is not sexually active. She does not smoke
cigarettes or drink alcohol. She appears ill but is not in acute distress.
Temperature is 38.6°C (101.5°F), pulse is 110/min, respirations are 22/min,
and blood pressure is 90/60 mm Hg. No wheezing is heard on auscultation.
Cardiac examination shows no abnormalities. Laboratory studies show:
Hemoglobin = 13.8 g/dL
Leukocyte count = 1100/mm³
Segmented neutrophils = 10%
Eosinophils = 10%
Lymphocytes = 40%
Monocytes = 40%
Platelet count= 320,000/mm³
Platelet count 320,000/mm³
Results of other laboratory studies are within the reference ranges. Which
of the following is the most likely cause of this patient’s hematologic
abnormality? Please choose the best option among the choices below.
explanation:
The most likely cause is: Bold the correct option exactly as written: D)
methimazole? The options are labeled letters; instruction: bold only the answer
words. Use exactly the option text. Therefore: Bold "Methimazole".
Strongly symptomatic severe neutropenia with leukopenia and fever in a patient
on an antithyroid drug is classic for antithyroid drug–induced agranulocytosis.
Methimazole is a well-established cause, typically early in therapy, and requires
immediate discontinuation, infection workup, and broad-spectrum antibiotics ±
G-CSF.[1][2] According to a review published in JAMA:
"Agranulocytosis occurs in approximately 1 in 500 patients, is dose-
related with methimazole, and almost always develops within the first
90 days of drug initiation. Agranulocytosis can also develop upon
reexposure to the drug after an interval of years."
—Henry B. Burch, MD1,2 et al., Endocrinology Service, Department of
Medicine, Walter Reed National Military Medical Center, Bethesda,
Maryland and other institutions. Management of Graves Disease: A
Review. JAMA. December 15, 2015.
Used under license from JAMA Network.
Other listed agents have far weaker associations with profound neutropenia:
Atovaquone-proguanil: rare neutropenia reported; clinically significant
marrow suppression mainly described with proguanil in severe renal
impairment, not typical in healthy travelers.[3][4][5]
• 
Fluticasone (inhaled): systemic hematologic toxicity is not a recognized
effect.[6]
Hepatitis A and yellow fever vaccines: transient post-vaccination
neutropenia has been observed in trials but is typically mild/benign; yellow
fever vaccine serious adverse events involve viscerotropic or neurologic
disease rather than isolated agranulocytosis.[7][8][9][10]
Omeprazole: very rare idiosyncratic neutropenia case reports exist, but far
less common than with methimazole.[11][12]
Clinical takeaway: profound neutropenia (ANC ≈ 110/mm³) with febrile symptoms
in this context should be attributed to methimazole until proven otherwise; stop
methimazole immediately and manage as febrile neutropenia.
Answer: Methimazole
To underscore the antithyroid drug risk profile, the following table summarizes
adverse reactions to antithyroid drugs from JAMA 2015, including
agranulocytosis:
Table 4. Adverse Reactions to Antithyroid Drugs

references: Used under license from JAMA Network.
Hyperthyroidism: A Review. Lee SY, Pearce EN.. JAMA.

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 16-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department because of a 2-
day history of fever, nausea, vomiting, headache, chills, and fatigue. He has
not had any sick contacts. He underwent splenectomy for traumatic injury
at the age of 13 years. He has no other history of serious illness and takes
no medications. He appears ill. His temperature is 39.2°C (102.5°F), pulse is
130/min, respirations are 14/min, and blood pressure is 110/60 mm Hg. On
pulmonary examination, scattered crackles are heard bilaterally. Abdominal
examination shows a well-healed midline scar and mild, diffuse tenderness
to palpation. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in
management?
explanation:
Immediate empirical antibiotic therapy is the most appropriate next step in
management for a febrile asplenic adolescent, as early treatment is essential to
prevent rapidly progressive and potentially fatal sepsis.[1]
Asplenia confers lifelong, high risk for fulminant sepsis (e.g., S. pneumoniae,
N. meningitidis, H. influenzae). Any febrile illness warrants immediate
empiric therapy without waiting for imaging or labs.[1]
Obtain blood cultures promptly, but do not delay antibiotics. Reasonable
empiric parenteral options include ceftriaxone (e.g., 50 mg/kg up to 2 g IV/
IM) with or without vancomycin if concern for resistant pneumococci or
severe illness; adjust per local resistance patterns and clinical status.[1]
Supportive care and diagnostic evaluation (e.g., chest radiograph if
respiratory signs, targeted labs) can proceed after antibiotic initiation.
Imaging such as CT chest or abdominal x-ray, antiemetics alone, or
reassurance are inappropriate initial steps given the risk of overwhelming
postsplenectomy infection.[1]
Correct answer: Antibiotic therapy.[1]

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A previously healthy 29-year-old woman is admitted to the hospital
because of a 1-day history of moderate headache, fever, and lethargy. Two
days ago, she underwent wisdom tooth extraction. She takes no
medications. She cannot remember her vaccination history. She does not
smoke cigarettes, drink alcohol, or use illicit drugs. She is a college student,
lives in an apartment complex with other students, and eats all her meals in
the campus dining hall. She says her study partner had a “really bad fever”
during the past week. The patient appears sleepy. Temperature is 40.0°C
(104.0°F), pulse is 140/min, and blood pressure is 90/40 mm Hg.
Examination shows a diffuse purpuric rash over the trunk and all
extremities. There is nuchal rigidity. Lungs are clear to auscultation. Heart
sounds are normal. Neurologic examination shows no other focal findings.
Which of the following is most likely to have prevented this patient’s
current condition?

options.B: Avoidance of eating meals in the campus dining hall
options.D: Isolation of other students on campus with febrile illness
options.E: are indicated for close contacts of an identified

explanation:
Adherence to immunization guidelines
Presentation (fever 40°C, tachycardia, hypotension, nuchal rigidity, diffuse
purpura) is classic for fulminant meningococcal disease (meningococcemia with
meningitis) in a young adult in a congregate living/learning environment with a
sick contact.[1][2][3] Routine meningococcal vaccination prevents primary
disease in this setting. The United States Centers for Disease Control and
Prevention/Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices recommend
MenACWY at age 11–12 years with a booster at 16 years, and specifically advise
vaccination for first-year college students living in residence halls; prompt
recognition/treatment and chemoprophylaxis are for case management, not
primary prevention in this patient.[4] College settings have documented
increased transmission/carriage and outbreaks mitigated by vaccination;
behavioral measures (hand hygiene, dining-hall avoidance, isolating febrile
students) are not reliable primary prevention for invasive disease acquisition.[5]
[6][7]
Notes on alternatives:
Prophylactic antibiotics (E) are indicated for close contacts of an identified
case to prevent secondary cases, not as routine primary prevention prior to
exposure.[4]
Hand hygiene, food avoidance, and isolation of febrile peers have limited
impact on nasopharyngeal acquisition of N. meningitidis, which spreads via
respiratory secretions during close contact and social mixing; vaccination
addresses this risk.[5][7]
Given this patient’s likely exposure and age, adherence to MenACWY
recommendations before college entry would have been the most effective
preventive measure.[4][6]
references: 0°F), pulse is 140/min, and blood pressure is 90/40 mm Hg.
Preventing Meningococcal Infection in College Students. Harrison LH..

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 27-year-old primigravid woman comes to the physician for her first
prenatal visit 10 weeks after a home pregnancy test was positive. She feels
well, and her pregnancy has been uncomplicated. She previously used an
oral contraceptive but discontinued it 3 months ago. She has no history of
sexually transmitted diseases. She is sexually active in a monogamous
relationship with a male partner who is the father. She has had five lifetime
sexual partners. She has a pet cat. Examination shows a uterus consistent
in size with a 10-week gestation. Testing for which of the following
infectious conditions is the most appropriate recommendation for this
patient?
explanation:
The most appropriate test to recommend at this first prenatal visit is Human
immunodeficiency virus.
HIV screening is universally recommended at the initial prenatal visit for all
pregnant patients (opt-out), regardless of risk factors. Early diagnosis and
antiretroviral therapy reduce perinatal transmission risk from approximately
30% without intervention to less than 2%, and optimize maternal care.[1][2][3]
CDC (United States Centers for Disease Control and Prevention), ACOG, and
USPSTF all endorse universal first-trimester HIV testing and consideration of
third-trimester repeat testing based on risk and local epidemiology.[1][2][3]
Routine screening for the other listed infections is not recommended in
asymptomatic, average-risk pregnancy:
Bacterial vaginosis: USPSTF recommends against screening asymptomatic
pregnant persons not at increased risk for preterm delivery.[4]
CMV: Routine serologic screening in pregnancy is not recommended;
prevention focuses on hygiene; testing is reserved for suspected primary
infection.[5][6]
EBV: No role for routine screening in pregnancy.
HPV: Cervical cancer screening follows age-based intervals; pregnancy
does not change indications. HPV testing/cytology is done only if due per
routine screening schedules, not because of pregnancy.[7]
The visual below highlights standard cervical cancer screening eligibility and
cadence, underscoring that HPV-related screening is guided by age and prior
screening status rather than pregnancy.
•

• 
•
