question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
An investigator would like to decrease the incidence of type 2 diabetes
mellitus among adult patients with risk factors including obesity, decreased
physical activity, and family history of diabetes mellitus. A total of 200 men
and women aged 30 to 64 years will be enrolled in a regular exercise
program for 6 months. A nurse reviews the details of the study with each
participant, and each participant signs an informed consent form. One
month after the study begins, one of the participants asks to drop out of
the study. Which of the following represents the most appropriate next
step by this study participant?

options.A: Complete the study because she signed the consent form
options.B: Discuss withdrawal with the institutional review board
options.C: Negotiate her withdrawal with the principal investigator
options.D: Wait to withdraw until after the investigator consults with an ethics

explanation:
Withdraw from the study now
Respect for persons/autonomy in human subjects research establishes that
participation is voluntary and that participants may discontinue participation at
any time, for any reason, without penalty or loss of benefits. This right persists
regardless of a signed consent form and does not require approval or negotiation
with the investigator, IRB, or an ethics committee. These protections are
embedded in the Belmont Report, federal regulations governing human subjects
research (45 CFR 46), and international ethical frameworks such as the
Declaration of Helsinki; they are also standard elements communicated during
consent and in patient information materials.[1][2][3][4][5][6] Ethically, any
coercion or undue influence to remain enrolled is impermissible; consent must
remain ongoing and voluntary throughout the study.[1][2][5][7]
references: "Tell Me What You Suggest, and Let's Do That, Doctor": Patient
Informed Consent. Grady C, Cummings SR, Rowbotham MC, et al.. The
Rates of and Factors Associated With Patient Withdrawal of Consent in

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 70-year-old man comes to the clinic because of a 3-day history of
fatigue, dark urine, decreased urine output, mild pain with urination, and a
low-grade fever. He has gout, hypertension, and osteoarthritis. His
medications are allopurinol, hydrochlorothiazide, and acetaminophen.
Temperature is 37.9°C (100.2°F), pulse is 88/min, respirations are 24/min,
and blood pressure is 160/95 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an
oxygen saturation of 97%. Jugular venous pressure is 7 cm H2O (N=5–9).
Cardiopulmonary examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the
following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.B: risk worsening retention-related hydronephrosis; empiric TMP-SMX
options.D: without confirming UTI and ruling out obstruction may delay appropriate
options.E: is not first-line in suspected obstructive or prerenal AKI.[1][2][4]

explanation:
Measurement of postvoid residual volume (C).
An elderly man with 3 days of dark urine, oliguria, mild dysuria, and low-grade
fever raises concern for acute kidney injury with a plausible postrenal etiology
(bladder outlet obstruction from BPH or malignancy) versus infection. In older
men, urinary retention/obstruction is common, reversible, and time-sensitive;
immediate assessment with bladder scan for postvoid residual (PVR) is
recommended to identify retention and direct source control (catheterization) if
present, prior to empiric antibiotics or fluid loading.[1][2][3][4] PVR measurement
is rapid and noninvasive, and is a core step in evaluating LUTS and suspected
obstruction (abnormal generally ≥100–300 mL, interpreted in context).[3][5] IV
fluids (B) risk worsening retention-related hydronephrosis; empiric TMP-SMX
(D) without confirming UTI and ruling out obstruction may delay appropriate
decompression; acetaminophen cessation (A) does not address the problem;
biopsy (E) is not first-line in suspected obstructive or prerenal AKI.[1][2][4]
This approach aligns with diagnostic pathways for older men with suspected
UTI/obstruction that emphasize immediate assessment of residual urine and
upper tract evaluation when febrile or obstructed.
The figure below illustrates the evaluation pathway for older men with suspected
UTI, highlighting the role of residual urine assessment and imaging to exclude
obstruction before definitive therapy.
references: Urinary Tract Infections in Older Men. Schaeffer AJ, Nicolle LE.. The New
Lower Urinary Tract Symptoms in Men. Wei JT, Dauw CA, Brodsky CN..

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 52-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 2-day history of
severe pain and markedly decreased range of motion in her right elbow.
She has rheumatoid arthritis in her elbows, wrists, and hands. She reports
episodes of moderate pain approximately four times yearly. Previous
treatment with naproxen, methotrexate, and hydroxychloroquine has
provided moderate relief. Treatment with prednisone was begun after her
most recent episode 9 weeks ago. She has not had generalized fatigue or
muscle aches. She has no other history of serious illness. Her other
medications are naproxen, prednisone, and omeprazole. Temperature is
38.4°C (101.1°F), pulse is 90/min, respirations are 12/min, and blood
pressure is 110/76 mm Hg. Range of motion of the wrists and ankles is
decreased; the right elbow is more severely limited by pain than the left
elbow. There is warmth, tenderness, erythema, and edema of the right
elbow. Fine-needle aspiration of the right elbow is performed. Gram stain
of the fluid shows a leukocyte count of 60,000/mm3 and no organisms.
Which of the following is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy?
explanation:
Vancomycin
The presentation (acute monoarthritis with severe pain, warmth, erythema,
markedly decreased ROM) plus synovial WBC 60,000/mm³ in an
immunosuppressed patient with RA strongly suggests septic arthritis. Gram
stain is often negative; when clinical suspicion is high, empiric antibiotics should
be started promptly after aspiration. Staphylococcus aureus (including MRSA) is
the most common cause; empiric MRSA coverage is recommended when Gram
stain is negative and risk is significant (RA, corticosteroids, methotrexate).[1][2]
[3] Vancomycin provides appropriate initial coverage for gram-positive cocci,
including MRSA; additional gram-negative coverage (e.g., a third-/fourth-
generation cephalosporin) is typically added based on risk and local
epidemiology, but among the listed choices vancomycin is the correct agent.[1]
[2]
Agents to avoid in this setting:
Colchicine: targets crystal arthropathy, not infection.[2][4]
Ketorolac and methylprednisolone: analgesics/anti-inflammatories that do
not treat infection; corticosteroids are not initiated in suspected active
septic arthritis before effective antimicrobial control.[1][5][6]
Infliximab: TNF-α inhibitor is contraindicated in active/suspected infection
and increases serious/opportunistic infections.[7][8]
If dosing context is needed: vancomycin dosed per actual body weight to achieve
guideline-recommended trough/AUC targets, with renal adjustment; add gram-
negative coverage when indicated; urgently arrange serial drainage.[1][2][3]
references: 4°C (101.1°F), pulse is 90/min, respirations are 12/min, and blood
Approach to Septic Arthritis. Horowitz DL, Katzap E, Horowitz S, Barilla-

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
Abstract:
Objective:
In patients with cirrhosis and acute bleeding esophageal varices, how do
endoscopic sclerotherapy and emergency portacaval shunt
compare for control of bleeding and survival?
Methods
Design: Randomized controlled trial (San Diego Bleeding Esophageal
Varices Study). ClinicalTrials.gov NCT00690027.
Allocation: Concealed.
Blinding: Blinded (gastroenterologist who evaluated patients for portal-
systemic encephalopathy).
Follow-up period: Up to 17 years.
Setting: University of California San Diego Medical Center.
Patients: 211 patients (mean age 49 years, 77% men) with acute bleeding
esophageal varices resulting from cirrhosis, who required a transfusion of ≥
2 units of blood and, for patients transferred from other hospitals,
observation of upper gastrointestinal bleeding within 48 hours of transfer.
Exclusion criterion was > 1 previous session of endoscopic sclerotherapy.
Intervention: Endoscopic sclerotherapy (n = 106) or emergency portacaval
shunt (n = 105). Emergency portacaval shunt comprised a direct side-to-
side or direct end-to-side portacaval shunt done within 8 hours of initial
contact.
Outcomes: Control of bleeding at > 30 days, survival, readmissions for
variceal or nonvariceal bleeding requiring transfusion of packed red blood
cells, and recurrent portal-systemic encephalopathy.
Patient follow-up: 100% (minimum follow-up until death or 9.4 years).
Main results
15-year survival was lower with endoscopic sclerotherapy than with
emergency portacaval shunt (10/106 vs 48/105, relative benefit reduction
79%, 95% CI 62 to 89; number needed to harm 3, CI 2 to 4). Other main
results are shown in the Table.
Endoscopic sclerotherapy (EST) vs emergency portacaval shunt (EPCS) in
patients with cirrhosis and acute bleeding esophageal varices
Outcomes | Child-Pugh risk class | EST | EPCS | P value |
————————————————————————————————————
Control of bleeding at > 30 days* | | 20% | 100% | <.001 |
Median survival (years) | A | 4.62 | 10.43 | .003. |
| B | 2.61 | 6.19 | <.001 |
|. C | 0.58 | 5.30 | .005. |
Mean number of readmissions for variceal bleeding requiring packed red
blood cell transfusion | | 6.8 | 0.4 | <.001 |
Recurrent portal-systemic encephalopathy† | | 35% | 15% | < .001 |
———————————————————————————————————
*Excluding indeterminate deaths at 14 days from nonbleeding causes.
†In patients who survived 30 days and left hospital.
———————————————————————————————————
Conclusion
In patients with cirrhosis and acute bleeding esophageal varices,
emergency portacaval shunt was better than endoscopic sclerotherapy for
control of bleeding, recurrent encephalopathy, and survival.
Sources of funding: National Institutes of Health and Surgical Education
and Research Foundation.
Structured abstract based on: Orloff MJ, Isenberg JI, Wheeler HO, et al.
Randomized trial of emergency endoscopic sclerotherapy versus
emergency portacaval shunt for acutely bleeding esophageal varices in
cirrhosis. J Am Coll Surg.
2009;209:25-40. 19651060
A 52-year-old man with hepatic cirrhosis comes to the emergency
department because of a 3-hour history of
vomiting blood. Esophagogastroduodenoscopy confirms actively bleeding
esophageal varices. Based on the abstract
shown, the physician is considering an emergency portacaval shunt (EPCS)
procedure rather than endoscopic
sclerotherapy (EST). According to the results in the abstract, approximately
how many patients must be treated with
EPCS rather than EST to prevent one case of recurrent portal-systemic
encephalopathy?
explanation:
(C) 5
Recurrent portal-systemic encephalopathy (PSE) occurred in 35% with
endoscopic sclerotherapy (EST) versus 15% with emergency portacaval shunt
(EPCS), an absolute risk reduction (ARR) of 20%.[1][2][3][4] The number needed
to treat is NNT = 1/ARR = 1/0.20 = 5, meaning treating five patients with EPCS
instead of EST prevents one case of recurrent PSE. This directionality is
consistent with the randomized trial’s long-term follow-up and is supported
contextually by meta-analytic data showing no excess encephalopathy with
surgical shunts compared with endoscopic therapy, despite superior bleeding
control.[5][6]
references: 20 = 5, meaning treating five patients with EPCS
Fifty-three Years’ Experience With Randomized Clinical Trials of

Which of the following most strongly limits the generalizability of this
study's findings?

options.B: Emergency portacaval shunt is available only at specialty centers
options.C: The follow-up period was too short
options.E: Unmeasured confounders were not controlled by the study design

explanation:
(B) Emergency portacaval shunt is available only at specialty centers
External validity hinges on whether the intervention can be delivered in
typical practice. Emergency portacaval shunt (EPCS) requires immediate
access to highly experienced surgical teams and perioperative
infrastructure that are concentrated in tertiary/quaternary centers; thus,
results from trials where EPCS is routinely and rapidly available may not
generalize to most hospitals or patient populations.[1][2][3]
Allocation concealment and blinding primarily affect internal validity. In
acute bleeding, patient blinding is generally infeasible, and lack of blinding
does not meaningfully constrain applicability. Likewise, randomized design
mitigates measured and unmeasured confounding (choice E pertains to
observational designs). Follow-up in the EPCS vs sclerotherapy trials was
long and complete, not short.[1][2][3]
Contemporary practice patterns favor pharmacologic/endoscopic therapy
with early TIPS in selected high-risk patients; surgical shunts are rarely
used outside specialized centers, further constraining real-world
applicability of EPCS-centric results.[4]
references: Orloff MJ, Isenberg JI, Wheeler HO,

explanation:

Rationale:
Multiple randomized trials from the UCSD program demonstrate that,

portacaval shunt (EPCS) achieves permanent bleeding control in nearly all
patients and markedly reduces subsequent transfusion needs and hospital
readmissions for variceal bleeding. For example, EPCS patients required
fewer PRBC units and had fewer readmissions than EST, with large,
statistically significant differences.[1][2][3][4]
Systematic reviews and meta-analyses corroborate that surgical shunts
have substantially lower rebleeding rates than endoscopic therapy,
supporting fewer bleeding-related readmissions and transfusions with
shunt strategies.[5][6][7]
Why the other choices are less appropriate:
(A) The trials show superior survival with EPCS across Child-Pugh classes;
absent explicit CIs for the Child A subgroup difference that include 0, this
statement is not supported by the reported significant survival advantages.
[1][2]
(C) While outcomes worsen with higher Child class, the specific claim about
a statistically significant difference in median survival between Child C and
B after EPCS requires class-specific median survival estimates and
hypothesis testing not established in the summary; the cited trials
emphasize overall and class-stratified survival favoring EPCS over EST
rather than formal B vs C median comparisons.[1][2]
• 
• 
• 
• 
(D) Baseline distributions (including Child class) were similar, suggesting
randomization functioned to reduce selection bias; long follow-up and
intention-to-treat analyses further mitigate bias, even if not eliminating it.
[1][2]
references: Orloff MJ, Isenberg JI, Wheeler HO,
Disability Index in a Randomized Controlled Trial of Emergency
Portosystemic Shunts Versus Endoscopic Intervention With or Without

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 42-year-old man comes to the office as a new patient. Three weeks ago,
he was hospitalized for treatment of community-acquired pneumonia and
diagnosed with HIV infection. He has no other history of serious illness and
takes no medications. Vital signs are within normal limits. Examination
shows no abnormalities. CD4+ T-lymphocyte count is 490/mm³, and
plasma HIV viral load is 45,000 copies/mL. Results of other laboratory
studies are within the reference ranges. Test results for syphilis and
hepatitis B and C are negative. The patient agrees to begin antiretroviral
therapy. He asks what his long-term prognosis will be. With adherence to
the medication regimen, this patient is at greatest risk of death from which
of the following?
explanation:
For a patient initiating ART with CD4 ≈490/mm³ and expected viral suppression,
long-term mortality is dominated by non-AIDS comorbidities rather than
opportunistic infections or AIDS-defining malignancies. Across large cohorts and
contemporary guidelines, cardiovascular disease (CVD) consistently emerges as
the leading or co-leading cause of serious non-AIDS events and death in virally
suppressed populations, driven by persistent immune activation/inflammation,
traditional risk factors, and aging.[1][2][3][4][5][6][7][8][9] Non–HIV-related
malignancies are also common, but in well-controlled HIV without other major
comorbidities, CVD generally poses the greatest long-term mortality risk.[2][4]
[5][6][7][8] Thus, the best answer is: Cardiovascular disease.
Context for the visual: The shift from AIDS-related to non-AIDS morbidity in
treated HIV is highlighted by the spectrum of HIV-associated non-AIDS (HANA)
conditions. The table below (from a JAMA review) lists emerging comorbidities—
prominently including CVD—that increasingly account for morbidity and
mortality as ART extends survival, aligning with cohort data showing CVD and
non-AIDS cancers dominating serious events in virally suppressed patients.[3][5]
[6][7]
Table 3. Selected Emerging Comorbidities in Chronic HIV Infectiona
Used under license from JAMA Network.
Key implications for management:
Aggressively address modifiable CVD risks (smoking cessation, BP, lipids,
diabetes, weight) per American Heart Association guidance and IDSA/
HIVMA primary care guidance.[1][4]
Recognize elevated relative risks for MI and stroke despite viral
suppression, and consider that CVD mortality remains substantial in
modern cohorts.[1][6][7][8][9]
Answer: Cardiovascular disease (A).
references: Selected Emerging Comorbidities in Chronic HIV Infectiona
Management of Human Immunodeficiency Virus Infection in Advanced
Global Burden of Atherosclerotic Cardiovascular Disease in People Living

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 40-year-old divorced woman comes to the office because of a 1-year
history of difficulty sleeping. She says she sometimes does not fall asleep
before 2 AM and does not feel rested at 6:30 AM when she has to get up
and get her children ready for school. Sometimes she wakes after 1 to 2
hours of sleep and cannot fall back to sleep. On these occasions, she
typically gets up for about 30 minutes, does some housework, and smokes
a cigarette. She smokes one pack of cigarettes every three to four days. She
works in a customer service call center and says she enjoys helping people
resolve their problems. She now finds her job much harder and less
enjoyable because she feels tired and sleepy. She says she would take a nap
if she could, but she picks her children up as soon as she leaves work and
spends the evening doing homework with them, making their dinner, and
preparing lunches for the following day. By 9 PM, the children are in bed so
she eats her own dinner and does some housework. She says that after 9
PM is when she smokes most of her cigarettes. She has not had depressed
mood, excessive worry, or change in weight. Vital signs are within normal
limits. Examination shows dark circles under the eyes and injected
conjunctivae. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial step in
management?

options.D: Setting a date for smoking cessation

explanation:
Education on sleep hygiene
Cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia (CBT‑I) is first‑line for chronic
insomnia and begins with behavioral and educational components (sleep
hygiene, stimulus control, sleep restriction) that target maladaptive behaviors
such as late nicotine use and irregular sleep schedules. Multiple high‑quality
guidelines and reviews support initiating CBT‑I rather than medications for
chronic insomnia without comorbid mood/anxiety disorders [American College
of Physicians; NEJM reviews; AAFP review].[1][2][3][4] In this case, prominent
evening nicotine use and delayed sleep onset are modifiable with sleep hygiene
and CBT‑I (e.g., eliminating nicotine within 4 hours of bedtime, fixed wake time,
consistent wind‑down), and evening nicotine use is associated with worse sleep
continuity and increased WASO, further supporting targeting these behaviors
first.[5]
Pharmacologic options are not first‑line:
Z‑drugs (e.g., zolpidem) carry risks (dependence, complex sleep behaviors)
and have only weak guideline support as alternatives or adjuncts after
CBT‑I.[4]
Mirtazapine is off‑label for insomnia; limited, time‑limited benefit and
adverse effects (weight gain, daytime sedation) argue against initial use in
nondepressed patients.[4][6]
Sertraline can cause or worsen insomnia and is not indicated without
depression/anxiety.[7][8]
Smoking cessation is important for overall health, but setting a quit date is not
the initial step for managing insomnia per se; instead, incorporate
nicotine‑timing counseling within sleep hygiene and CBT‑I.[1][2][4][5][9]
references: D, Forciea MA, Cooke M, Denberg TD.. Annals of Internal Medicine.
Management of Insomnia. Morin CM, Buysse DJ.. The New England Journal
"The Worst Thing Is Lying in Bed Thinking 'I Want a Cigarette'" a

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 58-year-old woman is brought to the office by her husband because of a
6-week history of increasing confusion. Her husband says that, initially, she
had difficulty doing “normal” things, such as preparing meals. Since then,
she has become progressively less able to care for herself or even use a
telephone. During this time, she also has had increased anxiety and
restlessness. She has type 2 diabetes mellitus well controlled with an oral
hypoglycemic agent. She is alert and appears anxious. She is oriented to
person but not to place or time. She startles when the door to the
examination room is slammed shut. Her temperature is 36.8°C (98.2°F),
pulse is 78/min and regular, and blood pressure is 132/94 mm Hg. Physical
examination shows no other abnormalities. On neurologic examination, she
can repeat three objects immediately but is unable to recall them after 3
minutes. She is unable to copy a drawing of a cube. Results of a complete
blood count and serum electrolyte concentrations are within the reference
ranges. Her serum glucose concentration is 155 mg/dL. CT scan of the
head shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most
appropriate next step in diagnosis?

options.B: Measurement of cerebrospinal fluid 14-3-3 protein concentration
options.C: Measurement of cerebrospinal fluid beta amyloid concentration
options.D: Measurement of cerebrospinal fluid methylmalonic acid concentration
options.E: Measurement of serum angiotensin-converting enzyme activity

explanation:
Measurement of cerebrospinal fluid 14-3-3 protein concentration
The presentation is a rapidly progressive dementia over 6 weeks with anxiety,
startle, impaired memory/visuospatial function, normal CT, and unrevealing
routine labs—features that strongly suggest sporadic Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease
(sCJD) among rapidly progressive dementia etiologies.[1][2] CSF 14-3-3 (often
with total tau) is an appropriate next diagnostic step: in the right clinical context
it has high sensitivity and specificity for sCJD and is recommended by the
American Academy of Neurology to reduce diagnostic uncertainty when sCJD is
suspected.[1][3][4] Positive results must be interpreted with clinical/MRI/EEG
correlation given false positives in acute neurologic injury.[1][3]
Why others are less appropriate:
Hemoglobin A1c: may relate to long-term glycemia and cognition in
diabetes but is not a diagnostic test for rapidly progressive dementia and
would not explain this acute course.[5]
CSF beta-amyloid: current literature does not support adequate standalone
sensitivity/specificity for AD diagnosis in this context; AD typically
progresses more slowly.[6]
CSF methylmalonic acid: B12 deficiency can cause cognitive changes but
generally subacute with other neurologic signs; MMA is measured in serum,
not CSF, for B12 assessment.
Serum ACE: limited value in diagnosing neurosarcoidosis and nonspecific;
presentation lacks supportive features.[7]
Serum protein electrophoresis: useful for paraproteinemic neuropathies or
CNS infiltration but less likely given the clinical picture and normal exam.
references: Sporadic Creutzfeldt-Jakob Disease. Salehi P, Clark M, Pinzon J, Patil A..
4. Comprehensive Medical Evaluation and Assessment of Comorbidities:

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 38-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department by
paramedics 90 minutes after she fell through ice into a stream while hiking.
She was stuck in the ice until emergency responders rescued her. On
arrival, she has severe pain in her feet. She has no history of serious illness
and takes no medications. Temperature is 34.8°C (94.6°F); other vital signs
are within normal limits. Examination shows blue discoloration over the
toes bilaterally, and the feet are cold to touch. Which of the following is the
most appropriate next step in management?

options.A: Place the feet in front of a dry space heater
options.B: Rub the feet with a steaming hot towel
options.C: Submerge the feet in a moderately warm whirlpool.
options.D: Wrap the feet in a room-temperature wet-to-dry dressing

explanation:
The most appropriate next step is rapid, controlled rewarming of the frostbitten
feet in circulating water maintained near body temperature. Therefore, the
correct choice is: (C) Submerge the feet in a moderately warm whirlpool.
The American Heart Association and American Red Cross First Aid guideline
recommends rapid rewarming of frostbite by warm-water immersion at 37–
• 
40°C (98.6–104°F) for approximately 20–30 minutes, avoiding hotter
temperatures and refreezing risk. The Wilderness Medical Society (2019
and 2024 updates) similarly endorses 37–39°C until the tissue is soft and
pliable, and advises against dry heat sources and rubbing due to thermal
and mechanical injury. The ACSM consensus aligns with these
recommendations. Observation or room-temperature dressings alone are
inadequate for frozen tissue. [1][2][3][4][5]
After rewarming: air dry (do not rub), apply dry bulky dressings, elevate,
provide analgesia (e.g., ibuprofen 12 mg/kg/day divided BID), update
tetanus, and assess perfusion; consider advanced therapies (e.g.,
thrombolysis or iloprost) in severe, proximal injuries within appropriate
time windows.[2][6]
This algorithmic approach to initial evaluation and early management after
rewarming is summarized in the NEJM figure below, which emphasizes prompt
thawing, perfusion assessment, and triage to thrombolysis when indicated.
• 
Figure 2. Algorithm for Evaluation of Frostbite and Initial Management, with Availability of Catheter-
Directed Thrombolysis.
Used under license from The New England Journal of Medicine.
references: Algorithm for Evaluation of Frostbite and Initial Management, with Availability of Catheter-
Diagnosis and Treatment of Frostbite. Sheridan RL, Goverman JM, Walker

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 52-year-old woman comes to the emergency department because of a 1-
day history of bleeding gums and moderate pain in her knees. She first
noticed the gum bleeding while brushing her teeth this morning; she has
been unable to stop the bleeding. She has systemic lupus erythematosus
with inflammatory arthritis and Raynaud disease. Medications are tramadol
and hydroxychloroquine. Vital signs are within normal limits. Examination
shows bleeding, friable gums. There are multiple ecchymoses and
petechiae over the upper extremities. Cardiac and abdominal examinations
show no abnormalities. The knees are cool to the touch; there are
effusions, and range of motion is limited by pain. Neurologic examination
shows no focal findings. Laboratory studies show:
Hemoglobin = 11 g/dL
Hematocrit = 33%
Erythrocyte count = 4.2 × 10¹²/L
Mean corpuscular volume = 90 μm³
Leukocyte count = 9000/mm³
Segmented neutrophils = 60%
Bands = 3%
Eosinophils = 2%
Basophils = 1%
Lymphocytes = 30%
Monocytes = 4%
Platelet count = 20,000/mm³
Serum
Creatinine = 0.9 mg/dL
Ferritin 1= 0 ng/mL
Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
explanation:
Prednisone therapy is the most appropriate next step in management for this
patient with SLE-associated severe immune thrombocytopenia, as supported by
current guidelines and evidence. The presentation (isolated severe
thrombocytopenia at 20,000/mm³ with mucocutaneous bleeding in known SLE)
is most consistent with SLE-associated immune thrombocytopenia. High-dose
glucocorticoids are recommended first-line to rapidly increase platelet counts
and control bleeding; IVIG can be added if a more rapid rise is needed or if
bleeding is severe/refractory.[1][2][3][4][5][6]
Factor replacement therapy: not indicated without a coagulation factor
deficiency; bleeding pattern and normal counts otherwise support ITP
rather than a coagulopathy.
Methotrexate: not a treatment for acute ITP; may exacerbate
thrombocytopenia.[7]
Plasmapheresis: reserved for thrombotic microangiopathies (e.g., TTP) or
catastrophic APS; clinical picture does not suggest these.[3]
• 
• 
• 
Splenectomy: generally second/third-line after failure of medical therapy;
typically deferred early in disease.[8][9]
The Pan-American League of Associations of Rheumatology/GLADEL guideline
suggests high-dose glucocorticoids for severe lupus thrombocytopenia, with
IVIG or rituximab for refractory or life-threatening cases.[1] NEJM reviews
similarly endorse glucocorticoids first-line for severe thrombocytopenia in APS/
SLE contexts.[3] Practical dosing commonly used: prednisone 1 mg/kg/day; IV
methylprednisolone pulses may be considered if more urgent response is
required, and IVIG 1 g/kg for 1–2 days for rapid augmentation when indicated.[1]
[3][5]
To illustrate first-line positioning of glucocorticoids for severe
thrombocytopenia in APS/SLE contexts, the following table from NEJM
summarizes therapeutic strategies and highlights steroids and IVIG as primary
options for severe thrombocytopenia:
• 
Table 3. Our Treatment Strategies for Antiphospholipid-Antibody (aPL)–Positive Patients.*
Used under license from The New England Journal of Medicine.
Answer: Prednisone therapy
references: Our Treatment Strategies for Antiphospholipid-Antibody (aPL)–Positive Patients.*

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 13-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department because of a 3-
day history of progressive fatigue, shortness of breath, and difficulty
walking up stairs. He had been well until 1 week ago, when he developed
rhinorrhea, cough, and a sore throat. These symptoms resolved
spontaneously 2 days ago. He has no history of serious illness and takes no
medications. On arrival, he is pale and in moderate respiratory distress.
Pulse is 120/min, respirations are 25/min, and blood pressure is 80/40 mm
Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 91%. On
pulmonary examination, crackles are heard throughout the lung fields. On
cardiac examination, a gallop is heard. S1 is soft. A grade 2/6, high-pitched
holosystolic murmur is heard best at the apex. The remainder of the
examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most
likely diagnosis?
explanation:
Myocarditis
The presentation is most consistent with acute myocarditis with decompensated
heart failure: recent viral prodrome; rapid progression to dyspnea, fatigue,
hypotension, hypoxemia; diffuse crackles; gallop and soft S1 suggesting
depressed systolic function; and a new apical holosystolic murmur likely from
functional MR due to LV dilation. The American Heart Association notes that
pediatric myocarditis commonly follows a viral illness and can present with acute
heart failure, arrhythmias, and new murmurs; troponin may be elevated but is
neither sensitive nor specific.[1] The American College of Cardiology emphasizes
early recognition, ECG/biomarkers/echo, and triage for hemodynamic
instability, which this child has (80/40 mm Hg).[2]
Alternatives are less likely:
Pneumonia: would explain crackles but not new murmur, gallop, or
cardiogenic shock physiology; fever is not reported and the cardiovascular
exam is strongly abnormal.[3]
Rheumatic fever: typically 2–4 weeks after GAS pharyngitis with arthritis/
fever/rash/chorea; timing and features here are inconsistent.[4]
Glomerulonephritis: presents with hematuria/proteinuria and usually
hypertension, not hypotension with gallop/murmur.[5]
Immediate management priorities (not asked but implied by severity) include
PICU admission, oxygen, cautious diuresis/inotropes as needed, ECG, troponin/
BNP, and urgent echocardiography; consider CMR when stable and involve
advanced HF support per the American College of Cardiology pathway.[2]
references: 2024 ACC Expert Consensus Decision Pathway on Strategies and Criteria
Recommendations for the Use of Echocardiography in the Evaluation of

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 10-day-old female newborn is brought to the emergency department
because of a 2-day history of bilious vomiting. She was born at term
following an uncomplicated pregnancy and spontaneous vaginal delivery.
She was 50.8 cm (20 in) in length and weighed 3260 g (7 lb 3 oz) at birth.
Examination following delivery showed no abnormalities, and she was
discharged at the age of 2 days. She is exclusively breast-fed. She appears
ill. Today, she is 50.8 cm (20 in) in length and weighs 3062 g (6 lb 12 oz).
Temperature is 38.3°C (100.9°F), pulse is 180/min, and respirations are 52/
min. The abdomen is distended; the patient cries on palpation of the
abdomen. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
Volvulus[1]
The presentation—sudden bilious emesis in the first 2 weeks of life,
abdominal distention, toxicity (fever, tachycardia, tachypnea), and
• 
abdominal tenderness—is classic for midgut volvulus from malrotation and
represents a surgical emergency due to risk of intestinal ischemia. Rapid
weight loss over a few days supports acute obstructive/ischemic
pathology.[1]
Why others are less likely:
Duodenal atresia: typically presents within the first day or two of life with
bilious emesis and classically minimal distention; often associated with
“double-bubble” and Down syndrome; many are diagnosed antenatally.
Presentation at day 10 with toxicity and marked distention is atypical.[2]
Intussusception: uncommon in neonates; peak 3 months–5 years; emesis
often nonbilious early; intermittent colicky pain and bloody stools are more
typical.[3]
Meconium ileus: usually presents soon after birth with failure to pass
meconium/abdominal distention and association with cystic fibrosis;
timing and exclusive bilious emesis at day 8–10 without prior obstructive
history are less consistent.[4]
Necrotizing enterocolitis: predominantly preterm; can occur in term
infants but often later and with feeding intolerance, abdominal
discoloration, and systemic instability; radiographic pneumatosis may be
seen but presentation here is more typical of volvulus.[5]
Key diagnostic/management point: Upper GI contrast study is highly
sensitive and may show a corkscrew configuration; urgent operative
management (Ladd procedure with detorsion and broadening of
mesentery) is required to prevent necrosis.[1]
references: 8 cm (20 in) in length and weighed 3260 g (7 lb 3 oz) at birth.
8 cm (20 in) in length and weighs 3062 g (6 lb 12 oz).

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
An 85-year-old man with severe dementia, Alzheimer type, is admitted to
the hospital because of a nonhealing ulcer
on his right second toe. He lives in a nursing care facility and has been
bedridden for the past 3 years. He has type 2
diabetes mellitus, hypertension, and peripheral vascular disease. His
medications are metformin, amlodipine, and
aspirin. Noninvasive vascular studies of the lower extremities show marked
bilateral vascular disease. Subsequent
peripheral angiography shows severe stenoses of the right superficial
femoral and the left common iliac arteries. The
patient undergoes uncomplicated placement of two sequential vascular
stents in the superficial femoral artery and
placement of an additional stent in the left common iliac artery. The type
of stents used was recently approved by
the US Food and Drug Administration and has been heavily marketed as a
long-lasting solution to restenosis. Each
stent costs the hospital $15,000. Alternatives to this approach include
amputation of the toe, balloon angioplasty,
and use of less expensive stents. Which of the following best describes the
public policy implications of this
scenario?

options.A: Absence of resource stewardship. The scenario reflects

explanation:
The best answer is (A) Absence of resource stewardship. The scenario reflects
overuse of a costly, newly approved endovascular device in a patient with
advanced dementia and limited expected benefit, despite viable, lower-cost
alternatives (eg, amputation, balloon angioplasty, older stents). Overuse and
waste are central targets of resource stewardship and high-value care initiatives.
[1][2][3] The American College of Cardiology/American Heart Association
guideline for lower-extremity PAD emphasizes aligning revascularization
decisions with prognosis and functional status in older adults; in patients >70
years with CLTI and limited predicted survival, invasive strategies may not
improve quality-of-life or health status over medical therapy.[4] Ethical analyses
in advanced dementia further underscore distributive justice and prioritization
of outcomes that matter, given resource scarcity.[5] Alternatives (B–E) do not
best fit: there is no evidence of discrimination (ageism), inequity (disparities),
illegal conduct (fraud/abuse), or barriers to receiving care (access).[6][7][8][9]
The magnitude of waste from overtreatment underscores the policy relevance of
stewardship; the following table categorizes waste domains and highlights
overtreatment as a major contributor to excess spending.[2]
Caption: Estimates of Annual US Health Care Waste, by Categorya
<|END VISUAL|>
Citations: ACC/AHA PAD guidance on older adults and limited survival benefit
from revascularization; IOM overuse framework; ethical resource allocation in
Alzheimer’s disease; waste/overtreatment framing; high-value, cost-conscious
care as a professional competency.[1][2][3][4][5]

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 62-year-old woman with myasthenia gravis comes to the office because
of a 2-week history of falling. She says
her legs "give out" once daily; she falls to the ground but does not hit her
head. She took pyridostigmine until
2 weeks ago when she decided that it no longer worked. Respirations are
30/min. Examination shows a hypophonic
nasal voice and ptosis bilaterally. Muscle strength is 2/5 in all extremities.
The patient is told that she is in imminent
danger of respiratory compromise and that she should be admitted to the
hospital. She says she knows her breathing
could pose a problem, but she hates hospitals and does not want to
undergo more tests or therapy. She asks if she
can have a prescription for her former medication and if she can go home.
Which of the following is the most
appropriate next step in management?
explanation:
The clinical picture (bulbar dysarthria with hypophonia, ptosis, diffuse 2/5
weakness, tachypnea) is consistent with an impending myasthenic crisis
requiring hospital-level monitoring and rapid-acting therapy (eg, IVIG or PLEX)
and airway support as needed.[1][2][3] Because she is refusing clearly beneficial,
potentially life-saving care, the immediate step is to determine whether this
refusal is valid. A structured evaluation of medical decision-making capacity is
ethically and legally required before honoring or overriding her refusal; capacity
is decision-specific and should assess understanding, appreciation, reasoning,
and ability to communicate a choice.[4][5] If she lacks capacity, involuntary
admission and emergent care are justified; if she has capacity, her informed
refusal must be respected.
Interventions such as pulmonary function testing (FVC/NIF) can assist
management but should not delay the capacity assessment in the face of refusal.
[6] Restarting pyridostigmine is not appropriate in suspected crisis and may
complicate management.[2][7][8] Oxygen by nasal cannula does not address
ventilatory failure risk and is not the next step in the context of refusal.
The most appropriate next step is: (A) Assessment of medical decision-making
capacity.
Gilhus’ algorithm for severe exacerbations underscores the urgency of ICU-level
care with fast-acting immunomodulation and airway support in impending crisis,
reinforcing the high stakes behind this capacity determination.[1]
Figure 3. Proposed Treatment Algorithms for Generalized Myasthenia Gravis and for Severe
Exacerbations of Generalized Disease.

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 55-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by paramedics
45 minutes after his daughter found him
unresponsive in his apartment. Paramedics initiated intravenous fluid
warming and resuscitation at the scene. The
patient has major depressive disorder, hypertension, and alcoholism. His
medications are chlordiazepoxide,
fluoxetine, and lisinopril. On arrival, he is arousable but confused.
Temperature is 34.0°C (93.2°F), pulse is 64/min,
respirations are 14/min, and blood pressure is 94/62 mm Hg. Physical
examination shows no sign of trauma or
prolonged immobilization. On mental status examination, he is oriented to
person but not to place or time. His
speech is verbose and nonsensical. Serum glucose concentration is 44 mg/
dL, and blood alcohol concentration is
312 mg/dL. Urine toxicology screening is positive for alcohol and
benzodiazepines. The most appropriate next step
in management is administration of which of the following?
explanation:
The most appropriate next step is to correct life-threatening hypoglycemia while
preventing Wernicke encephalopathy. In a chronically alcohol-dependent
patient, administer thiamine before or concurrently with dextrose. Therefore,
the best choice is Vitamin B1 (thiamine).
Rationale:
Hypoglycemia (glucose 44 mg/dL) requires immediate IV dextrose; in
alcohol use disorder, thiamine should precede or accompany glucose to
mitigate precipitating Wernicke encephalopathy/Korsakoff syndrome.[1][2]
[3]
Flumazenil is contraindicated in benzodiazepine-tolerant patients and
mixed ingestions (benzodiazepines plus alcohol) due to seizure/withdrawal
and dysrhythmia risks, per the American Heart Association and FDA
warnings.[4][5]
Naloxone is reserved for suspected opioid toxicity with respiratory
depression, not present here.[6][7]
Lorazepam treats alcohol withdrawal/delirium tremens but is not the
immediate priority; this patient is sedated from alcohol/benzodiazepines
and hypoglycemic. After stabilization, benzodiazepines are used if
withdrawal emerges.[1][8]
Practical approach:
Thiamine 100 mg IV immediately, then 50% dextrose (e.g., 25 g IV bolus)
with ongoing glucose monitoring; escalate thiamine dosing if Wernicke’s is
suspected (e.g., 500 mg IV TID).[2][8]
• 
• 
• 
• 
• 
The following table summarizes benzodiazepine-based management of severe
alcohol withdrawal, reinforcing that benzodiazepines are indicated for
withdrawal syndromes (not the immediate issue here), while thiamine is
routinely administered in hospitalized patients with alcohol use disorder.[1][8]
Table 3. Suggested Treatment of Alcohol Withdrawal Delirium (Delirium Tremens).
Used under license from The New England Journal of Medicine.
references: Suggested Treatment of Alcohol Withdrawal Delirium (Delirium Tremens).
Electrolyte Disturbances in Patients with Chronic Alcohol-Use Disorder.

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 16-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department because of a 6-
hour history of severe right-sided scrotal
pain. He has not had penile discharge, fever, abdominal pain, vomiting, or
diarrhea. He has no history of similar pain
or any trauma to the region. He is sexually active with one female partner
and uses condoms inconsistently. Vital
signs are within normal limits. Abdominal examination shows no
abnormalities. Genitourinary examination shows
moderate edema and diffuse tenderness to palpation of the right scrotum;
there is mild erythema. Lifting the right
testicle does not relieve the pain. The left testicle is lower than the right
testicle. Cremasteric reflex is present on the
left and absent on the right. Which of the following is the most likely
diagnosis?
explanation:
Testicular torsion is most likely. The sudden, severe unilateral scrotal pain with
high-riding testis (contralateral lower position), absent ipsilateral cremasteric
reflex, and no relief with elevation (negative Prehn sign) are classic for torsion
and mandate emergent urologic exploration to maximize salvage within the 4–8
hour window.[1] Epididymitis is less likely given absence of dysuria/urethritis
and typically preserved cremasteric reflex; torsion of appendix testis often
shows a blue dot sign; tumor usually presents as a painless mass; varicocele is a
chronic “bag of worms” and not acutely painful; UTI would have urinary
symptoms or fever.[2][3][4][5][6]
Context for visual: While this case is torsion, testicular tumors are a key
alternative in the differential for scrotal pathology. The following figure outlines
the diagnostic workflow when tumor is suspected (history/PE, scrotal
ultrasound, serum tumor markers) to distinguish tumor from other causes of
scrotal findings.[6]
Figure 2. Assessment, Diagnosis, Staging, and Management of Testicular Germ Cell Tumors
Used under license from JAMA Network.
Key supporting points:
Torsion: acute onset, severe pain, high-riding horizontal testis, absent
cremasteric reflex; do not delay surgery for imaging when strongly
suspected.[1]
Epididymitis: often dysuria, urethral symptoms, epididymal tenderness,
augmented peritesticular perfusion on ultrasound; maintain suspicion for
torsion in sudden onset cases (United States Centers for Disease Control
and Prevention).[2][3]
Torsion of appendix testis: blue dot sign; typically less dramatic than
torsion.[2]
• 
• 
• 
Tumor: typically painless testicular mass; ultrasound and tumor markers
(AFP, β-hCG, LDH) when suspected.[6]
Varicocele: chronic, “bag of worms,” reduces when supine.[4]
UTI: dysuria, frequency/urgency, fever or CVA tenderness in
pyelonephritis; not an acute scrotal pain presentation.[5]
references: Assessment, Diagnosis, Staging, and Management of Testicular Germ Cell Tumors
Differentiation of Epididymitis and Appendix Testis Torsion by Clinical and
Testicular Germ Cell Tumors: A Review. Singla N, Bagrodia A, Baraban E,

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 64-year-old man comes to the clinic because of a 2-week history of
severe pain and generalized muscle weakness.
He has hypercholesteremia and hypertension. Three months ago, he had a
myocardial infarction. At that time, he
began atorvastatin therapy. Other medications are clopidogrel, lisinopril,
and amiodarone. He is 178 cm (5 ft 10 in)
tall and weighs 82 kg (180 lb); BMI is 26 kg/m²
Pulse is 80/min and blood pressure is 125/67 mm Hg. Muscle
strength is 4/5 on abduction of the shoulders and flexion of the hips. Deep
tendon reflexes are 2+ throughout. No
other abnormalities are noted. Measurement of which of the following is
the most appropriate initial step in
diagnosis?
explanation:
Serum creatine kinase activity
The presentation (new proximal myalgias and weakness within months of
starting a statin, concomitant amiodarone increasing statin exposure) is classic
for statin-associated myopathy; the recommended initial test is CK to assess for
myopathy/myositis and to triage for possible rhabdomyolysis and AKI risk
[American Heart Association; American College of Cardiology].[1][2][3] The AHA
scientific statement advises measuring CK in any patient with significant
unexplained muscle symptoms on statins; marked elevations (>10× ULN, or >5× in
vulnerable patients) warrant immediate statin cessation and aggressive
hydration, with consideration of hospitalization if AKI risk is present.[1] While
many patients with SAMS have normal CK, objective weakness raises concern for
true myopathy, where CK measurement is particularly important.[2][4] ESR/CRP
are nonspecific and more useful when evaluating idiopathic inflammatory
myopathies; they are not first-line in this statin-associated scenario.[1][2][5][6]
Amiodarone can raise exposure to CYP3A4-metabolized statins and increases
myopathy risk, reinforcing the need for prompt CK assessment and drug–drug
interaction review.[3]
references: Cardiovascular Disease: A Scientific Statement From the American Heart

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 23-year-old man who is on active duty in the US Army comes to the clinic
1 hour after sustaining a bee sting to
his right cheek. The patient reports continued pain at the site of the sting,
but he has not had shortness of breath,
throat tightness, loss of vision, or other neurologic symptoms. Medical
history is unremarkable. He takes no
medications. The patient has no known allergies. He is 178 cm (5 ft 10 in) tall
and weighs 64 kg (140 lb); BMI is 20
kg/m². Temperature is 37.0°C (98.6°F), pulse is 86/min, respirations are 10/
min, and blood pressure is
120/70 mm Hg. Examination of the right cheek shows an erythematous,
swollen area of induration surrounding a
central clear area with the stinger in situ. The swelling encroaches on the
lower aspect of the patient's right orbit,
which appears puffy and mildly dusky. After the stinger is removed, which
of the following is the most appropriate
next step in management?

options.B: Admit the patient to the hospital for intravenous vancomycin therapy
options.C: Cleanse the site and apply ice

explanation:
Cleanse the site and apply ice
This presentation is consistent with a large local reaction to a Hymenoptera
sting without systemic manifestations or ocular injury. After prompt stinger
removal, symptomatic care is recommended: cleanse the site, apply cold/ice to
reduce pain and edema, and consider oral analgesics; oral antihistamines may
help pruritus if present.[1][2] The American Heart Association/American Red
Cross First Aid Guidelines support topical ice/cold packs for pain and swelling
after bee/wasp stings, emphasizing rapid stinger removal to limit venom
delivery.[1] The American Academy of Allergy, Asthma, and Immunology practice
parameter advises cold compresses and symptomatic therapy for large local
reactions; routine antibiotics are not indicated unless clear evidence of
secondary infection exists, as swelling/lymphangitis can be mediator-driven.[2]
Reviews similarly caution against unnecessary antibiotics or epinephrine in
isolated large local reactions.[3]
Epinephrine is reserved for anaphylaxis, which is not present. [1][2]
Hospital admission/IV vancomycin or oral clindamycin are inappropriate
without clinical cellulitis or systemic illness. [2][4]
Ophthalmology consultation is warranted for direct ocular involvement
(corneal sting, vision changes, severe ocular findings), not simple periorbital
extension of local edema from a facial sting.[1][5][6][7]
references: Stinging Insect Hypersensitivity: A Practice Parameter Update 2016.

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 32-year-old man is brought to the emergency department after being
found by his wife in their bedroom with a
gun in his hands. His wife reports that the patient “has not been himself”
since their son was stillborn 6 months ago.
She says that during the past month, he hardly has slept and has not
showered or shaved; he sits in the dark and
refuses to eat. He began fluoxetine therapy 3 months ago, but his
symptoms have continued to worsen. He appears
disheveled. He is 175 cm (5 ft 9 in) tall and weighs 52 kg (115 lb); BMI is 17 kg/
m². His pulse is 90/min, and blood
pressure is 100/60 mm Hg. Physical examination shows decreased skin
turgor and dry mucous membranes. When asked about his mood, he says,
“I don’t know.” He has a tearful affect. He responds to most questions with
one-word answers in a soft voice. He says he hears a voice telling him that
he is a failure and says he wants to join his son in heaven. He refuses to
answer questions about suicide. After removing all firearms from the
patient’s house and admitting him to a psychiatric facility, which of the
following is the most appropriate next step in management?
explanation:
Electroconvulsive therapy.
ECT is the most appropriate next step for this patient because it is the most
effective and rapid treatment for severe, psychotic major depression with
suicidality, medical compromise (weight loss, dehydration), and failure to
respond to an SSRI. The American Academy of Family Physicians summary of the
VA/DoD guideline explicitly endorses ECT for severe major depression with
psychosis, severe suicidality, or need for rapid response, and notes it is more
effective than pharmacotherapy in this context.[1] Contemporary expert reviews
highlight high response (≈60–80%) and remission (≈50–60%) rates with ECT in
treatment‑resistant and psychotic depression and its ability to rapidly reduce
suicidal drive.[2] Algorithmic guidance for psychotic depression likewise places
ECT as the most effective modality for inpatients with severe psychotic
depression.[3] Compared with other options listed:
Switching SSRIs (e.g., sertraline) or starting imipramine is slower and less
likely to achieve rapid stabilization in life‑threatening illness.[1][3]
Mirtazapine augmentation has not shown benefit over placebo in persistent
severe depression per VA/DoD guidance.[1]
Repetitive transcranial magnetic stimulation may help TRD but is slower,
less robust in psychotic depression, and not preferred when urgent
response is required.[1][4][5][6]
In this scenario—severe psychotic depression with suicidality and medical
instability after SSRI nonresponse—ECT is the recommended next step.[1][2][3]
• 
• 
•
references: Therapeutic Strategies for Treatment-Resistant Depression: State of the

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 60-year-old woman comes to the office as a new patient. She recently
moved to the area. She feels well. She has
hypertension treated with amlodipine and asthma well controlled with
inhaled fluticasone and inhaled albuterol as
needed. According to the medical records she brought with her, a
mammography 6 months ago showed no
abnormalities, results of laboratory studies 1 year ago were within the
reference ranges, and colonoscopy 5 years ago
showed no abnormalities. Her past three Pap smears have shown no
abnormalities; the most recent Pap smear was
1 year ago. Her mother has type 2 diabetes mellitus, and her maternal aunt
had breast cancer at the age of 55 years.
The patient does not smoke cigarettes. She drinks two glasses of wine
weekly. She works as a nurse. She has been
monogamous with her husband for 30 years. Pulse is 76/min, respirations
are 16/min, and blood pressure is
132/86 mm Hg. Examination shows no abnormalities. Screening for which
of the following is the most appropriate
next step in management?
explanation:
E) Hepatitis C
The US Preventive Services Task Force (USPSTF) recommends one-time HCV
screening for all adults aged 18–79 years, irrespective of risk factors, because of
substantial prevalence of undiagnosed infection and the availability of highly
effective direct-acting antivirals with high SVR and favorable safety, extending
benefit to older adults.[1]
Why the other options are not indicated now:
Abdominal aortic aneurysm: USPSTF recommends against screening
asymptomatic women who have never smoked and have no family history
of AAA.[2][3]
Breast cancer: She had a normal mammogram 6 months ago; routine
biennial screening from 40–74 years is appropriate, so no immediate repeat
is indicated.[4]
Chlamydia trachomatis infection: USPSTF recommends screening women
≤24 years and ≥25 years only if at increased risk. A 60-year-old in a long-
term mutually monogamous relationship without other risk factors is not
indicated for screening.[5]
Colon cancer: Normal colonoscopy 5 years ago; average-risk adults with a
normal colonoscopy repeat at 10 years, so not due now.
Most appropriate next step: one-time HCV antibody screening with reflex RNA
testing if positive, consistent with the USPSTF recommendation for adults 18–79
years.[1]
• 
• 
• 
•

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 12-year-old girl is brought to the physician for a follow-up examination 8
days after starting a course of oral
penicillin for streptococcal pharyngitis. Temperature is 38.6°C (101.5°F).
Examination shows an erythematous
morbilliform rash, urticaria, mild swelling of the hands and feet, arthralgias,
and lymphadenopathy. Leukocyte count
is 13,000/mm³ (62% segmented neutrophils, 10% eosinophils, and 28%
lymphocytes). Urinalysis shows 2+ protein.
Which of the following is most appropriate to prevent recurrence of these
symptoms if the patient is diagnosed with
streptococcal pharyngitis again?

options.C: Skin testing with benzylpenicilloyl polylysine prior to treatment
options.E: Use of a single injection of benzathine penicillin to treat the pharyngitis

explanation:
The presentation 8 days into penicillin is most consistent with a serum sickness–
like reaction (fever, morbilliform/urticarial rash, arthralgias, edema,
lymphadenopathy, leukocytosis with eosinophilia, and proteinuria), a non–IgE-
mediated hypersensitivity commonly triggered by β-lactams in children.[1][2]
Recurrence can occur with re-exposure to the culprit or related β-lactams. Skin
testing detects IgE-mediated allergy and does not predict serum sickness–like
reactions; pretreatment with antihistamines or corticosteroids does not prevent
recurrence.[2][3] Therefore, future streptococcal pharyngitis should be treated
with an alternative to penicillins.
The most appropriate prevention strategy is: (D) Use of a nonpenicillin
antibiotic to treat the pharyngitis.
For group A streptococcal pharyngitis in patients who should avoid penicillins,
the Infectious Diseases Society of America recommends non–β-lactam options
such as clindamycin or macrolides (noting local resistance patterns); first-
generation cephalosporins are options for IgE-mediated penicillin allergy but
may be avoided here given concern for recurrence of serum sickness–like
reactions with β-lactams.[4][5] IDSA dosing examples: clindamycin 7 mg/kg/
dose three times daily (max 300 mg/dose) for 10 days; azithromycin 12 mg/kg on
day 1 (max 500 mg), then 6 mg/kg/day (max 250 mg) on days 2–5.[4][5]
Benzathine penicillin re-exposes to the culprit class and is contraindicated.[2][4]
Skin testing with benzylpenicilloyl polylysine is not useful for serum sickness–
like reactions.[3]

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 45-year-old woman is hospitalized for management of Staphylococcus
aureus endocarditis with persistent
bacteremia. The patient is discussed during interdisciplinary rounds, which
includes physicians, nurses, pharmacists,
and social workers. During rounds, a pharmacy student notices that the
patient missed two doses of her scheduled
antibiotic last week but is unsure why. The physician and nurse are
unaware of these missed doses, and the student
does not mention her observation. Which of the following measures is
most likely to improve communication within
this interdisciplinary health care team?

options.A: Conduct interdisciplinary rounds in a quieter location
options.B: Encourage questions from all team members
options.C: Implement a checklist for standardizing patient rounds
options.D: Use computers during rounds to review medications

explanation:
(B) Encourage questions from all team members
Encouraging questions and input from every team member promotes
psychological safety—an environment where individuals at all levels feel safe to
speak up, ask clarifying questions, and share concerns without fear of
embarrassment. Psychological safety is consistently associated with improved
interprofessional communication, error reporting, and patient safety; leadership
inclusiveness and explicit invitations to speak up mitigate hierarchical barriers
that often silence trainees and non-physician clinicians.[1][2][3][4] In this
vignette, such a culture would make it more likely that the pharmacy student
would raise the missed doses, prompting timely problem-solving.
By contrast:
Checklists and standardized rounds can improve efficiency and ensure key
topics are addressed, but they do not inherently overcome hierarchy or
ensure that all voices are heard unless coupled with explicit behaviors that
invite participation.[5][6][7][8][9]
Using computers/EHRs during rounds may aid data retrieval but can
impede rich, in‑person communication and does not by itself foster
speaking up.[10][11]
A quieter location may reduce distractions but does not address the core
barrier of hesitancy to voice concerns.
Deliberate behaviors that invite questions (eg, “What are we missing?” directed
to each discipline and trainees) and leader inclusiveness are practical, evidence-
aligned strategies to surface critical information and improve team performance.
[1][2][3][4]
references: Exploring the Barriers and Facilitators of Psychological Safety in Primary
A Systematic Review Exploring the Content and Outcomes of Interventions
The Medical Ward Round: Evidence, Pitfalls, and Tips. Bassetti S, Arpagaus
Johnson CG, Winget M, et al.. JAMA Network Open. 2023;6(5):e2313178.

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 37-year-old man comes to the physician for a routine health maintenance
examination. He has no history of
serious illness and takes no medications. He does not smoke cigarettes,
and he exercises four times weekly. His
mother died of colon cancer at the age of 76 years. He is 183 cm (6 ft 0 in)
tall and weighs 100 kg (220 lb); BMI is
30 kg/m². Pulse is 68/min and blood pressure is 138/74 mm Hg. The
remainder of the examination shows no
abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

options.A: Test of the stool for occult blood
options.C: Measurement of serum thyroid-stimulating hormone concentration

explanation:
(D) Serum lipid studies
For asymptomatic men, lipid screening is recommended starting at age ≥35,
and earlier (age 20–34) if cardiovascular risk factors (e.g., obesity) are
present; thus a 37-year-old with BMI 30 kg/m² warrants lipid testing to
assess ASCVD risk and guide primary prevention. The American College of
Cardiology/American Heart Association framework then uses these data
for risk estimation and consideration of statin therapy with risk enhancers
as appropriate.[1][2][3]
Colorectal cancer screening is not indicated at age 37 in this setting:
average-risk screening begins at 45–50; with a single first-degree relative
diagnosed at ≥60 years, most guidance recommends starting at age 40 with
any test, not before; his mother’s diagnosis at 76 does not accelerate timing
before 40.[1][2][4]
Routine CBC or TSH is not recommended in asymptomatic adults without
specific indications; obesity alone does not mandate TSH or CBC absent
symptoms/findings. Diabetes screening is reasonable beginning at age 35
with overweight/obesity, but among the options listed, lipid studies are the
most appropriate next step; glucose/A1c testing is not offered as a choice
here.[1][2][5]
In summary, lipid screening is indicated now; stool occult blood, CBC, and TSH
are not indicated, and doing nothing would miss cardiovascular risk assessment.
[1][2][3][4][5]
references: The Adult Well Male Examination. Heidelbaugh JJ, Tortorello M.. American
AGA Clinical Practice Update on Risk Stratification for Colorectal Cancer
Clinical Management of Obesity – Third Edition. Caroline M. Apovian MD,

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 35-year-old woman is evaluated for a persistent fever 4 days after
admission to the hospital for treatment of
pyelonephritis. She has type 2 diabetes mellitus poorly controlled with
insulin. She has received ciprofloxacin since
admission. Results of initial blood cultures are pending. Temperature is
40.0°C (104.0°F), pulse is 110/min,
respirations are 20/min, and blood pressure is 100/60 mm Hg.
Examination shows marked right costovertebral angle
tenderness. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in
management?

options.A: Addition of clindamycin to the medication regimen
options.B: Addition of vancomycin to the medication regimen
options.D: Doppler ultrasonography of the lower extremities
options.E: Repeat blood cultures and fungal cultures

explanation:
Computed tomography scan of the abdomen
In a diabetic patient with acute pyelonephritis who remains febrile and clinically
unstable after 72 hours of appropriate therapy, prompt cross-sectional imaging
is indicated to evaluate for complications such as renal/perinephric abscess,
obstructing urolithiasis, or emphysematous pyelonephritis. The American
College of Radiology (American College of Radiology Appropriateness Criteria)
rates contrast-enhanced CT abdomen/pelvis as the preferred modality in
complicated cases or lack of clinical improvement, given its superior sensitivity
for abscess and gas-forming infections compared with ultrasound.[1]
Contemporary reviews similarly recommend imaging and repeat cultures when
there is no response within 48–72 hours, with CT as the test of choice in
complicated hosts (e.g., poorly controlled diabetes).[2][3] CT also rapidly
identifies emphysematous pyelonephritis, which is strongly associated with
diabetes and requires urgent source control in addition to antibiotics.[4][5]
Adding clindamycin or vancomycin is not indicated without evidence of
gram-positive pathogens (e.g., enterococci or MRSA), which are uncommon
in community-acquired pyelonephritis; empiric expansion should be
culture-directed.[2][4][6]
Doppler ultrasound of the legs is not relevant absent concern for DVT.
Repeat blood/fungal cultures may be obtained if there is suspicion for new
bloodstream infection or candidemia, but imaging to identify a urologic
source/complication is the priority next step.[3][7]
If CT reveals obstruction or abscess, urgent decompression or drainage should
accompany antimicrobial optimization.[1][3]
references: Management of Pyelonephritis and Upper Urinary Tract Infections.

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 23-year-old man is being evaluated in the intensive care unit because of
steadily decreasing blood pressure since
he was admitted 2 hours ago for treatment of septic shock caused by
pneumonia. Medical history is remarkable for
cystic fibrosis and severe obstructive lung disease. In the emergency
department, the patient was intubated, had three
peripheral intravenous catheters placed, and was treated empirically with
broad-spectrum antibiotics,
bronchodilators, a continuous infusion of propofol and fentanyl, and 4 L of
0.9% saline. Immediately after
administration of fluids, the patient's pulse was 105/min and blood
pressure was 100/50 mm Hg. The patient is 173
cm (5 ft 8 in) tall and weighs 59 kg (130 lb); BMI is 20 kg/m². Pulse is 135/
min and blood pressure is 79/43 mm Hg.
Auscultation of the lungs discloses diffuse rhonchi and inspiratory crackles
with low-pitched bilateral expiratory
wheezes. Cardiac examination discloses no murmurs, rubs, or gallops.
Bedside ultrasonography of the abdomen
shows the inferior vena cava is 1.8 cm in diameter with minimum
respirophasic collapsibility, and cardiac
ultrasonography shows that the left ventricular ejection fraction is
approximately 0.65. Results of laboratory studies
obtained on admission and now are shown:
On Admission, the White Blood Cell count is 21,000/mm³ and Blood Lactic
acid is 5.2 mg/dL. Now the patient's White Blood Cell count is 19,000/mm³
and Blood Lactic acid is 4.0 mg/dL
Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.A: Administer an additional 1-L bolus of 0.9% saline
options.D: Begin a continuous infusion of norepinephrine
options.E: Order CT scan of the head

explanation:
The most appropriate next step is to begin a continuous infusion of
norepinephrine.
Rationale:
Persistent hypotension after 4 L crystalloid with ultrasound evidence of
non–fluid responsiveness (IVC 1.8 cm with minimal collapsibility, preserved
LVEF) argues against additional fluid and for early vasopressor support.
Liberal additional fluids risk harm without hemodynamic gain.[1][2]
The Society of Critical Care Medicine/IDSA Surviving Sepsis Campaign
recommends norepinephrine as first-line vasopressor for septic shock
when hypotension persists after adequate fluids; early initiation is standard
of care. Typical starting dose: 0.01–0.1 µg/kg/min, titrated to MAP ≥65 mm
Hg; add vasopressin (0.03 U/min) if escalating norepinephrine doses are
needed.[3][4]
Hydrocortisone is considered when shock persists despite vasopressors
(e.g., ongoing vasopressor requirement), not before vasopressor initiation.
• 
• 
• 
Empiric voriconazole lacks indication without specific fungal risk features.
Head CT is not indicated for isolated hemodynamic instability.[3][5]
The table below summarizes randomized trial–informed advances supporting
norepinephrine as first-line vasopressor in septic shock, aligning with the
management step here.
Caption: Major Therapeutic Advances in Septic Shock in Selected Randomized
Clinical Trials<|END VISUAL|>

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 32-year-old nulligravid woman comes to the office for counseling prior
to conception. Menses occur at irregular
40- and 60-day intervals. She has impaired glucose tolerance controlled by
diet. She takes no medications. She is
165 cm (5 ft 5 cm) tall and weighs 84 kg (185 lb); BMI is 31 kg/m². Vital signs
are within normal limits.
Examination shows no abnormalities. Her fasting serum glucose
concentration is 120 mg/dL. This patient is at
greatest risk for developing which of the following?
explanation:
The clinical picture (BMI 31 kg/m², oligomenorrhea suggesting PCOS, and
preconception impaired fasting glucose 120 mg/dL) confers the highest relative
risk for developing gestational diabetes compared with the other listed
outcomes. Obesity independently raises GDM risk regardless of gestational
weight gain.[1] Pre-pregnancy impaired fasting glucose substantially increases
subsequent abnormal GCT and GDM diagnoses, and early abnormal glucose
metabolism (fasting glucose ≥110 mg/dL) marks individuals at high risk for later
GDM per the American Diabetes Association (ADA).[2][3][4] PCOS further
augments GDM risk, with risk modified upward by higher BMI.[5][6] Meta-
analytic data link early elevated fasting glucose to markedly increased GDM risk.
[7] Collectively, these factors are additive and outweigh risks for placenta previa
or intrauterine growth restriction; preterm birth risk is increased in obesity/
PCOS, but the magnitude of association is smaller and less consistent than for
GDM.[1][6][7] Therefore, the best answer is: Gestational diabetes.
Preconception actions aligned with ADA guidance (American Diabetes
Association): screen for undiagnosed diabetes, target A1C <6.5% before
conception if diabetes/prediabetes is diagnosed, and pursue weight reduction
before pregnancy to lower GDM risk.[3][8][9] Lifestyle-first weight loss
counseling is recommended; preconception weight loss ≥5% reduces GDM
recurrence risk in trials.[8]
The figure below summarizes management considerations for women with
obesity before and during pregnancy, including counseling on weight loss and
the benefits of reducing risks such as gestational diabetes, which directly applies
to this patient profile.[9]
Caption: Management Considerations for Women with Obesity before, during,
and after Pregnancy.
<|END VISUAL|>
Rationale with supporting evidence:
Obesity (prepregnancy BMI ≥30) increases GDM risk independent of
gestational weight gain.[1]
Pre-pregnancy impaired fasting glucose (100–125 mg/dL) is associated with
higher rates of GDM and abnormal screening tests; ADA highlights fasting
glucose ≥110 mg/dL as identifying higher risk and later GDM.[2][3][4]
• 
• 
PCOS is associated with higher GDM risk, amplified with higher BMI.[5][6]
Elevated early fasting glucose predicts later GDM in meta-analysis.[7]
ADA preconception guidance emphasizes weight management and
glycemic optimization to mitigate this risk.[3][8]
references: 15. Management of Diabetes in Pregnancy: Standards of Care in
Diagnosis and Classification of Diabetes: Standards of Care in

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 17-year-old girl is brought to the physician by her parents because they
are concerned she is using illicit drugs.
They report that, “she just isn’t herself anymore.” She has no history of
serious illness and takes no medications. She
is upset about being “tricked” to come to the doctor. Physical examination
shows no abnormalities. On mental status
examination, she has an angry mood and anxious affect. The patient’s
parents privately request a toxicology
screening for their daughter but ask that she be told that her blood will be
taken to “check her thyroid gland.” After
discussing the parents’ wish for secrecy, which of the following is the most
appropriate next step in management?

options.B: Inform the parents that everything the patient tells the physician is
options.C: Inform the parents that if the patient’s toxicology screening is positive,
options.D: Inform the parents that the physician cannot deceive the patient

explanation:
(D) Inform the parents that the physician cannot deceive the patient
Deception about the purpose of testing violates core ethical obligations of
honesty, undermines trust, and is inconsistent with adolescent confidentiality
standards. The American Academy of Pediatrics (AAP) emphasizes centering the
adolescent’s perspective, clearly explaining confidentiality and its limits, and
avoiding deceptive practices; testing and disclosures should be discussed
directly with the adolescent when feasible.[1][2][3] The American College of
Obstetricians and Gynecologists likewise advises private time with adolescents,
explicit discussion of confidentiality boundaries, and transparency about
information handling; deception is not endorsed.[4]
Routine toxicology screening without the adolescent’s knowledge is not justified
unless there is an acute safety concern or a mandated-reporting situation; even
then, clinicians should explain limits of confidentiality and strive to engage the
adolescent in the disclosure process.[1][2][5] Additionally, many jurisdictions
permit minors to consent confidentially to certain substance use evaluations,
reinforcing the need to engage the adolescent rather than deceive her.[2][5]
Practically, the next steps include:
Meet with the adolescent alone, explain confidentiality and its limits, and
explore safety concerns.
Encourage family alignment while centering the adolescent’s well-being
and autonomy.
Proceed with testing only with the adolescent’s informed assent/consent
unless an applicable legal mandate or safety emergency requires otherwise,
documenting the rationale and plan.[1][2][3][5]
references: Confidentiality in the Care of Adolescents: Policy Statement. Chung RJ, Lee

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 52-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes mellitus comes to the office for
a follow-up examination. Two weeks
ago, she was treated with amoxicillin for a urinary tract infection. At that
time, her blood pressure was 165/98 mm
Hg, and serum creatinine concentration was 1.2 mg/dL; lisinopril therapy
was also begun. During the past week, she
has had increased swelling of the lower extremities and shortness of
breath. Her dysuria has resolved. She appears
anxious. Temperature is 36.8°C (98.2°F), pulse is 110/min, respirations are
20/min, and blood pressure is 185/105
mm Hg. Diffuse bilateral crackles are heard on pulmonary examination. An
S3 gallop is heard. There is 3+ pitting
bilateral pretibial edema.
The laboratory studies showed that the levels for Serum Urea nitrogen is
45 mg/dL and Serum Creatinine is 4 mg/dL. As for the urine, its specific
gravity is 1.010, no blood, and has moderate numbers of Hyaline casts.
Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
The most likely diagnosis is Acute kidney injury (C).
Rationale:
Rapid rise in creatinine from 1.2 to 4.0 mg/dL over 2 weeks fulfills
functional criteria for AKI and correlates with stage 3 AKI by creatinine
change; AKI commonly presents with volume overload (pulmonary edema,
S3) when excretory function acutely declines.[1][2][3]
Urinalysis is bland: isosthenuria (specific gravity 1.010) and hyaline casts
without hematuria or proteinuria favor intrinsic/hemodynamic AKI over
glomerulonephritis or AIN.[1][3][4]
Lack of fever, rash, eosinophilia, or eosinophiluria makes drug-induced AIN
from amoxicillin less likely; AIN often shows urine abnormalities (pyuria/
eosinophils, mild proteinuria).[5][6][7][8][9][10]
Acute glomerulonephritis is unlikely without hematuria, RBC casts, or
significant proteinuria.[11]
Postrenal obstruction is not suggested by history/exam; prerenal volume
depletion contradicts the clinical picture of hypertension and overt volume
overload.[1][3][12]
Recent initiation of an ACE inhibitor in a high-risk host (diabetes,
hypertension) can precipitate hemodynamically mediated AKI by lowering
efferent arteriolar tone and GFR, particularly when renal autoregulation is
vulnerable; though up to 30% creatinine rise on RAS blockade can be
• 
• 
• 
• 
• 
• 
acceptable, this increase is far greater and accompanied by volume
overload.[13][14]
The KDIGO/consensus framework highlights AKI as a functional syndrome
defined by creatinine/urine output, independent of cause; this patient meets
those criteria.
The classification framework underpinning the diagnosis is summarized here:
In the KDIGO-style functional definitions, AKI is classified by changes in serum
creatinine and urine output, which this patient meets based on creatinine rise
alone.[2]
Table 1. Classifications of Acute Kidney Injury and Chronic Kidney Disease.
