question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 15-year-old girl is brought to the emergency department by her father
because of a 2-hour history of right leg pain
that began after she fell from her bicycle. Medical history is remarkable for
bilateral hearing loss, a fracture of the
humerus at age 8 years, and multiple rib fractures. She takes no
medications. Family history is remarkable for
kyphoscoliosis, hearing loss, and early onset osteoporosis in her mother;
the mother died from complications related
to a hip fracture at age 44 years. The patient is a refugee who has recently
immigrated. The patient appears to be in
distress. She is below the 5th percentile for height, at the 70th percentile
for weight, and above the 95th percentile
for BMI. Her temperature is 37.0°C (98.6°F), pulse is 118/min, respirations
are 12/min, and blood pressure is
130/68 mm Hg. Examination shows kyphosis and hypermobile joints of the
hands, wrists, and feet. Examination of
the right lower extremity shows superficial abrasions and ecchymoses;
there is tenderness to palpation and
movement of the extremity. Which of the following is the most likely
underlying diagnosis?
explanation:
Osteogenesis imperfecta
The constellation of recurrent fractures from minimal trauma, short stature,
kyphosis, hypermobile joints, bruising/ecchymoses, and conductive/
sensorineural hearing loss with a family history of bone fragility is classic for this
inherited connective tissue disorder due to defects in type I collagen. The
American Academy of Pediatrics notes bone fragility with low-trauma fractures
plus secondary features including scoliosis, hearing loss, short stature, and joint
hyperextensibility as characteristic.[1]
Alternatives are less consistent:
Idiopathic juvenile osteoporosis lacks extra-skeletal connective tissue
features (hearing loss, hypermobility) and a strong family history pattern.[2]
Juvenile Paget disease presents in early childhood with rapid bone
turnover, marked hyperphosphatasemia, deformities, and often
osteosclerosis—features not described here.[3]
Osteomalacia and rickets involve defective mineralization with biochemical
abnormalities (elevated ALP, secondary hyperparathyroidism, ±
hypocalcemia) and rickets-specific deformities (wrist/ankle swelling, genu
varum/valgum, rachitic rosary), which are not reported.[4][5]
Given the clinical scenario and family history, osteogenesis imperfecta best
explains the findings.[1]

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 22-year-old primigravid woman at 10 weeks’ gestation comes to the
emergency department (ED) because of a 3-
day history of persistent vomiting. She is treated with intravenous
hydration, pyridoxine, and doxylamine. As the
resident physician prepares the patient for discharge, he asks who will be
picking her up from the ED. She says her
husband will be arriving soon and asks if the hospital staff can refer to her
symptoms as a “stomach flu.” She does
not want anyone to mention to her husband that she is pregnant because
the pregnancy resulted from an oral
contraceptive failure and “he is not expecting this.” Which of the following
is the most appropriate next question to
ask this patient?

options.A: and (D) are tangential and risk shifting away from immediate safety
options.B: “How do you think he will react?”
options.C: prematurely directs disposition without first clarifying risk and
options.D: “Was there a problem with your birth control?”
options.E: is leading and may shut down disclosure; begin with open-ended

explanation:
The request to conceal pregnancy from a spouse is a red flag for intimate partner
violence (IPV) or reproductive coercion and warrants a private, nonjudgmental
safety assessment using open-ended questions before discharge. The most
appropriate next question is: “How do you think he will react?” This invites
disclosure, respects autonomy, and helps assess immediate risk without making
assumptions, consistent with best practices for IPV screening in pregnancy and
universal education approaches.[1][2][3][4][5][6]
Why not the other options:
(A) and (D) are tangential and risk shifting away from immediate safety
assessment.
(C) prematurely directs disposition without first clarifying risk and
preferences.
(E) is leading and may shut down disclosure; begin with open-ended
exploration, then proceed to targeted risk and safety questions.
If concern is elicited, proceed with private screening, brief counseling, safety
planning, and offer linkage to advocacy resources; targeted tools can help gauge
risk for severe harm.[3]
To illustrate targeted risk assessment after an initial open-ended question, the
following brief tool screens for risk of severe or lethal assault in IPV:
The brief DA-5 can be used after open-ended exploration to assess imminent
danger (e.g., escalating violence, strangulation risk, weapon threats), guiding
urgency of interventions and referrals.[3]

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
Five days after admission to the hospital for management of an ST-
elevation myocardial infarction, a 59-year-old
man has the sudden onset of severe abdominal and bilateral lower
extremity pain, cyanosis of the lower body, and
paresthesias. On hospital days 1 and 2, he received vasopressor and
inotropic therapy. On hospital day 2,
echocardiography showed extensive anterolateral akinesis of the left
ventricle. He has no history of serious illness.
His medications are metoprolol, lisinopril, simvastatin, and dalteparin. He
appears to be in acute distress. Pulse is
105/min and regular, respirations are 24/min, and blood pressure is 80/60
mm Hg. Examination shows lower body
cyanosis to the level of the costal margins. Crackles are heard over the
lower lung fields bilaterally. Distant heart
sounds but no murmurs are heard. The abdomen is nondistended and
nontender. Pulses are 1+ in the upper
extremities and absent in the lower extremities. Muscle strength is 3/5 in
the lower extremities. Sensation to touch
and temperature is decreased over the lower extremities. ECG shows sinus
tachycardia. Which of the following is
the most likely explanation for this patient’s clinical findings?
explanation:
B. Aortic embolism
Presentation is most consistent with an acute aortic saddle embolus causing
complete aortoiliac occlusion: sudden severe abdominal and bilateral leg pain,
lower body cyanosis to the costal margin, absent lower-extremity pulses with
preserved upper-extremity pulses, and bilateral motor/sensory deficits. The
timing (day 5 after a large anterior STEMI with extensive LV akinesis) strongly
implicates embolization from a left ventricular mural thrombus, a recognized
complication after anterior MI that can lead to systemic embolization including
aortic saddle embolus.[1][2] Acute aortic occlusion typically presents with abrupt
bilateral limb ischemia, neurologic deficits from spinal cord or peripheral
ischemia, and shock if extensive territory is affected.[3]
Alternatives are less likely:
Aortic dissection usually presents with chest/back pain; malperfusion can
cause limb ischemia, but the symmetric lower-body-only findings with
preserved upper pulses and periumbilical cyanosis are classic for aortic
saddle embolus rather than dissection.[3][4]
Pericardial tamponade would produce hypotension with JVD and clear
lungs; diffuse pulse loss is not expected, and localized lower-body ischemia
with absent femoral pulses is not typical.[5][6]
Right ventricular failure causes hypotension and JVD with clear lungs, not
abrupt bilateral lower limb ischemia or absent lower pulses.
Ventricular free-wall rupture leads to hemopericardium and tamponade
physiology rather than isolated lower-body ischemia.[6]
Vasopressors can precipitate peripheral limb ischemia, but they do not explain
the abrupt, symmetric aortoiliac-level ischemia with neurologic deficits and
• 
• 
• 
• 
preserved upper extremity perfusion; the post-MI LV thrombus mechanism is
more consistent.[1][3][7]

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 75-year-old man comes to the clinic because of a 2-year history of
progressive shortness of breath. He also has
had an occasional dry cough for the past 6 months. He has not had chest
pain, palpitations, or swelling of his legs.
Medical history is unremarkable, and the patient takes no medications. He
has smoked one pack of cigarettes daily
for 57 years. He does not drink alcoholic beverages. Vital signs are
temperature 37.0°C (98.6°F), pulse 92/min,
respirations 18/min, and blood pressure 122/76 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on
room air shows an oxygen saturation of
89%. The patient has mild dyspnea when he speaks. Auscultation of the
lungs discloses fine and dry crackles in the
bases of both lungs. Cardiac examination discloses a normal S1 and S2. No
extra heart sounds are heard. Jugular
venous pulsation is measured at 2 cm above the sternal angle with the head
of the bed elevated at 30 degrees.
Examination of the extremities shows clubbing of the digits of both hands.
Results of complete blood count, serum
chemistry profile, and urinalysis are within the reference ranges. Chest x-
ray shows scattered reticular opacities in
both lung bases. There is no hilar lymphadenopathy. Which of the following
is the most appropriate next step in
evaluation?

options.B: High-resolution computed tomography scan of the chest
options.D: Serum antiglomerular basement membrane antibody assay

explanation:
The presentation (older man, progressive exertional dyspnea, chronic dry cough,
bibasilar “Velcro” crackles, clubbing, hypoxemia, basilar reticular opacities on
CXR) is classic for interstitial lung disease, particularly idiopathic pulmonary
fibrosis. High-resolution CT is the key next diagnostic step to establish the
presence and pattern of fibrosis (e.g., UIP vs alternatives), which often obviates
the need for biopsy and guides subsequent testing and management. The
American Thoracic Society/European Respiratory Society/Japanese Respiratory
Society/Latin American Thoracic Association guideline explicitly requires HRCT
in suspected IPF, and thoracic CT has superior sensitivity and utility versus chest
radiography for ILD subtyping.[1][2][3][4] Biopsy is reserved for cases with non-
diagnostic or discordant HRCT findings, and carries higher risk in older
hypoxemic patients.[5][6] Echocardiography, serologies, and PET do not replace
HRCT as the first imaging step in this context.[3][7][8][9][10]
Answer: (B) High-resolution computed tomography scan of the chest
The following table summarizes the typical clinical features prompting suspicion
for IPF that match this patient and supports proceeding to HRCT first.

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A previously healthy 20-year-old woman, who is a sophomore in college, is
brought to the emergency department
by her roommates because of an 8-hour history of weakness and vomiting
blood and a 2-day history of dizziness.
She has no history of similar symptoms. She says she overeats in response
to the stress of her classes. She induces
vomiting after consuming a significant amount of food because she feels
"too full" and "out of control." She now
vomits almost daily. She feels she is slightly overweight. She is physically
active and exercises moderately two to
three times weekly. She does not use laxatives or diuretics. She has many
friends and enjoys going out socially. She
takes no medications. She drinks three to four beers on weekends and does
not use illicit drugs. She appears
uncomfortable and is tearful and mildly diaphoretic. She is 157 cm (5 ft 2 in)
tall and weighs 59 kg (130 lb); BMI is
24 kg/m². Temperature is 37.5°C (99.5°F), pulse is 115/min and regular, and
blood pressure is 100/68 mm Hg.
Examination shows tender parotid glands bilaterally and poor dentition.
Results of laboratory studies are most likely
to show which of the following in this patient?

options.A: K+ is Decreased, Cl⁻ is decreased, HCO³⁻ is decreased
options.C: K+ is Decreased, Cl⁻ is increased, HCO³⁻ is decreased
options.D: K+ is Increased, Cl⁻ is decreased, HCO³⁻ is increased
options.E: K+ is Increased, Cl⁻ is increased, HCO³⁻ is decreased

explanation:
The clinical picture is consistent with bulimia nervosa with frequent self-induced
vomiting (parotid enlargement, dental erosion). Gastric HCl loss produces a
chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis with renal maintenance via RAAS
activation and distal H+/K+ secretion, yielding hypokalemia, hypochloremia, and
elevated bicarbonate. Therefore, the most likely laboratory pattern is: Potassium
is decreased, chloride is decreased, bicarbonate is increased (B). This pattern is
described across pediatric and adult guidelines and corroborated by clinical
studies and acid–base reviews.[1][2][3][4][5][6][7][8]
Context for visual: The NEJM physiologic framework separates chloride-
responsive metabolic alkalosis from chloride-resistant causes and explains how
volume depletion, hypokalemia, and hypochloremia maintain alkalemia after
vomiting—precisely the mechanism in purging bulimia.[6][8]
Key pathophysiology and clinical correlations:
Loss of gastric HCl increases serum HCO3−; volume and Cl− depletion
impair bicarbonate excretion; aldosterone-driven distal H+ and K+
secretion sustains alkalosis and hypokalemia.[6][7][8]
Hypokalemia and hypochloremia are frequently observed in bulimia
nervosa and correlate with purging frequency.[2][3][5][9]
Management is isotonic saline with KCl repletion to correct ECF volume,
chloride deficit, and potassium deficit; this reverses the alkalosis by
restoring renal bicarbonate excretion.[6][7][8]
• 
• 
• 
Safety considerations: monitor for arrhythmias with hypokalemia; correct
electrolytes cautiously and address ongoing purging behaviors per American
Psychiatric Association guidance.[2]

Integration of Acid–Base and Electrolyte Disorders. Seifter JL.. The New

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 58-year-old man comes to the clinic for a follow-up examination 3
months after beginning warfarin therapy for
deep venous thrombosis. He noted blood in his urine 1 week ago but has
not seen any since that time. He has not had
pain, fever, or increased urinary frequency. He has not sustained any recent
trauma. His prothrombin time and INR
have been within the therapeutic ranges during his previous
measurements. He has hypertension and benign
prostatic hyperplasia. Medications are warfarin and hydrochlorothiazide.
He smoked one pack of cigarettes daily for
40 years but quit when diagnosed with deep venous thrombosis.
Temperature is 35.9°C (96.6°F), pulse is 88/min,
respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 135/87 mm Hg. The prostate
is moderately enlarged; no nodules are
noted.
Laboratory studies showed that the Prothrombin time is 19 sec (INR=2.5),
serum Urea nitrogen is 18 mg/dL, serum Creatinine is 1 mg/dL, and
Prostate-specific antigen is 3.8 ng/mL (N<4).
The laboratory result on urine showed the following: blood is 3+, protein is
1+, RBC is too numerous to count, WBC is 2/hpf, no casts, and negative for
Nitrites and Leukocyte esterase.
In addition to a CT scan of the abdomen, which of the following is the most
appropriate next step in management?

options.A: Adjustment of the warfarin dosage to maintain an INR between 1.5 and

explanation:
Cystoscopy is the most appropriate next step because guidelines and evidence
indicate that all patients with gross hematuria and risk factors—including those
on therapeutic anticoagulation—require prompt urologic evaluation to exclude
malignancy. The patient has gross hematuria, is male, age >35, with a heavy
smoking history; anticoagulation at a therapeutic INR is not considered a
sufficient explanation, and malignancy must be ruled out with cystoscopy plus
upper tract imaging.[1][2][3][4] The American Urological Association (AUA/SUFU)
also recommends cystoscopy as part of risk-based evaluation for hematuria; the
American College of Physicians emphasizes that antithrombotic therapy should
not preclude evaluation.[4][5] Observational data show antithrombotic therapy
can unmask otherwise silent bladder cancers, reinforcing the need for
cystoscopy despite therapeutic INR.[3] Empiric antibiotics are not indicated
without infection, finasteride is a consideration only after exclusion of
malignancy, hydrochlorothiazide is not causally implicated, and down-titration
of warfarin is inappropriate with therapeutic INR.[1][2][6][7]
Therefore, in addition to CT imaging of the upper tracts, the next step is 
Cystoscopy.
To contextualize the diagnostic approach, comparative modeling of guideline
strategies shows that cystoscopy is a core test across risk-based pathways for
hematuria; CT urography is generally reserved for higher-risk presentations
such as gross hematuria or smokers, aligning with this patient’s profile.[8]

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 32-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department
immediately after being involved in a motor vehicle
collision. She was the unrestrained driver. On arrival, she says she has
severe pain in her right groin. On
examination, any movement of the right hip produces pain. An x-ray of the
pelvis shows a completely displaced
fracture of the femoral neck. In addition to intravenous morphine, which of
the following is the most appropriate
management?

options.D: Limited weight bearing with use of crutches

explanation:
Operative fixation is indicated. In a young adult with a completely displaced
femoral neck fracture from high-energy trauma, urgent anatomical reduction
and stable internal fixation is the standard to preserve the femoral head and
mitigate risks of osteonecrosis and nonunion; nonoperative options (bed rest,
traction, bracing, limited weight bearing) are inadequate and associated with
poor outcomes.[1][2][3] Contemporary evidence in this age group favors fixed-
angle constructs over multiple cancellous screws alone for displaced patterns,
with augmentation (e.g., sliding hip screw plus medial femoral neck buttress
plate and antirotation screw) improving construct success; surgery should occur
within 24–48 hours when feasible.[1][3][4][5][6]
Correct answer: Operative reduction and internal fixation.
Figure 4 from NEJM illustrates the treatment schema: internal fixation for
femoral-neck fractures and arthroplasty options for displaced fractures in older
adults; for young patients with displaced femoral-neck fractures, the goal is head
preservation via reduction and internal fixation rather than arthroplasty.[1] This
visual reinforces that conservative measures are not definitive care for this injury
pattern.
Figure 4. Selected Treatments for Hip Fracture.
Used under license from The New England Journal of Medicine.
Why not the other options:
Bed rest, traction, hip spica, or limited weight bearing do not address
vascular compromise or instability and carry high risks of nonunion and
avascular necrosis in displaced femoral-neck fractures.[1][2][5][6]
Closed reduction followed by traction is obsolete for definitive care;
definitive internal fixation is required.[1][2][3]
• 
• 
Key operative considerations:
Urgent closed or open reduction to restore alignment and decompress
intracapsular pressure; stable internal fixation with a fixed-angle construct,
often augmented with a medial buttress plate and antirotation screw, has
lower failure than screws alone in displaced fractures in younger adults.[4]
[5]
Early surgery within 24–48 hours optimizes outcomes.[1][3]
references: Modern Methods of Treating Hip Fractures. Parker MJ, Gurusamy K..

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 57-year-old man comes to the physician 1 week after he had a 45-minute
episode of slurred speech and
clumsiness of his right hand. The episode occurred during his daily walk;
the symptoms resolved during the next 24
hours. He has no history of similar symptoms. He has a 20-year history of
hypertension and a 15-year history of
hypercholesterolemia controlled with a low-cholesterol diet and daily
exercise. He takes no medications. He has
smoked one pack of cigarettes daily for 40 years and drinks one to two
glasses of wine nightly with dinner. He is
alert and fully oriented. He is 178 cm (5 ft 10 in) tall and weighs 86 kg (190
lb); BMI is 27 kg/m². His temperature is
37.0°C (98.6°F), pulse is 88/min, respirations are 10/min, and blood
pressure is 170/90 mm Hg. Examination,
including neurologic examination, shows no other abnormalities. Daily
enteric-coated aspirin therapy is begun.
Which of the following is the most appropriate next step to decrease this
patient’s risk for subsequent cerebral
infarction?
explanation:
Antihypertensive therapy.
Rationale: After TIA, blood pressure lowering provides the largest proven
reduction in recurrent stroke risk. The American College of Cardiology/
American Heart Association recommend initiating or intensifying
antihypertensive therapy a few days after TIA when BP is ≥140/90 mm Hg, with
preferred agents including a thiazide diuretic, ACE inhibitor, ARB, or a thiazide–
ACE inhibitor combination; a target <130/80 mm Hg is reasonable for most
patients.[1][2] Trials summarized by the American Stroke Association show BP
lowering after stroke/TIA reduces recurrence by roughly 30–40% (e.g.,
indapamide alone; perindopril ± indapamide) and support prioritizing these
classes.[3] This patient’s BP (170/90 mm Hg) is markedly above target, making
antihypertensive therapy the most immediate and impactful next step.
Other measures:
High-intensity statin therapy is recommended for presumed
atherosclerotic TIA and reduces recurrent vascular events, but BP control
has stronger direct evidence for recurrent stroke reduction and is the most
urgent abnormality here.[2][3][4]
Smoking cessation improves outcomes after stroke/TIA and should be
counseled, but effect sizes in observational data are smaller and less
immediate than BP lowering.[3][5]
Alcohol reduction and weight optimization are advisable for risk factor
control, but evidence for preventing recurrent stroke events is less direct.
[3][4]
Agent selection and dosing should be individualized; commonly used regimens
include a thiazide (e.g., chlorthalidone 12.5–25 mg daily) and/or ACE inhibitor
• 
• 
• 
(e.g., perindopril 4–8 mg daily) or ARB, titrated to achieve <130/80 mm Hg as
tolerated.[1][2][3]

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 27-year-old primigravid woman at 21 weeks’ gestation comes to the
emergency department because of a 2-day
history of moderate headache, shortness of breath, nausea, vomiting,
muscle aches, and malaise. She also has had
cough occasionally productive of sputum. Pregnancy had been
uncomplicated. She has no history of serious illness,
and her only medication is a prenatal vitamin. She immigrated to the USA
from India 6 months ago. Temperature is
38.3°C (100.9°F), pulse is 100/min, respirations are 18/min, and blood
pressure is 100/60 mm Hg. On examination,
breath sounds are decreased at the right lung base. Fundal height is 22 cm.
Fetal heart rate is 160/min. Chest x-ray
shows right-sided interstitial infiltrates. Which of the following is the most
likely infectious agent?
explanation:
Influenza A virus
Key reasoning:
Presentation is acute (2 days) with systemic symptoms (fever, myalgias,
headache, malaise, nausea/vomiting) plus cough and tachycardia, which is
typical for influenza-associated community-acquired pneumonia (CAP) in
otherwise healthy young adults; chest radiograph often shows interstitial
infiltrates.[1][2][3]
Pregnancy increases risk for severe influenza and lower respiratory tract
involvement due to physiologic and immunologic changes; influenza A/B is
a leading viral etiology of CAP, and viral etiologies are common in younger
outpatients.[1][4][5][6]
Epidemiology in pregnancy supports influenza as a major cause of acute
respiratory illness with lower respiratory tract involvement; studies in
pregnant cohorts show influenza frequently identified among viral ALRTIs.
The ACOG and the Infectious Diseases Society of America emphasize
prompt recognition and treatment of suspected influenza in pregnancy.[3]
[5][6][7][8]
Less likely alternatives:
Streptococcus pneumoniae and Haemophilus influenzae typically cause
lobar or bronchopneumonia rather than predominant interstitial patterns
and lack prominent systemic myalgias; onset can be less abruptly systemic.
[1][2][4]
Mycobacterium tuberculosis usually presents subacutely/chronic, often
with constitutional symptoms over weeks and upper-lobe involvement;
acute myalgias and abrupt symptom onset are atypical.[1]
Legionella pneumophila would suggest high fevers, GI symptoms,
hyponatremia, and exposure risk; not evident here.[1]
Pseudomonas aeruginosa is uncommon without structural lung disease or
healthcare exposure.[1]
• 
• 
• 
• 
• 
• 
• 
Clinical implication: In pregnancy with suspected influenza, initiate
neuraminidase inhibitor therapy promptly; oseltamivir 75 mg orally twice daily
for 5 days is recommended by the Infectious Diseases Society of America and
ACOG, ideally within 48 hours but not withholding if later.[6][8]

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 7-year-old boy is brought to the physician because of a 1-year history of
poor performance in school. His parents
say that he is bright, has many friends, and seems to want to do well in
school. His teachers report that he seems
frustrated with his own progress and his inability to meet the expectations
of his parents. Speech fluency and
articulation and motor skills are appropriate for age. Physical examination
shows no abnormalities. When asked to
read during the examination, he has significant difficulty sounding out
words he is unfamiliar with. Visual acuity
test and audiometry show no abnormalities. Which of the following is the
most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
The most likely diagnosis is Learning disorder.
The child has persistent, specific reading impairment—difficulty sounding out
unfamiliar words—despite normal speech, motor skills, and sensory function,
and with otherwise intact cognition and sociability. This pattern is characteristic
of specific learning disorder with impairment in reading (dyslexia), which
requires academic skills substantially below age expectations, persists despite
targeted help, begins in school years, and is not better explained by intellectual
disability, language disorder, or sensory deficits.[1] Expressive language disorder
would present with impaired expressive language (e.g., syntax, verb use), not
isolated decoding failure.[2] Intellectual developmental disorder requires global
deficits in intellectual and adaptive functioning, which are absent here.[3] ADHD
is not suggested by attention/hyperactivity symptoms. Social anxiety disorder
centers on fear of scrutiny and avoidance, not isolated phonologic decoding
deficits.[4] The course is chronic and non-urgent.[5]
To contextualize why social anxiety is unlikely in this vignette (despite poor
school performance), the DSM-5 criteria emphasize fear of negative evaluation
and avoidance of social or performance situations, which are not described in
this case.[4]
Table 1. Diagnostic Criteria for Social Anxiety Disorder.
Used under license from The New England Journal of Medicine.
Key features supporting specific learning disorder (reading/dyslexia):
Persistent inaccurate/effortful word reading and difficulty sounding out
words.[1]
Academic underachievement confined to reading; normal speech fluency/
articulation and normal hearing/vision.[1][2]
No global intellectual/adaptive deficits.[3]
Not better accounted for by anxiety or attentional disorders.[1][4]
The American Psychiatric Association’s DSM-5-TR criteria (2022) specify these
diagnostic elements and exclusion criteria.[1]
• 
• 
• 
•
references: Diagnostic Criteria for Social Anxiety Disorder.

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 23-year-old woman who is an active-duty enlisted airman in the US Air
Force comes to the base clinic because of
a 4-week history of an itchy sensation in her right ear. She has not had
ringing in her ears or hearing loss. She works
as a fuel specialist and at first attributed her symptom to the earplugs she
uses when she is on the flight line. The
symptom has not improved despite the patient's wearing headphones
instead of the earplugs during the past 2 weeks.
She has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. Vital signs
are within normal limits. During
examination, pulling of the right pinna inferiorly to examine the ear canal
produces pain. The right ear canal appears
erythematous and edematous. The tympanic membrane cannot be fully
visualized because of the presence of
cerumen. Examination of the left ear shows no abnormalities. Which of the
following is the most likely diagnosis?

options.C: The most likely diagnosis is otitis externa because the patient’s symptoms and

explanation:
Otitis externa (C)
The most likely diagnosis is otitis externa because the patient’s symptoms and
examination findings—especially pain with pinna movement and canal
inflammation—are classic for this condition and are best explained by infection
of the external auditory canal rather than cerumen impaction, trauma, or
dermatologic disease.[1][2][3][4][5] Pruritus, canal erythema/edema, and marked
tenderness with manipulation of the pinna or tragus are hallmark features;
occupational earplug use and minor canal trauma are common precipitants.[1][2]
[3][5][6][7] Cerumen impaction alone would not cause pain with pinna
movement or diffuse canal edema; trauma typically requires a clear inciting
injury with focal findings; polychondritis involves the cartilaginous pinna sparing
the lobule, often bilateral and systemic; seborrheic dermatitis causes scaling/
erythema without severe tragal/pinna tenderness or canal edema.[2][3][4][8]
The diagnosis is clinical; absence of systemic symptoms and unilateral canal
inflammation further support uncomplicated acute otitis externa in this setting.
[1][2][3][4]
references: Clinical Practice Guideline: Acute Otitis Externa. Rosenfeld RM, Schwartz
ACR Appropriateness Criteria® Inflammatory Ear Disease. Agarwal M,
Otitis Externa: A Practical Guide to Treatment and Prevention. Sander R..
Ear Pain: Diagnosing Common and Uncommon Causes. Earwood JS, Rogers

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 34-year-old woman who is on active duty in the US Army comes to the
military urgent care clinic because of a 4-
day history of headache, purulent nasal discharge, and left facial pain. She
has not had fever or chills. She has
chronic bilateral knee pain, which she attributes to multiple parachute
jumps while she was assigned to an airborne
unit 7 years ago. The knee pain is exacerbated by long-distance running on
pavement. Her only medication is
occasional ibuprofen for the knee pain. The patient has no known
medication allergies. Temperature is 37.1°C
(98.8°F), pulse is 60/min, respirations are 14/min, and blood pressure is
122/80 mm Hg. Cervical lymph nodes are
not palpable. There is tenderness to palpation over the left maxillary sinus.
Nasal examination shows inflamed
turbinates and purulent discharge. The patient needs to blow her nose
twice during the examination. Bilateral
otoscopic examination discloses no abnormalities. Examination of the
oropharynx shows scant purulent discharge
posteriorly. The remainder of the examination discloses no abnormalities.
Which of the following is the most
appropriate next step in management?

options.B: Gram stain and culture of nasal discharge

explanation:
The most appropriate next step is symptomatic management with analgesics and
decongestants for uncomplicated acute rhinosinusitis of 4 days’ duration.
Therefore, the correct answer is: Ibuprofen and decongestant therapy.
Rationale:
Presentation is consistent with viral acute rhinosinusitis (<7–10 days,
nonsevere, no “double sickening”), for which watchful waiting and
symptomatic therapy are recommended; antibiotics are reserved for
persistent (>10 days), severe, or worsening illness.[1][2][3][4]
Imaging (CT or X-ray) and routine Gram stain/culture of nasal discharge
are not indicated in uncomplicated cases.[2][3][5][6]
Trimethoprim–sulfamethoxazole is not recommended empirically due to
high resistance in S. pneumoniae and H. influenzae (IDSA).[7]
Practical symptomatic options:
Ibuprofen as needed for pain; consider intranasal corticosteroids and brief
topical nasal decongestant use (≤3–5 days) or oral decongestants for
congestion; saline irrigation may help with purulent drainage.[1][2][4][8]
If symptoms persist beyond 7–10 days or worsen, initiate antibiotics (e.g.,
amoxicillin or amoxicillin–clavulanate per guideline).[1][2][4]
To contextualize when antibiotics would be used if criteria are later met, the
following table lists recommended antibiotic options and dosing from NEJM.[1]
• 
• 
• 
• 
Here is a concise table of recommended first-line antibiotic options and dosing
for adults with acute bacterial sinusitis, useful if watchful waiting fails or severe
criteria develop:

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 16-year-old boy with VATER syndrome is brought to the physician
because of 2-week history of harsh cough that
began with a common cold. He receives no medications. Temperature is
37°C (98.6°F), pulse is 70/min, respirations
are 20/min, and blood pressure is 112/64 mm Hg. Examination shows a
harsh vibratory sound in the mid anterior
chest with forced expiration. A systolic murmur is heard best at the lower
left sternal border. There is mild scoliosis.
Spirometry shows an FVC of 2.54 L (78% of predicted) and a slow vital
capacity of 2.88 L (85% of predicted). A
flow volume loop shows scooping on the expiratory limb. Which of the
following is the most likely cause of the
discrepancy between this patient’s forced and slow vital capacity
measurements?
explanation:
Tracheal collapse
The clinical context (history of TEF in VATER association, harsh expiratory
vibratory sound) and spirometry pattern (expiratory scooping on the flow–
volume loop) point to intrathoracic tracheomalacia with dynamic expiratory
collapse. During a forced maneuver, dynamic central airway collapse and early
airway closure reduce exhaled volume, so FVC underestimates true vital
capacity, whereas SVC obtained with a slow maneuver is less affected and closer
to the actual VC. This produces the observed FVC < SVC discrepancy. The
American Thoracic Society/European Respiratory Society technical standards
recognize that dynamic airway collapsibility can lead to a lower FVC than SVC
and that inspiratory–expiratory loop abnormalities help localize intrathoracic
central airway obstruction.[1] Tracheomalacia is classically associated with TEF/
EA and VACTERL, and presents with barking/harsh cough and expiratory noise;
expiratory flow–volume “scooping” and dynamic collapse are well described in
pediatric tracheobronchomalacia.[2][3][4][5]
Alternatives are less consistent:
Bronchospasm primarily causes airflow limitation but does not typically
explain a marked SVC > FVC difference attributable to dynamic central
airway collapse.[6][7][8]
Laryngeal braking and extrathoracic lesions (subglottic mass, upper airway
compression) produce inspiratory plateaus or variable extrathoracic
patterns rather than intrathoracic expiratory collapse with scooped
expiratory limb.[1]
Thus, dynamic intrathoracic airway collapse best explains the discrepant FVC
and SVC in this patient.[1][2][3][4][5]
references: 54 L (78% of predicted) and a slow vital
Tracheobronchomalacia and Excessive Dynamic Airway Collapse: Current

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A chief medical officer at a hospital has received several reports that one of
her colleagues, a 64-year-old male
surgeon, has “not been acting like himself” recently. An administrative
assistant says that during the past 6 months,
the surgeon’s dictations have become hard to follow and appear to be
missing information; previously, they were
comprehensive and easy to transcribe. The assistant adds that after the
surgeon missed three meetings in a row
during the past 2 months, he asked her to send him a text message 5
minutes before the next meeting to let him
know its location. Previously, he was always in attendance and on time for
meetings. A senior resident also has
noted unusual behavior by the surgeon; the resident says that while on
rounds 3 days ago, the surgeon listed
incorrect indications for a specific surgical procedure. A scrub nurse
reported that the surgeon used unsterile
practices three times during a 2-hour procedure today. The chief medical
officer meets with the surgeon and asks
him about these reported incidents. The surgeon says that “nothing is
wrong” and the people who reported him are
angry with him for various reasons and “are trying to stir up trouble.”
Which of the following is the most appropriate
action for the chief medical officer to take?

options.A: Accept the surgeon’s answer and only pursue further action if there is
options.B: Determine whether other staff have noticed anything different about
options.C: Inform the surgeon that he should consider retirement
options.D: Instruct the surgeon to take a temporary leave of absence and undergo

explanation:
The most appropriate action is to Instruct the surgeon to take a temporary
leave of absence and undergo a medical evaluation.
Recent cognitive and behavioral changes with patient safety implications
(incorrect indications, unsterile technique) constitute a sentinel concern that
warrants immediate restriction of clinical duties and prompt, formal fitness-for-
duty evaluation, irrespective of the surgeon’s denial or lack of insight. Deferring
action until further complaints or informally canvassing staff risks patient harm
and delays necessary assessment.[1][2][3][4]
Best practices include:
Immediate temporary removal from clinical duties to protect patients while
evaluation proceeds.[2][3][4]
Comprehensive assessment encompassing medical, psychiatric, substance
use, sensory, and cognitive/neuropsychological testing (e.g., MoCA,
MicroCog; escalation to full neuropsychological assessment as indicated) to
determine fitness and guide remediation or scope modification.[1][2][3][5]
[6][7][8]
A supportive, policy-based process through the medical staff well-being/
credentialing structure, with pathways for remediation, restriction, or
retirement calibrated to findings.[1][2][3][5]
• 
• 
• 
Rationale: Physicians are poor self-assessors; peer reports alone are insufficient
safeguards. Institutional policies and the American College of Physicians
emphasize acting on credible concerns to ensure patient safety, with
rehabilitation and reintegration when possible.[1][3][7][8]
references: The Impaired Surgeon. Sudan R, Seymour K.. The Surgical Clinics of North

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 33-year-old woman is admitted to the hospital because of visual field loss
following a 48-hour history of acute eye
pain. She also has a 3-week history of numbness and a feeling of
"heaviness" in both legs. She has no history of
serious illness and takes no medications. Six weeks ago, she returned from
a hiking expedition in the northeastern
USA; she has no recent history of international travel. Vital signs are within
normal limits. Muscle strength is 5/5 in
the upper extremities, 3/5 in the hip flexors and knee extensors, and 4/5
in the knee flexors and ankle dorsiflexors.
Patellar and ankle reflexes are 3+ bilaterally, and there is nonsustained
ankle clonus bilaterally. Sensation to
pinprick is absent throughout the lower extremities. Ophthalmologic
examination discloses optic neuritis and
reconfirms the visual field loss. Which of the following studies is most likely
to confirm the diagnosis?

options.A: CT scan of the cervical spine
options.C: Lumbar puncture for examination of cerebrospinal fluid
options.D: Measurement of serum Lyme (Borrelia burgdorferi) antibody

explanation:
Magnetic resonance imaging of the brain
The presentation (acute optic neuritis, painful visual loss, and concurrent
pyramidal and sensory signs with hyperreflexia and clonus) is classic for a
demyelinating clinically isolated syndrome highly suggestive of multiple sclerosis
(MS). Brain MRI is the most sensitive test to demonstrate characteristic
demyelinating lesions in typical locations and to establish dissemination in space
and, with contrast or new lesions over time, dissemination in time, enabling
confirmation per contemporary diagnostic practice.[1][2][3][4] In optic neuritis
cohorts, abnormal brain MRI strongly predicts conversion to MS and
outperforms other modalities for initial confirmation, while optic nerve/3D-DIR
sequences further increase sensitivity to optic pathway involvement in MS
contexts.[1][2][3]
CSF analysis for oligoclonal bands supports diagnosis and can substitute for
dissemination in time under the 2017 McDonald framework, increasing
diagnostic sensitivity when MRI shows dissemination in space; however, MRI
remains the primary study to confirm MS in this clinical scenario and LP is
typically reserved for atypical or equivocal cases.[2][4][5][6][7]
Computed tomography lacks sensitivity for demyelination. Electromyography/
nerve conduction studies evaluate peripheral neuropathies and neuromuscular
disorders rather than central demyelination, and are not confirmatory here.[8][9]
Lyme serology is not first-line in this presentation without specific
epidemiologic or clinical features suggesting neuroborreliosis.
references: Brownscheidle CM.. Annals of Neurology. 1997;41(3):392-8. doi:10.1002/
CSF Abnormalities Can Be Predicted by VEP and MRI Pathology in the

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 68-year-old woman comes to the office because she is concerned about
developing Alzheimer disease. She says
she has had increasing difficulty recalling names of people and objects
during the past 6 months. She has not had
difficulty with her activities of daily living, finances, or shopping. She
teaches part-time at a local community
college. She has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. She
does not smoke cigarettes. She drinks
one 5-oz glass of wine daily. Her mother developed dementia, Alzheimer
type, in her mid 70s. Physical and
neurologic examinations of the patient show no abnormalities. On
cognitive testing, she has delayed recall of three
of five words. She is able to perform serial sevens, knows similarities, is
fully oriented, and has intact executive,
visuospatial, and attention abilities. Which of the following is the most
appropriate next step in management?

options.C: Measurement of beta-amyloid in the cerebrospinal fluid
options.D: MRI of the brain with volumetric analysis
options.E: No additional diagnostic studies are indicated

explanation:
No additional diagnostic studies are indicated
This presentation is typical amnestic mild cognitive impairment (MCI): objective
memory deficit with preserved IADLs and normal neurologic exam. In such
cases, consensus guidance emphasizes clinical monitoring and risk-factor
management rather than advanced biomarker testing, because routine use of
amyloid PET, CSF biomarkers, APOE genotyping, or MRI volumetrics does not
change management or improve outcomes in typical MCI without atypical
features or rapid progression.[1][2][3][4][5][6] The American Academy of
Neurology (AAN) advises serial follow-up to track progression and address
modifiable contributors (e.g., vascular risk, depression, medications), with
counseling on exercise and cognitive/social engagement; no disease-modifying
pharmacotherapy has proven benefit at this stage.[1][2]
Why not the other options:
Amyloid PET: Appropriate only when results are expected to alter
management after expert evaluation in atypical/unclear cases; not for
routine MCI with typical features, and outcome benefit is unproven.[4][5]
APOE genotyping: Not recommended for routine evaluation; does not alter
management.[4]
CSF beta-amyloid: May be considered when diagnosis is unclear or atypical;
not routinely indicated in straightforward MCI.[4]
MRI with volumetrics: Imaging in MCI is adjunctive to exclude structural
mimics when clinically suspected; routine volumetrics are not required in a
typical case.[3]
Plan: longitudinal cognitive follow-up; optimize vascular risk factors, sleep,
mood; encourage aerobic exercise and cognitive/social engagement.[1][2]
• 
• 
• 
•
references: ACR Appropriateness Criteria® Dementia: 2024 Update. Soderlund KA,
0. Minoshima S, Drzezga AE, Barthel H, et al.. Journal

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 55-year-old man with major depressive disorder is brought to the
emergency department 1 1/2 hours after he was
found by his brother in his home acting strangely. On arrival, he is babbling
incoherently and cannot answer
questions. There are two empty pill bottles in his pockets; one is for
paroxetine and the other is for duloxetine. He is
restless, diaphoretic, and says he has a severe headache. Temperature is
38.5°C (101.3°F), pulse is 130/min, and
blood pressure is 145/105 mm Hg. The pupils are 8 mm and reactive to
light. Deep tendon reflexes are 4+ and
symmetric in all extremities. There is spontaneous myoclonus in the upper
extremities. Results of laboratory studies,
including a complete blood count, serum electrolyte concentrations,
creatine phosphokinase activity, and thyroid
function tests, are within the reference ranges. After admitting the patient
to the hospital for observation and
supportive care, what are you going to give or change in terms of
pharmacotherapy?

options.A: Add carbamazepine to the medication regimen
options.B: Add dantrolene to the medication regimen
options.C: Add haloperidol to the medication regimen

explanation:
The presentation (abrupt onset after overdose of two serotonergic agents,
agitation, hyperthermia 38.5°C, tachycardia, hypertension, dilated pupils,
hyperreflexia 4+, and spontaneous myoclonus) meets Hunter/Sternbach features
of serotonin syndrome rather than NMS or anticholinergic toxicity.[1][2][3][4][5]
[6] First-line management is immediate cessation of serotonergic drugs and
supportive care; antidopamine agents or dantrolene are not indicated for
serotonin toxicity.[4][5][7][8][9] Cyproheptadine can be used for moderate–
severe cases but is not the pharmacotherapy “to add” in this stem after initial
stabilization.[4][5][8]
Therefore, the best choice is to stop the offending agents: Discontinue
paroxetine and duloxetine.[4][5][7][8][9]
Why not the other options:
Dantrolene treats NMS, not serotonin syndrome; its use in SS lacks
evidence.[2][9]
Haloperidol and olanzapine may worsen hyperthermia/rigidity or confound
NMS vs SS; they do not treat SS pathophysiology.[2][9]
Carbamazepine offers no benefit for SS and adds drug–drug risks.
The following comparative table underscores the distinguishing neuromuscular
findings (hyperreflexia/clonus in SS vs rigidity and reduced reflexes in NMS),
supporting discontinuation of serotonergic agents as the correct action in this
scenario.[2]
This table contrasts serotonin syndrome with NMS across key clinical features,
highlighting hyperreflexia and clonus as signals of serotonin toxicity that direct
management toward stopping serotonergic agents and supportive care rather
than dantrolene or antipsychotics.
• 
• 
• 
Table 1. Disorders That Simulate Neuroleptic Malignant Syndrome (NMS).*

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 32-year-old man comes to the office for a follow-up examination 2
months after he was diagnosed with
hypertension. He arrives late for the appointment, stating that he had to
make sure no one followed him here because
some "drug dealers" are after him. He insists that he be seen in an
examination room with no windows. He says he is
"fine" but is reluctant to answer questions because "doctors hold
everything against me." He doubts his diagnosis
and says, "Doctors and drug companies are in cahoots trying to get my
money." He refuses to speak with the office
manager about his bill because he thinks "she looks at me funny and makes
nasty remarks about me behind my
back." He says he has no history of psychiatric illness; his medical record
indicates that he has made similar
statements during the past 7 years concerning doctors and office staff but
he always refused to elaborate on his
statements. His medications are metoprolol and hydrochlorothiazide. He
does not drink alcohol or use illicit drugs.
Vital signs are within normal limits. Physical and neurologic examinations
show no abnormalities. Results of
screening laboratory studies are within the reference ranges. Which of the
following is the most appropriate next
step in management?

options.D: Determine whether the patient has friends or family who can monitor
options.E: Determine whether the patient has had thoughts of harming others

explanation:
Determine whether the patient has had thoughts of harming others.
Safety assessment is the first priority in a patient with chronic paranoid ideation.
The American Psychiatric Association recommends that the initial psychiatric
evaluation explicitly assess current and prior homicidal or aggressive thoughts,
past violent behaviors, access to weapons, targets, and related risk factors, with
documentation of risk estimation.[1] Paranoid delusions can be associated with
violence risk; rapid determination of dangerousness informs need for urgent
referral, possible involuntary hold, or hospitalization as outlined in pediatric-to-
young-adult care pathways when safety is a concern.[2] Only after establishing
safety should treatment (e.g., antipsychotic initiation for suspected delusional
disorder) be pursued; while second-generation antipsychotics such as
risperidone can be effective in persistent delusional disorder, therapy selection
and setting hinge on the risk assessment.[3][4]
Practical next steps after asking about homicidal ideation include:
If any positive or concerning responses: assess specifics (intent, plan,
means, targets), access to firearms, impulsivity, substance use, and arrange
ED/psychiatric evaluation urgently as needed.[1][2]
If risk is low and no dangerousness: proceed with outpatient management,
including discussing antipsychotic options and adherence strategies, and
consider collateral information and monitoring supports.
• 
• 
Initiating clonazepam or sertraline does not address psychosis and is not
appropriate before safety is established; starting risperidone may be reasonable
subsequently but should follow a structured risk assessment.[1][2][3]

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
An 87-year-old woman, gravida 2, para 2, is brought to the emergency
department from a skilled nursing care
facility because of profuse vaginal bleeding for 2 days. At the age of 41
years, she underwent a cold knife cone
biopsy for cervical dysplasia. Annual follow-up Pap smears until the age of
65 years showed no abnormalities. She
is in moderate distress. Temperature is 36.1°C (97.0°F), pulse is 84/min,
respirations are 30/min, and blood pressure
is 140/90 mm Hg. Abdominal examination shows mild distention and
tenderness; no masses are palpated. Pelvic
examination shows a large verrucous lesion on the perineum partially
covering the anal verge. Speculum
examination shows atrophic changes on the vagina and cervix. Which of
the following is the most likely explanation
for these findings?
explanation:
Vulvar carcinoma
A large verrucous perineal lesion in an elderly woman, partially covering the anal
verge, with associated bleeding is most consistent with vulvar squamous cell
carcinoma, often with verrucous morphology in this age group. Verrucous
carcinoma of the vulva presents as an exophytic, warty mass in older women and
may bleed or ulcerate.[1][2][3][4] The anatomic location (perineum/vulva
abutting the anal verge) and morphology argue against rectal carcinoma or
polyps, which typically present intraluminally; external hemorrhoids are not
verrucous and usually cause perianal pain/itching with defecation rather than
profuse vaginal bleeding.[5] Cervical carcinoma is less likely given decades of
normal screening through age 65, atrophic cervix on exam, and the lesion’s
location and appearance.[6] While colorectal and anal primaries can rarely mimic
gynecologic disease, the described exophytic verrucous vulvar/perineal mass in
this clinical context favors a primary vulvar malignancy.[7] Biopsy is required for
confirmation and staging.[2]

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 47-year-old woman comes to the office for a routine health maintenance
examination. She feels well and has no
history of serious illness. Examination shows a 2-cm, soft, nontender
nodule in the right lobe of the thyroid gland.
There is no lymphadenopathy. Her serum thyroid-stimulating hormone
concentration is within the reference range.
Which of the following is the most appropriate initial step in management?
explanation:
The most appropriate initial step is to obtain a diagnostic neck ultrasound. In an
asymptomatic, euthyroid patient with a newly palpated 2-cm thyroid nodule,
ultrasound is the first test to characterize composition, echogenicity, margins,
calcifications, shape, and cervical nodes; these sonographic features, together
with size, determine whether fine-needle aspiration (FNA) is indicated.[1][2][3][4]
[5] CT and surgery are not first-line without compressive or invasive features;
short-interval clinical reexamination without imaging is not recommended.[1][6]
[7]
Therefore, the correct choice is: Ultrasonography of the thyroid gland.
How ultrasound guides next steps:
If high/intermediate suspicion features are present, US-guided FNA is
recommended at ≥1 cm; for low suspicion, ≥1.5 cm; for very low suspicion,
consider FNA at ≥2 cm or observation. Given a 2-cm nodule, FNA would be
indicated if the ultrasound is ≥low suspicion.[4][5]
The table below, endorsed by contemporary guidelines, summarizes how
standardized ultrasound risk systems direct FNA decisions, which is precisely
why ultrasound is the first step.
• 
Table 1. Standardized Sonographic Scoring Systems Proposed or Endorsed by Practice Guidelines for
Risk-Based Fine-Needle Aspiration Biopsy Guidance for Thyroid Nodules
Used under license from JAMA Network.
Why other options are not first:
Fine-needle aspiration: performed selectively based on ultrasound
features/size, not before ultrasound.[1][4][5]
CT neck: not recommended for routine initial evaluation; reserved for
suspected invasive disease, substernal extension, or nodal disease not well-
visualized on ultrasound.[6][7]
Surgical excision: not indicated without cytologic/clinical suspicion.[1][4]
[5]
• 
• 
• 
Reexamination in 3 months: delays essential risk stratification; ultrasound
should be performed now.[1][5]
references: Standardized Sonographic Scoring Systems Proposed or Endorsed by Practice Guidelines for
Contemporary Management of Thyroid Nodules. Kobaly K, Kim CS, Mandel
2015 American Thyroid Association Management Guidelines for Adult

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 1-hour-old male newborn is examined in the hospital nursery. He was
born at term to a 22-year-old woman,
gravida 1, para 1, with HIV infection. The mother took antiretroviral therapy
throughout her pregnancy, and her HIV
viral load was consistently <1000 copies/mL. She received intravenous
zidovudine (AZT) during labor. The
patient’s Apgar scores were 8 and 9 at 1 and 5 minutes, respectively. He is at
the 50th percentile for length, weight,
and head circumference. He appears well. Examination shows no
abnormalities. In addition to administration of the
hepatitis B vaccine, which of the following is the most appropriate
pharmacotherapy for this newborn?
explanation:
Zidovudine (AZT)
This term newborn is low risk for perinatal HIV transmission given maternal ART
with sustained low viral load and intrapartum IV zidovudine. U.S. guidance
(American Academy of Pediatrics and HHS Perinatal Panel) recommends
neonatal zidovudine monotherapy started as soon as possible after birth
(preferably within 6 hours). Duration can be shortened in low-risk infants;
current HHS guidance favors 2 weeks for infants with sustained maternal
suppression, with longer courses used in higher-risk scenarios.[1][2][3][4] Typical
dosing for term infants: ZDV 4 mg/kg orally every 12 hours (if IV needed: 3 mg/
kg every 12 hours); adjust per gestational age.
Multi-drug presumptive therapy is reserved for high-risk exposures (e.g.,
maternal viremia near delivery, no antepartum ART, or drug resistance).[1][2]
Lopinavir-ritonavir is not recommended in neonates due to safety concerns and
age restrictions, particularly in the immediate postnatal period.[1][2][5][6]
Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole is not indicated at birth; it is used starting at 4–
6 weeks only for infants with indeterminate or confirmed HIV infection for
Pneumocystis prophylaxis.[1] Penicillin is used for congenital syphilis, not HIV
prophylaxis.[7]
references: Kaletra. Food and Drug Administration. Updated date: 2023-04-01

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 25-year-old woman comes to the office because she has not had a
menstrual period for 6 months. Previously,
menses occurred at regular 28-day intervals. She has not had blurred or
double vision. She has no history of serious
illness and takes no medications. Temperature is 37.0°C (98.6°F), pulse is
72/min, and blood pressure is
108/60 mm Hg. The pupils are equal and reactive to light; extraocular
muscles are intact. Funduscopic examination
shows no abnormalities. Visual field testing shows bitemporal hemianopia.
Milky discharge can be expressed from
the nipples. Pelvic examination shows no abnormalities. Serum studies
show a thyroid-stimulating hormone
concentration of 2.0 μU/mL and a prolactin concentration of 100 ng/mL. A
urine pregnancy test is negative. MRI of
the pituitary gland shows a 1.2 × 1.5-cm mass compressing the optic
chiasm. Which of the following is the most
appropriate next step in management?

options.C: Gamma knife radiation to the pituitary gland

explanation:
The most appropriate next step is Cabergoline therapy.
Rationale:
This presentation is classic for a macroprolactinoma (1.2 × 1.5 cm) with
chiasmal compression causing bitemporal hemianopia and
hyperprolactinemia. First-line therapy for prolactinomas, including
macroadenomas with visual field defects, is a dopamine agonist (preferably
cabergoline), which both normalizes prolactin and rapidly shrinks tumor
volume, often reversing visual compromise. According to a review
published in JAMA:[1][2][3][4][5]
"Dopamine agonist therapy is first-line treatment for patients with
prolactinomas and for patients with idiopathic hyperprolactinemia
(hyperprolactinemia without a discernible cause) who are
symptomatic, those with macroadenomas, or those who are
considering pregnancy. Bromocriptine (2.5-10 mg/d orally) and
cabergoline (0.5-2.0 mg/wk orally) are approved by the US Food and
Drug Administration (FDA) for treating hyperprolactinemia and
prolactinomas."
—Nicholas A. Tritos, MD, DSc1,2 et al., Neuroendocrine Unit,
Massachusetts General Hospital, Boston and other institutions. 
Diagnosis and Management of Pituitary Adenomas: A Review. JAMA.
April 25, 2023.
Used under license from JAMA Network.
Cabergoline typical starting dose: 0.25–0.5 mg twice weekly (0.5–1 mg/
week), titrated to normalize prolactin and reduce tumor size, with close
follow-up of visual fields and MRI to document early decompression. Rapid
• 
• 
visual improvement can occur within days to weeks as the tumor involutes.
[2][3][4]
Surgery is not first-line in the absence of acute, rapidly progressive vision
loss, apoplexy, dopamine-agonist intolerance, or resistance. Gamma knife is
reserved for refractory disease after medical and surgical options. Biopsy is
unnecessary given classic biochemical and radiographic findings. Oral
contraceptives do not treat the underlying adenoma.[1][2][4][6]
Additional note:
Comparative data suggest similar short-term visual outcomes between
initial medical and surgical approaches in macro/giant prolactinomas,
further supporting initial dopamine agonist therapy unless there is urgent
neuro-ophthalmologic deterioration.[7]
Answer: Cabergoline therapy (choice B).

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 2-week-old boy is evaluated in the neonatal intensive care unit because
of temperature instability and decreased
activity. He was born by cesarean delivery at 30 weeks’ gestation because
of premature rupture of membranes. Since birth, he has been fed breast
milk by orogastric tube. He was feeding and growing appropriately until
today, when
his axillary temperature was 36.5°C (97.7°F) and he had decreased activity.
During the past 4 hours, gastric residue
has been noted in the orogastric tube when it is aspirated prior to feedings.
Temperature is now 37.0°C (98.6°F),
pulse is 160/min, respirations are 50/min, and blood pressure is 70/40 mm
Hg. The anterior fontanel is soft.
Cardiopulmonary examination shows no abnormalities. The abdomen is
mildly distended, and there is mild
tenderness to palpation diffusely; there are no masses. Bowel sounds are
decreased. There is a small amount of
blood-streaked stool in the diaper. The remainder of the examination
shows no abnormalities. Leukocyte count is
18,000/mm³ (N=5000–34,000) (80% segmented neutrophils, 5% bands, 10%
lymphocytes, and 5% monocytes), and
platelet count is 100,000/mm³. Blood culture results are pending. Which of
the following is most likely to confirm the diagnosis?

options.A: may identify coagulopathy but does not confirm NEC.
options.C: evaluates infectious diarrhea; yield is limited and not
options.D: is not first-line in suspected NEC given risk of

explanation:
E) X-ray of the abdomen
Abdominal radiography is the most likely study to confirm necrotizing
enterocolitis (NEC) in this clinical context. Classic radiographic hallmarks include
pneumatosis intestinalis, portal venous gas, and pneumoperitoneum, which are
specific for NEC and establish the diagnosis in a compatible clinical setting. This
patient’s temperature instability, abdominal distension, decreased bowel sounds,
blood-streaked stool, and thrombocytopenia are highly concerning for NEC,
where abdominal X-ray is the diagnostic test of choice due to speed, availability,
and ability to visualize pathognomonic findings.[1]
PT/PTT (A) may identify coagulopathy but does not confirm NEC. 
Stool reducing substances (B) assess carbohydrate malabsorption; not
diagnostic for NEC. 
Stool culture (C) evaluates infectious diarrhea; yield is limited and not
confirmatory for NEC, and culture-independent tests are preferred when
bacterial gastroenteritis is suspected per the Infectious Diseases Society of
America. [2]
Upper GI series (D) is not first-line in suspected NEC given risk of
perforation and limited utility.
In neonates with hematochezia and abdominal distension, NEC is a leading
cause; abdominal X-ray provides specific evidence to confirm the diagnosis and
guide urgent management.[1][2]

• 
• 
•
