question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 13-month-old boy is brought to a small rural emergency department (ED)
near his family's community because of an erythematous, edematous mass
in his perianal area that is hot to touch. He has no history of serious illness
and receives no medications. Growth and development are appropriate for
age. Vaccinations are up-to-date. He is at the 50th percentile for length
and weight. Temperature is 39.4°C (103.0°F), pulse is 160/min, and
respirations are 36/min. Examination shows fluctuance and exquisite
tenderness to palpation of the perianal mass. Oral acetaminophen and a
dose of oral cephalexin are administered, and the parents are told that
their child’s case is beyond the capabilities of the hospital. They are
referred to the nearest hospital with a pediatric service, a suburban
hospital 50 miles away. The suburban hospital is contacted regarding the
referral and a pediatric surgeon accepts transfer of the patient. When the
patient is brought to the ED of the suburban hospital, a triage nurse
obtains his oral temperature of 37.7°C (99.8°F) and pulse of 126/min. The
nurse notes that the family has no health insurance and states that the case
is beyond the capabilities of the hospital. The nurse recommends taking
the patient to the ED of a downtown university hospital 4 miles away. The
parents follow this recommendation, and the patient undergoes incision
and drainage of the abscess at the downtown hospital later that day. Which
of the following is the most accurate characterization of this patient's
pattern of care?

options.A: The patient received appropriate care under the circumstances
options.B: The pediatric surgeon at the suburban hospital should have insisted on
options.C: The rural hospital should have sent the patient directly to the
options.D: The suburban hospital executed unethical but legal conduct in this case
options.E: The suburban hospital violated the Emergency Medical Treatment and

explanation:
(E) The suburban hospital violated the Emergency Medical Treatment and
Active Labor Act (EMTALA)
EMTALA imposes a nondiscriminatory duty on Medicare-participating hospitals
with emergency departments to provide an appropriate medical screening
examination (MSE) to anyone who presents for emergency care and to provide
necessary stabilizing treatment or an appropriate transfer if an emergency
medical condition exists, regardless of insurance status.[1][2][3] The American
College of Emergency Physicians emphasizes that the MSE and stabilizing care
must not be delayed or denied based on ability to pay and that on-call specialists
must respond within a reasonable time to provide stabilizing care within the
hospital’s capabilities.[4] Pediatric patients are fully covered by EMTALA; consent
or insurance issues cannot delay the MSE or stabilizing treatment, as noted by
the American College of Emergency Physicians’ guidance on minors.[5][6]
Here, the suburban hospital accepted the transfer for a child with a likely
perianal abscess—an emergent, painful condition that typically requires timely
incision and drainage. Redirecting the family upon arrival based solely on lack of
insurance, without an MSE and stabilizing care, constitutes impermissible
“patient dumping” under EMTALA’s screening and stabilization mandates.[1][2][7]
The literature further underscores that financially motivated redirection or
transfer is the type of conduct EMTALA was enacted to prevent.[8][9][10]
references: EMTALA (Emergency Medical Treatment and Active Labor Act) Obligations:
The Implications of the Emergency Medical Treatment and Labor Act and

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 25-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department by
paramedics because of a seizure that began 45
minutes ago at home. Intravenous antiepileptic therapy begun en route to
the hospital has not stopped her seizure.
Her parents report that she initially began crying, then fell to the floor and
exhibited asymmetric kicking and
thrashing of all extremities. Fifteen years ago, she was diagnosed with a
seizure disorder. Her seizures had been well
controlled with oral antiepileptic therapy until this morning. Today, while
the physician is in the patient’s room, the
movements of her extremities gradually subside. The patient then sits up
and asks the physician what happened. She
is fully oriented. Vital signs are within normal limits. Examination shows no
evidence of tongue biting, urinary
incontinence, or other abnormalities. Which of the following is most likely
to confirm this patient’s diagnosis?

options.C: Measurement of serum creatine kinase activity

explanation:
The semiology described (prolonged event, ictal crying, asynchronous
thrashing/kicking, preserved orientation, no tongue biting/incontinence, and
refractoriness to AEDs) is most consistent with psychogenic nonepileptic
seizures (PNES). The diagnostic gold standard is capturing a typical event
without ictal epileptiform activity on simultaneous video-EEG. Therefore, the
best confirmatory test is: Video electroencephalogram monitoring.
PNES is suggested by long duration, fluctuating/asynchronous movements,
ictal crying, eye closure, and absence of postictal confusion or injury. [1][2]
[3]
While neuropsychological testing and psychometrics may support the
assessment, they do not confirm PNES and have limited standalone
discriminative power. [4][5]
Postictal labs (CK, prolactin) can be supportive but are neither necessary
nor sufficient to confirm PNES and have limited sensitivity/negative
predictive value. [6][7][8]
Drug levels do not distinguish PNES from epilepsy. 
In refractory or atypical cases, inpatient EMU evaluation with vEEG is
recommended; expert consensus and the National Association of Epilepsy
Centers emphasize vEEG as essential for differentiating epileptic from
nonepileptic events.[9][10][11]
references: Avbersek A, Sisodiya S.. Journal of Neurology, Neurosurgery, and Psychiatry.

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 32-year-old woman comes to the emergency department because of a 1-
day history of sharp, right-sided chest
pain that worsens with coughing and sneezing. Four days ago, she had a
mild sore throat and runny nose followed
by nonproductive cough 1 day later. Over-the-counter decongestant and
aspirin mildly relieved the symptoms. She
has not had shortness of breath, blood-tinged sputum, fever, or chills. She
has a long-standing history of recurrent
aphthous ulcers. Her only medication is an oral contraceptive. Temperature
is 37.2°C (99.0°F), pulse is 65/min, and
respirations are 14/min. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen
saturation of 99%. Splinting is observed over
the right hemithorax with deep breathing. On cardiac examination, no
abnormalities are heard. The remainder of the
examination shows no abnormalities. Chest x-ray shows no abnormalities.
Which of the following is the most
appropriate next step in management?
explanation:
Ibuprofen therapy
The presentation (young woman with viral prodrome, sharp pleuritic right-sided
chest pain, normal vitals and oxygenation, normal chest radiograph, and chest
wall splinting) is most consistent with viral pleurisy/nonspecific pleuritic chest
pain. For uncomplicated, low-risk pleuritic chest pain after a viral URI, first-line
management is NSAIDs for analgesia and anti-inflammatory effect; further
testing is not indicated when serious causes have been reasonably excluded
clinically and by chest radiography.[1][2][3][4] The American College of
Cardiology/American Heart Association multisociety chest pain guideline
emphasizes prioritizing exclusion of life-threatening causes; in a low-risk patient
without red flags, additional testing (ECG, CTA, echocardiography) is not
routinely required.[2][3] Pericarditis is unlikely given absence of positional
component, friction rub, ECG changes, or effusion; if suspected, NSAIDs are also
first-line.[5]
Reason against azithromycin: no evidence of bacterial pneumonia (afebrile,
normal CXR), and antibiotics are not indicated for acute bronchitis/viral
pleurisy in this setting.[1][6][7]
Reason against prednisone: no indication; short courses carry measurable
risks (e.g., VTE), particularly relevant in an oral contraceptive user.[8]
Reason against CTA/ECG/echo: no features suggesting PE, ACS, or
structural cardiac disease; PE pretest probability is low despite OCP use,
and oxygenation/exam are normal.[1][2][3][4]
Typical NSAID dosing for pleuritic pain: ibuprofen 400–600 mg orally every 6–8
hours as needed, for several days with gastroprotection as appropriate.[1][5]
Reassess if red flags develop (dyspnea, hypoxemia, fever, hemoptysis, syncope,
calf pain/swelling) or pain persists/worsens.
• 
• 
•
references: Navigating a Complicated World: The American Heart Association/
Diagnosis, Risk Stratification, and Treatment of Pericarditis: A Review.

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 30-year-old nulligravid woman, who is a military veteran, comes to the
office because she has not had a menstrual
period for the past year. She has not had nausea, vomiting, or night sweats.
Menses previously occurred at regular
28-day intervals. She is sexually active with one male partner and uses
condoms consistently. Three weeks ago, she
returned from a 1-year deployment in the Middle East, where she worked
as an army combat medic. She resumed
work as a teacher 2 weeks ago. She appears tearful but otherwise in no
distress. Temperature is 37.1°C (98.7°F),
pulse is 88/min, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 116/72 mm
Hg. Physical examination, including
pelvic examination, shows no abnormalities. Serum studies show:
Thyroid-stimulating hormone: 2 μU/mL
Follicle-stimulating hormone: 7 mIU/mL
Luteinizing hormone: 10 mIU/mL
Prolactin: 10 ng/mL
β-hCG: negative
Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient’s
amenorrhea?
explanation:
The most likely cause is hypothalamic hypogonadism (functional hypothalamic
amenorrhea). This patient has secondary amenorrhea with normal TSH and
prolactin, low–normal gonadotropins (FSH 7 mIU/mL, LH 10 mIU/mL), and no
vasomotor or hyperandrogenic features. Her recent prolonged military
deployment and transition stress are classic precipitants of stress-related GnRH
suppression leading to anovulation and amenorrhea.[1][2][3][4][5] The Endocrine
Society (US) emphasizes psychosocial stress, energy imbalance, and intensive
training (including military settings) as common triggers for FHA with reduced
GnRH/LH pulsatility and low/normal gonadotropins.[3][5] Alternative diagnoses
are less consistent: Turner syndrome and primary ovarian insufficiency typically
show elevated FSH; prolactinoma shows hyperprolactinemia; and PCOS requires
hyperandrogenism and/or polycystic ovarian morphology, which are absent
here.[2][6][7][8]
To clarify the ovulatory dysfunction differential that includes PCOS, it is helpful
to recall that PCOS diagnosis requires combinations of hyperandrogenism,
ovulatory dysfunction, and polycystic ovaries; this patient lacks clinical/
biochemical hyperandrogenism and has long-standing amenorrhea temporally
linked to stress, favoring FHA over PCOS.[6]
Table 1. Diagnostic Criteria for the Polycystic Ovary Syndrome.
Used under license from The New England Journal of Medicine.
Clinical pearls:
FHA: stress/energy deficit/exercise; low or low-normal LH/FSH; normal
prolactin/TSH; anovulation.[1][2][3][4]
PCOS: requires hyperandrogenism and/or polycystic ovarian morphology
plus ovulatory dysfunction; typically features clinical or biochemical
hyperandrogenism.[6]
POI/Turner: hypergonadotropic hypogonadism (elevated FSH) and often
vasomotor symptoms.[7][8]
Prolactinoma: elevated prolactin and often galactorrhea.[2]
Next steps for FHA: confirm exclusion of pregnancy/organic causes, assess
stress, nutrition, and exercise; address modifiable triggers; consider bone health
assessment; and reserve pharmacologic ovulation induction only if fertility is
desired and lifestyle measures fail.[1][3][4]
• 
• 
• 
•
references: Diagnostic Criteria for the Polycystic Ovary Syndrome.
Functional Hypothalamic Amenorrhea: An Endocrine Society Clinical

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 45-year-old woman comes to the office because she has not had a
menstrual period for 1 year. Menses previously
occurred at regular 30-day intervals. She feels well, has no history of
serious illness, and takes no medications. She
has smoked one-half pack of cigarettes daily for 20 years. She is sexually
active with one male partner and uses
condoms consistently. She is 163 cm (5 ft 4 in) tall and weighs 63 kg (140 lb);
BMI is 24 kg/m². Vital signs are
within normal limits. Physical examination, including pelvic examination,
shows no abnormalities. Measurement of
which of the following serum concentrations is most likely to confirm the
diagnosis?
explanation:
The clinical picture is consistent with natural menopause (12 months of
amenorrhea at age 45 without other causes). The single serum measurement
most likely to confirm ovarian failure in this context is an elevated follicle-
stimulating hormone, reflecting loss of ovarian negative feedback; estradiol and
LH fluctuate and are less reliable, and beta-hCG is used to exclude pregnancy,
not confirm menopause. Smoking increases the likelihood of earlier menopause
in this patient’s age range, further supporting the expectation of elevated FSH.[1]
[2][3][4][5][6][7]
Answer: Follicle-stimulating hormone
The diagnostic approach for ovarian insufficiency/menopause emphasizes
measuring FSH (and, when needed, confirming with concurrent low estradiol), as
shown below.[1] Although the Choosing Wisely statement advises against using
FSH to “identify the menopausal transition,” once 12 months of amenorrhea have
occurred, FSH elevation is the most specific laboratory corroboration of ovarian
failure.[1][2][3][4]
Figure 1. Diagnostic Approach in a Patient with Possible Primary Ovarian Insufficiency.
Used under license from The New England Journal of Medicine.
references: Diagnostic Approach in a Patient with Possible Primary Ovarian Insufficiency.
Management of Menopausal Symptoms: A Review. Crandall CJ, Mehta JM,
Cigarette Smoking and Risk of Early Natural Menopause. Whitcomb BW,

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 2-month-old girl is brought to the office by her mother for a routine
examination. Newborn screening
electrophoresis showed hemoglobin FS. The mother says her daughter has
not had fever or pallor. The patient is
feeding well and gaining weight appropriately. She receives no medications.
She appears well. She is at the 35th
percentile for length and weight. Temperature is 37.0°C (98.6°F), pulse is
158/min, and respirations are 28/min.
Examination shows no abnormalities. All routine immunizations are
administered. In addition to routine anticipatory
guidance about feeding and development, the mother is instructed to
observe the patient for pallor and fever and to
palpate for an enlarged spleen. Which of the following is most appropriate
to prevent complications in this patient?

options.A: Amoxicillin as needed for febrile illness
options.B: Instructions for the parents to palpate the long bones routinely
options.C: Instructions for the parents to take and record the patient’s pulse daily

explanation:
Oral prophylactic penicillin therapy
Hemoglobin FS on newborn screening indicates sickle cell disease (most
commonly HbSS), with functional asplenia in early infancy and high risk for
invasive pneumococcal disease. The American Academy of Pediatrics
recommends initiating penicillin V potassium prophylaxis by age 2 months for all
infants with HbSS or Sβ0-thalassemia to prevent life-threatening pneumococcal
sepsis, increasing the dose at age 3 years, and continuing until at least age 5
years or completion of an appropriate pneumococcal vaccine series, with
consideration of longer use in select patients (e.g., prior invasive pneumococcal
disease or postsplenectomy).[1] GeneReviews likewise recommends penicillin V
125 mg PO twice daily starting by 2 months, increased to 250 mg PO twice daily
at age 3 years, through at least age 5 years; amoxicillin 20 mg/kg/day may be
substituted for palatability or cost, and erythromycin for penicillin allergy.[2]
Randomized trials summarized in a Cochrane review support reduced
pneumococcal infections with prophylactic penicillin in young children with SCD
and minimal adverse effects.[3]
Other listed options are not appropriate for prevention of early invasive
pneumococcal disease: amoxicillin “as needed” for fever does not provide
prophylaxis; routine palpation of long bones and daily pulse checks are not
recommended preventive measures; low-dose aspirin is not indicated for SCD
prophylaxis in children given insufficient evidence and potential risks.[1][2][3][4]
references: Prophylactic Antibiotics for Preventing Pneumococcal Infection in Children

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 3-week-old boy is brought to the physician by his mother because of a 5-
day history of yellow eyes and skin and
white stools. Pregnancy and delivery were uncomplicated. The patient's
temperature is 37.1°C (98.8°F), pulse is
160/min, respirations are 40/min, and blood pressure is 85/50 mm Hg.
Examination shows diffuse jaundice.
Cardiopulmonary examination shows no abnormalities. The abdomen is
nontender. The liver edge is palpated 4 cm
below the right costal margin. The spleen tip is palpated 3 cm below the
left costal margin. Laboratory studies show:
Leukocyte count: 10,000/mm³
Serum
Bilirubin, total: 16 mg/dL
Direct: 12 mg/dL
AST: 800 U/L
ALT: 500 U/L
Amylase: 100 U/L
Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?
explanation:
Ultrasonography of the abdomen.
A 3-week-old with persistent jaundice, acholic stools, hepatosplenomegaly, and
direct hyperbilirubinemia has high pretest probability of neonatal cholestasis,
especially biliary atresia. Expedited evaluation is essential to enable timely Kasai
portoenterostomy when indicated. The North American Society for Pediatric
Gastroenterology, Hepatology and Nutrition/European Society for Pediatric
Gastroenterology, Hepatology and Nutrition and the American Academy of
Pediatrics endorse urgent workup of cholestatic infants and highlight acholic
stools as a “red flag” warranting targeted evaluation for biliary atresia.[1][2][3]
Abdominal ultrasound is the recommended first-line imaging: it is noninvasive
and can identify findings suggestive of biliary atresia (eg, abnormal or
nonvisualized gallbladder, triangular cord sign) and alternative obstructive
etiologies (eg, choledochal cyst), while also assessing for features of chronic
hepatopathy; normal US does not exclude biliary atresia but guides next steps.[1]
[4][5][6][7] Radiology appropriateness criteria list abdominal US as “usually
appropriate” initial imaging for cholestatic hyperbilirubinemia.[8] HIDA/MRCP/
biopsy are reserved for equivocal cases or subsequent steps; contrast GI studies
and endoscopy are not indicated in this presentation.[1][5][7][8]
Given this infant’s presentation, the most appropriate immediate diagnostic test
among the options is abdominal ultrasound to evaluate for biliary atresia and
other obstructive causes without delaying further management.[1][5][7][8]
references: Clinical Practice Guideline Revision: Management of Hyperbilirubinemia in
BM, et al.. Pediatric Radiology. 2021;51(8):1545-1554. doi:10.1007/
ACR Appropriateness Criteria® Abnormal Liver Function Tests. Arif-Tiwari

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 52-year-old woman comes to the physician for a follow-up examination.
She received the diagnosis of
hypertension 3 weeks ago, and lisinopril therapy was begun at that time.
She has had a constant, nonproductive,
hacking cough for 2 weeks but has not had any other problems. She has no
history of serious illness and takes no
other medications. She does not smoke. She drinks one to two glasses of
wine daily. She does aerobic exercise for
30 minutes four to five times weekly. Her blood pressure has decreased
from 164/92 mm Hg 3 weeks ago to 140/84
mm Hg today. Lungs are clear to auscultation. Which of the following is the
most appropriate next step in
management of this patient's cough?
explanation:
Discontinuation of lisinopril
ACE inhibitors commonly cause a persistent, nonproductive cough, especially in
women; the temporal relationship after initiation and absence of alternative
causes make ACE inhibitor–induced cough the leading diagnosis here.[1][2][3]
The appropriate management is to stop the ACE inhibitor; cough typically
resolves within days to a few weeks after discontinuation, with cohort data
showing resolution in most patients within ≤10 days.[4][5] The American College
of Cardiology/American Heart Association guideline advises substituting an
angiotensin receptor blocker (ARB) when cough develops on an ACE inhibitor, as
ARBs provide comparable BP control with a much lower risk of cough.[6][7][8]
Further diagnostics (pulmonary function tests, chest CT) are unnecessary
at this stage given the clear clinical attribution to the ACE inhibitor. [3][4]
Prednisone or cough suppressants do not address the mechanism and are
not recommended for ACE inhibitor–related cough. [4][9]
Observation without change is inappropriate because the offending agent is
ongoing.[4][6]
Plan: Discontinue lisinopril and initiate an ARB (e.g., losartan, valsartan) with
standard hypertension dosing and titration to BP targets per guideline practice,
monitoring BP, renal function, and potassium.[6][7]
references: Cough and Angioneurotic Edema Associated With Angiotensin-Converting

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 27-year-old primigravid woman at 41 weeks' gestation is admitted to the
hospital for induction of labor. She has
received cervical ripening agents and is now receiving oxytocin. The cervix
is 9 cm dilated and 100% effaced; the
vertex is at 0 station. Fetal monitoring shows contractions every 3 minutes
and a reassuring heart rate. She requests
pain medication. Which of the following is the most likely adverse effect of
an intravenous opioid in this patient?
explanation:
Neonatal respiratory depression.
Intravenous opioids administered in active labor cross the placenta rapidly and
can depress the neonatal respiratory drive, particularly when given close to
delivery. FDA labeling across multiple opioids (e.g., meperidine, hydrocodone,
sufentanil) explicitly warns of neonatal respiratory depression during labor and
recommends immediate availability of naloxone and neonatal monitoring.[1][2][3]
Reviews and consensus statements note that systemic opioids offer limited
mitigation of labor stress but are associated with fetal–neonatal depression;
respiratory depression is the clinically significant neonatal risk that necessitates
preparedness for resuscitation and reversal.[4][5][6][7] Small trials of IV fentanyl
during labor report generally normal Apgar and cord gases with careful dosing
and timing, yet acknowledge the need for neonatal observation and readiness for
naloxone/oxygen if distress occurs.[8] Broader public health guidance
emphasizes monitoring for opioid-related neonatal effects when exposure
occurs near delivery.[9]
Other listed options are less characteristic: maternal fever and tachycardia are
not typical opioid toxicities; neonatal acidosis and arrhythmia are not the
predominant risks compared with respiratory depression in this context.[4][5][7]
references: Dowell D, Ragan KR, Jones CM, Baldwin GT, Chou R.. MMWR.
Parenteral Opioids for Maternal Pain Management in Labour. Smith LA,
The Effects of Maternal Labour Analgesia on the Fetus. Reynolds F.. Best

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 7-year-old girl is brought to the clinic by her father because of behavior
problems at school since she started second grade 6 months ago. Her
teacher is concerned because she cannot sit still or complete tasks and is
not learning at the same pace as her classmates. Psychoeducational testing
shows normal intelligence. The father says his daughter’s behavior
problems have worsened since last year. She snores at night and wets the
bed several nights weekly. She has no history of serious illness and receives
no medications. She has been at the 25th percentile for height and weight
for the past 3 years. Pulse is 105/min and regular, respirations are 22/min,
and blood pressure is 105/65 mm Hg. The tonsils are 3+. Cardiopulmonary
and abdominal examinations show no abnormalities. On neurologic
examination, cranial nerves are intact. Deep tendon reflexes are 2+ in all
extremities. Finger-nose testing shows no abnormalities. Romberg sign is
absent. Her gait is normal. Which of the following is the most appropriate
next step in diagnosis?
explanation:
Polysomnography
Polysomnography is the most appropriate next diagnostic step. This child has
frequent snoring, nocturnal enuresis, behavioral dysregulation/inattention, and
enlarged tonsils—features classic for pediatric sleep-disordered breathing (SDB),
particularly obstructive sleep apnea (OSA). History and exam (including tonsil
size) cannot reliably distinguish primary snoring from OSA or define severity;
overnight polysomnography is the gold standard recommended for diagnosis by
the American Academy of Pediatrics and the American Academy of Sleep
Medicine.[1][2] In children with nocturnal enuresis and suspected SDB,
polysomnography is indicated to evaluate for OSA or periodic limb movements
when clinical suspicion is present.[3] SDB can mimic or exacerbate ADHD-like
symptoms, and neurobehavioral outcomes improve after effective treatment
(e.g., adenotonsillectomy) in many children, supporting confirmation with a sleep
study before pursuing alternative etiologies or neuroimaging.[4][5][6]
Other options are less appropriate:
EEG: no episodes suggestive of seizures.
Karyotype: normal intelligence and no syndromic features.
MRI brain: normal neuro exam and no focal neurologic signs.
Thyroid function tests: growth tracking at the 25th percentile and no
hypothyroid features; symptoms more characteristic of SDB.
The next step is overnight attended polysomnography to quantify the apnea–
hypopnea index and guide management.[1][2][7]
references: Sleep-Disordered Breathing and Cardiovascular Disease in Children and
Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder and Sleep Disordered Breathing in

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
An 8-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department (ED) by
ambulance 45 minutes after he was struck by a car while riding his bicycle.
He did not lose consciousness but has multiple rib fractures with
depression of the left anterior chest wall. His parents have been notified
and are on their way to the hospital. On the patient's arrival to the ED, his
temperature is 37.2°C (99.0°F), pulse is 180/min, respirations are 36/min,
and blood pressure is 65/30 mm Hg. The abdomen is distended with
exquisite tenderness to palpation. A rapid intravenous infusion of 0.9%
saline is begun. CT scans are quickly obtained and show a disrupted spleen
and an abdominal cavity filled with free fluid. The parents arrive at the
hospital and are informed that their son needs an emergency laparotomy
and splenectomy to control the internal bleeding. A saline infusion is
continued to support the patient's blood pressure. The parents provide
consent to proceed with the operation but refuse to consent to transfusion
of blood or blood products, regardless of consequences, because of their
religious beliefs. As the patient is transferred to the operating room, his
pulse is 210/min and blood pressure is 50/20 mm Hg. Hemoglobin
concentration is 3.8 g/dL. The surgeon agrees with the anesthesiologist
that the patient will die without transfusion of blood and plasma. Which of
the following is the most appropriate action?

options.A: Contact the national organization of the parents’ religion and insist that
options.B: Contact the parents’ religious advisor to request help in acquiring
options.C: Proceed with the blood transfusion while
options.D: Proceed with the operation and abide by the parents’ wishes that no
options.E: Transfer the patient by ambulance immediately to a bloodless surgical

explanation:
The ethically and legally appropriate action is to override parental refusal for this
unemancipated minor in extremis and transfuse immediately while contacting
the court. The best option is: (C) Proceed with the blood transfusion while
obtaining an emergency telephone court order.
Rationale:
In life-threatening emergencies for minors, parental authority is not
absolute; when refusal places the child at significant risk of death, clinicians
should provide lifesaving care and seek rapid judicial authorization in
parallel. The Society of Thoracic Surgeons/Society of Cardiovascular
Anesthesiologists and allied societies note that for unemancipated patients
younger than 18 years, transfusion may be administered in emergencies
over parental objections; courts typically intervene to protect the child. The
American College of Emergency Physicians and the AAP emphasize that
necessary emergency care should not be delayed for lack of consent or due
to parental refusal when a child is at risk of significant harm. This approach
is consistent with court precedent limiting parental religious decisions that
endanger children.[1][2][3][4]
Clinically, this child is in profound hemorrhagic shock with hemoglobin 3.8
g/dL and impending cardiovascular collapse. Damage-control resuscitation
with immediate blood products (balanced components or whole blood) is
standard and delay increases mortality. Options A, B, D, and E introduce
harmful delays or accept a lethal course.[5][6][7]
• 
• 
To underscore the standard-of-care resuscitation imperative, the following table
summarizes core principles of damage-control resuscitation, including
immediate use of blood products and avoidance of delays.
Caption: Principles of Damage-Control Resuscitation.
Content:
0
Avoid or correct hypothermia
Apply direct pressure or a tourniquet proximal to sites of hemorrhage in the
extremities; pack junctional wounds with hemostatic dressings
Delay fluid administration until the time of definitive hemostasis in selected
patients (those with penetrating trauma to the torso and short prehospital
transport times)
Minimize crystalloid infusions (<3 liters in the first 6 hr)
Use a massive-transfusion protocol to ensure that sufficient blood products are
rapidly available
"Avoid delays in definitive surgical, endoscopic, or angiographic hemostasis"
"Minimize imbalances in plasma, platelet, and red-cell transfusions in order to
optimize hemostasis"
"Obtain functional laboratory measures of coagulation (e.g., by means of
thromboelastography or rotational thromboelastometry) to guide the transition
from empirical transfusions to targeted therapy"
Selectively administer pharmacologic adjuncts to reverse any anticoagulant
medications and to address persistent coagulopathy
<|END VISUAL|>
Key actions now:
Activate massive transfusion protocol and administer blood products
without delay.[5][6][7]
• 
Proceed to operative hemorrhage control.
Simultaneously notify hospital legal counsel to obtain an emergency court
order/guardian authorization.[1][2][3][4]
references: 8 g/dL. The surgeon agrees with the anesthesiologist

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A female newborn has the onset of tachypnea 4 hours after birth. She was
delivered at term to a 24-year-old woman
following an uncomplicated pregnancy and delivery. Apgar scores were 9
and 9 at 1 and 5 minutes, respectively.
Her parents had refused prenatal screening. The patient's temperature is
37.2°C (99.0°F), pulse is 150/min,
respirations are 60/min, and blood pressure is 55/35 mm Hg. On cardiac
examination, a single, loud S₂ is heard. The
liver edge is palpated 3 cm below the right costal margin. Capillary refill
time is 6 seconds. Peripheral pulses are
difficult to palpate in all extremities. Arterial blood gas analysis on room air
shows:
pH: 7.24
PCO₂: 35 mm Hg (N=27–40)
PO₂: 68 mm Hg
HCO₃⁻: 15 mEq/L
Chest x-ray shows increased pulmonary haziness and vascular markings
suggestive of bilateral pulmonary edema.
The most appropriate next step in management is administration of which
of the following?
explanation:
Intravenous alprostadil (prostaglandin E₁)
Presentation (early shock with tachypnea at 4 hours of life, single loud S2, weak
pulses, hepatomegaly, prolonged capillary refill, metabolic acidosis, and CXR with
pulmonary edema) is most consistent with ductal-dependent congenital heart
disease with critical systemic or pulmonary blood flow obstruction rather than
primary pulmonary disease or sepsis. In this setting, immediate stabilization
requires reopening/maintaining ductal patency to restore systemic or
pulmonary circulation.[1][2]
The American Heart Association (AHA) recommends initiating IV PGE₁ for
neonates with suspected ductal-dependent systemic or pulmonary blood flow;
apnea and hypotension are dose-related adverse effects and warrant close
monitoring and preparedness for airway support if needed.[1] FDA labeling
supports starting 0.05–0.1 micrograms/kg/min and titrating to clinical
response; lower doses may mitigate apnea if the ductus appears at least partially
patent.[1][3]
Why the other options are inappropriate:
Bolus isotonic saline: in cardiogenic shock with pulmonary edema, large
boluses risk worsening respiratory failure; fluids should be used cautiously
only if clear hypovolemia exists.[4]
Sodium bicarbonate: not indicated for this degree of metabolic acidosis
without correcting the cause; potential harms include intracellular acidosis
and impaired myocardial function.[5][6]
• 
• 
Digoxin: not useful acutely and contraindicated in several obstructive
lesions; does not address ductal-dependent physiology.
Dopamine: may support blood pressure but does not correct the underlying
ductal dependence; initiate after PGE₁ if persistent hypotension.
Practical PGE₁ notes: Start 0.05–0.1 μg/kg/min IV infusion; titrate to effect
(typical effective range 0.01–0.1 μg/kg/min; max 0.4 μg/kg/min per labeling).
Monitor for apnea, hypotension, and fever; be prepared for airway support while
avoiding unnecessary elective intubation if possible.[1][3]
references: 05–0.1 μg/kg/min IV infusion; titrate to effect
Complexities, Benefits, Risks, and Clinical Implications of Sodium

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 43-year-old woman comes to the office for a follow-up examination 1
week after she was discharged from the hospital following an overnight
admission for an episode of crushing, aching chest pain. The pain was
located behind her sternum and radiated to her left jaw and shoulder. She
also had shortness of breath, palpitations, and nausea. Initial ECG obtained
on admission showed ST-segment depressions in leads V₃ through V₆.
Nitroglycerin was administered, and the chest pain resolved. Follow-up
serial ECGs disclosed no abnormalities and serum troponin concentrations
were within the reference ranges. Cardiac catheterization obtained the
next morning showed no substantial narrowing of the coronary arteries. At
discharge, the patient declined medication to prevent recurrence of pain.
The patient reports that since discharge, she has had two episodes of chest
discomfort lasting 2 to 4 minutes, similar in location and quality to the pain
precipitating her hospitalization. Medical history otherwise is
unremarkable and she takes no routine medications. Vital signs are within
normal limits. Physical examination discloses no abnormalities. Which of
the following is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy to prescribe at this
time?

options.E: No pharmacotherapy is indicated at this time

explanation:
Diltiazem is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy. The presentation—
recurrent, nitrate-responsive angina with transient ischemic ECG changes and
no obstructive CAD—fits vasospastic (Prinzmetal) angina. Calcium channel
blockers are first-line therapy to suppress epicardial coronary spasm and
prevent recurrent symptoms/events, per the American Heart Association/
American College of Cardiology (AHA/ACC) and contemporary expert
consensus.[1][2][3][4]
Why not propranolol? Nonselective beta-blockers can exacerbate coronary
spasm and are generally contraindicated in vasospastic angina.[3][4]
Why not lisinopril or warfarin? ACE inhibitors and anticoagulation are not
primary therapies for vasospastic angina symptom control or prevention in
the absence of another indication.[1][3][4]
Role of nitrates: Short-acting nitrates are appropriate for acute relief; long-
acting nitrates may be added if symptoms persist despite CCBs,
acknowledging tolerance concerns.[2][3]
Practical regimen: A non–dihydropyridine CCB such as diltiazem (e.g., 120–240
mg/day in divided doses or extended-release formulations, titrated to symptom
control and blood pressure/heart rate) is reasonable initial therapy; alternatives
include verapamil or a dihydropyridine (e.g., amlodipine) if bradycardia limits
dosing.[2][3][4] The AHA/ACC also supports risk factor modification (especially
smoking cessation) and consideration of adjunctive therapies (e.g., statins if
endothelial dysfunction/atherosclerosis is suspected).[1][3]
• 
• 
•

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 35-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department by police
after they found her wandering the streets and appearing intoxicated. On
arrival, she is tremulous and appears distressed. She says she moved out of
her abusive boyfriend’s apartment 1 week ago because she was afraid he
would kill her. She has no financial or emotional family support. She
worked at a grocery store until 1 month ago when she was fired for missing
four shifts during the course of 1 week. During the past week, she has slept
less than 4 hours nightly because she has slept on the streets or in shelters.
She has panic disorder treated with fluoxetine. She takes no other
medications. Ten years ago, she underwent 6 months of psychotherapy for
dependent personality disorder. During the past year, she has drunk four
or more glasses of wine and other alcoholic beverages nightly. She tried to
quit drinking alcohol twice during the past year but was unsuccessful. Her
last alcoholic drink was 24 hours ago. Pulse is 90/min and blood pressure is
140/90 mm Hg. Physical examination shows resting hand tremors and
delayed reflexes. Mental status examination shows slurred speech. She has
an irritable mood and anxious affect. Blood alcohol concentration is 140
mg/dL. Which of the following is the strongest predisposing risk factor for
suicide in this patient?
explanation:
Alcohol use disorder
AUD confers the largest and most direct increase in suicide risk among the listed
options. Large cohort and meta-analytic data consistently show several-fold
elevations in suicidal ideation, attempts, and deaths with AUD, independent of
other psychiatric comorbidity and familial confounding.[1][2][3][4] Substance use
disorders as a class are among the highest-risk psychiatric diagnoses for suicide,
often exceeding anxiety and most personality disorders in risk magnitude.[5][6]
This patient also demonstrates acute/proximal risk: positive BAC with recent
heavy use. Acute alcohol use markedly escalates short-term suicide risk via
disinhibition and impulsivity with dose-response effects, compounding the
chronic risk from AUD and current psychosocial stressors.[1][7][8][9]
Although panic disorder, dependent personality disorder, and a history of
physical abuse are associated with elevated suicide risk, their effect sizes are
generally smaller and less consistently potent than those observed for AUD in
comparative epidemiology.[5][9][10][11]
Clinical implication: prioritize intensive suicide risk assessment and alcohol
withdrawal management, with linkage to evidence-based AUD treatment, as both
chronic AUD and acute alcohol exposure heighten imminent risk.[1][3][4][7]

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 76-year-old woman, who is receiving home hospice care because of end-
stage metastatic lung cancer diagnosed
1 month ago, is examined at home at the request of her family because of a
3-hour history of labored breathing
despite receiving 100% oxygen at 5 L/min via face mask. Pulse is 92/min,
respirations are 18/min, and blood
pressure is 105/62 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on 100% oxygen via face mask
shows an oxygen saturation of 85%. The
patient appears cachectic and is in mild distress, showing increased work
of breathing with prominent
sternocleidomastoid muscles and intermittent agonal respirations. Which
of the following is the most appropriate
next step in management?

options.B: Admit the patient to the hospital for further evaluation
options.D: Transition the patient to noninvasive positive pressure ventilation

explanation:
The most appropriate next step is to treat refractory dyspnea with a systemic
opioid. Multiple US guidelines and reviews support immediate opioid therapy for
air hunger in advanced cancer despite oxygen, with intravenous titration
preferred for acute distress. The American Society of Clinical Oncology, the
American College of Physicians, the American Thoracic Society, and the NCCN all
endorse opioids—commonly morphine—as first-line for cancer-related dyspnea
at end of life; noninvasive ventilation and hospital transfer are generally
misaligned with hospice goals unless explicitly desired, and palliative sedation
(e.g., propofol) is reserved for rare cases refractory to opioids/benzodiazepines
under close monitoring.[1][2][3][4][5][6][7][8]
Recommended approach:
Administer IV morphine boluses, titrating to comfort. Practical regimen per
NEJM includes small IV boluses q5–10 min until relief, then consider a
continuous infusion if needed. ASCO endorses a time-limited opioid trial,
with route guided by setting and swallowing ability.[1][2]
Consider adjunct low-dose benzodiazepine for anxiety if present,
recognizing limited direct effect on dyspnea.[2][5]
Continue nonpharmacologic measures; oxygen has limited benefit beyond
hypoxemia but may be continued for comfort.[2][3][5]
Correct answer: Administer intravenous morphine.
To illustrate the end-of-life dyspnea management framework that places IV
opioids as first-line, see the NEJM summary table of symptom management:
Context for the visual: This table from NEJM delineates evidence-based symptom
control at the end of life and specifically highlights opioids (e.g., IV morphine
boluses, then infusion) as the treatment of choice for acute/severe dyspnea in
dying patients, aligned with hospice goals and supported by oncology and
palliative care guidelines.
• 
• 
• 
Table 5. Management of Symptoms Other Than Pain at the End of Life.
Used under license from The New England Journal of Medicine.
references: Management of Symptoms Other Than Pain at the End of Life.

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 42-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 1-month history
of moderate constant abdominal pain.
She was evaluated in the emergency department 6 weeks ago for renal
calculi and was told that her serum calcium
concentration was increased. She has labile hypertension treated with
hydrochlorothiazide and enalapril. Two
brothers have hyperparathyroidism, and one sister has an adrenal tumor.
The patient's temperature is 37.0°C
(98.6°F), pulse is 80/min, respirations are 12/min, and blood pressure is
182/98 mm Hg. Funduscopic examination
shows arteriovenous nicking. There is mild, diffuse tenderness to palpation
of the abdomen. The remainder of the
examination shows no abnormalities. Serum calcium concentration is 13.2
mg/dL. Urine metanephrine
concentration is 4000 μg/24 h (N=140–785). This patient is most likely at
risk for which of the following
conditions?
explanation:
This presentation is most consistent with MEN2A: symptomatic hypercalcemia
consistent with primary hyperparathyroidism, markedly elevated urine
metanephrines indicating pheochromocytoma, and a family history of
hyperparathyroidism and an adrenal tumor. MEN2A is defined by the triad of
medullary thyroid carcinoma (MTC), pheochromocytoma, and primary
hyperparathyroidism; MTC is highly penetrant and often the defining
malignancy. Therefore, the condition she is most at risk for is Medullary thyroid
carcinoma.[1][2][3]
MEN2 (RET mutations) features: MTC in nearly all affected individuals;
pheochromocytoma in ~50% and primary hyperparathyroidism in 10–20%
of MEN2A cases. GeneReviews (University of Washington) emphasizes early
identification and surveillance for MTC, pheochromocytoma, and
hyperparathyroidism in at-risk relatives, with RET testing and annual
biochemical screening (The University of Washington, Seattle).[1][2]
Surgical management of MTC requires ruling out pheochromocytoma
preoperatively to avoid catecholamine crisis.[3]
Why alternatives are unlikely:
Carcinoid syndrome: does not explain severe hypercalcemia or markedly
elevated metanephrines.
Hashimoto thyroiditis: unrelated to catecholamine excess or
hypercalcemia.
Familial hypocalciuric hypercalcemia: typically benign, lacks
pheochromocytoma/MTC associations; urinary calcium indices (not
provided) would be needed and would not explain elevated metanephrines.
[4][5]
Parathyroid carcinoma: rare; not characteristically associated with adrenal
tumors or high metanephrines; MEN2A parathyroid disease is usually
hyperplasia/adenomas rather than carcinoma.[1][2][6][7]
• 
• 
• 
• 
• 
•
references: 6°F), pulse is 80/min, respirations are 12/min, and blood pressure is
MEN2-related Pheochromocytoma: Current State of Knowledge, Specific
Differentiating Familial Hypocalciuric Hypercalcemia From Primary
Familial isolated hyperparathyroidism. National Library of Medicine

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 26-year-old woman with common variable immunodeficiency comes to
the office because of a 2-month history of
night sweats. She also has had a 10-kg (22-lb) weight loss during the past 3
months despite no change in appetite.
She has a 15-year history of recurrent sinusitis and otitis media. Her only
medication is intravenous immune
globulin. She is 163 cm (5 ft 4 in) tall and weighs 54 kg (120 lb); BMI is 21 kg/
m². Vital signs are within normal
limits. Examination shows cervical and epitrochlear lymphadenopathy.
Which of the following is the most
appropriate next step in management?
explanation:
A. Lymph node biopsy
CVID confers a markedly increased risk of lymphoproliferative disease and
lymphoma. The presence of B symptoms (night sweats, 10-kg weight loss) with
palpable cervical and epitrochlear nodes is highly concerning for lymphoma and
warrants tissue diagnosis. Biopsy distinguishes malignant lymphoma from benign
lymphoproliferation or granulomatous disease, both of which can occur in CVID,
and guides subsequent management.[1][2][3][4][5]
Empiric therapies should be avoided prior to establishing a diagnosis:
Corticosteroids may obscure histopathology and are reserved for defined
inflammatory complications (for example, GLILD), not unexplained
lymphadenopathy with B symptoms.[5][6]
Antibiotics (penicillin, TMP-SMX) are not indicated without evidence of
bacterial infection; TMP-SMX is used for specific infections or prophylaxis
in defined high-risk settings, not for unexplained adenopathy.[5][7]
Plasmapheresis has no role in the evaluation of lymphadenopathy in CVID.
Given the malignancy risk in CVID and red-flag features, excisional lymph node
biopsy is the most appropriate next step.[1][2][3][4][5]
references: Unexplained Lymphadenopathy: Evaluation and Differential Diagnosis.
The Efficacy and Safety of Systemic Corticosteroids as First Line

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 9-month-old boy is brought to the office by his mother because of a 6-
hour history of persistent fussiness and
decreased appetite. Two weeks ago, he was diagnosed with an upper
respiratory tract infection. The symptoms have
since resolved. He lives with his parents and a pet lizard. His diet consists
of cow milk-based formula, baby food,
and some solid foods. He has no history of serious illness and receives no
medications. Immunizations are up-to-date. Today, he is inconsolable. He
is at the 50th percentile for length and weight. Examination shows multiple
flaccid bullae on an erythematous base over the buttocks. The mother says
she had no knowledge of these findings.
Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.D: Rectal swab for group B streptococcus

explanation:
Skeletal survey
Key reasoning:
The infant is afebrile but inconsolable, with multiple flaccid bullae on an
erythematous base over the buttocks that the caregiver did not notice.
Unexplained skin lesions in a nonverbal infant, particularly in the diaper/
buttock region, warrant evaluation for non-accidental trauma (NAT). A
skeletal survey is the appropriate next step to assess for occult fractures
and other injuries. History/physical guide testing in crying infants; occult
serious disease can be missed without targeted evaluation.[1]
Infectious alternatives are less consistent:
Reptile exposure raises risk for nontyphoidal Salmonella, but there are no
GI symptoms; stool culture is not the priority in this presentation.[2]
Bullous impetigo (S. aureus) produces flaccid bullae, often intertriginous;
however, the management would be topical/oral antistaphylococcal
therapy per Infectious Diseases Society of America, and does not explain
the inconsolability alone; moreover, diagnosis is clinical and does not call
for the listed tests.[3][4]
Nitroblue tetrazolium testing evaluates chronic granulomatous disease and
is not indicated here.[5][6]
Anti–Saccharomyces cerevisiae antibodies are for inflammatory bowel
disease; no indication here.
Rectal GBS swab and abdominal ultrasound are not aligned with the
presentation.
Additional notes:
If impetigo were the leading diagnosis, IDSA recommends topical
mupirocin/retapamulin for limited disease or oral antistaphylococcal
therapy for numerous lesions; cultures can be obtained but are not
• 
• 
• 
• 
• 
• 
• 
• 
mandatory in typical cases. However, the priority in this scenario is ruling
out NAT with skeletal survey.[1][3][4]
references: Practice Guidelines for the Diagnosis and Management of Skin and Soft
Diagnostic Testing for Chronic Granulomatous Disease. Kuhns DB..

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 62-year-old woman is admitted to the hospital because of an 8-hour
history of severe right upper abdominal pain, nausea, and fever that began
after she ate fried fish and potatoes. During the past 3 years, she has had 12
episodes of similar pain after meals, but it has not lasted as long or been as
severe as this episode. She has hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and alcohol
use disorder. Her medications are amlodipine, benazepril, and simvastatin.
She drinks one 12-oz beer daily. She is 163 cm (5 ft 4 in) tall and weighs 86
kg (190 lb); BMI is 33 kg/m². Temperature is 38.5°C (101.3°F), pulse is 120/
min, respirations are 28/min, and blood pressure is 100/68 mm Hg. Pulse
oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 98%. On abdominal
examination, there is diffuse tenderness to palpation, rebound, and
guarding. Bowel sounds are decreased. Laboratory studies show:
Hematocrit: 35%
Leukocyte count: 16,000/mm³
Serum
Ca²⁺: 7.9 mg/dL
Urea nitrogen: 26 mg/dL
Glucose: 200 mg/dL
Creatinine: 2 mg/dL
Triglycerides: 180 mg/dL
AST: 162 U/L
ALT: 98 U/L
Amylase: 682 U/L
Lipase: 515 U/L (N=14–280)
Serum electrolyte concentrations and lactate dehydrogenase activities are
within the reference ranges. Which of the Following is the most
appropriate next step in diagnosis?

options.E: Ultrasonography of the right upper abdominal quadrant

explanation:
(E) Ultrasonography of the right upper abdominal quadrant
This patient meets revised Atlanta criteria for acute pancreatitis and has features
suggesting gallstone etiology (postprandial RUQ pain history, elevated
aminotransferases). In suspected acute pancreatitis with RUQ pain,
transabdominal RUQ ultrasound is the recommended initial imaging to detect
cholelithiasis, biliary sludge, and ductal dilation, thereby establishing a biliary
cause and guiding further management.[1][2][3][4][5][6][7][8] The American
College of Radiology Appropriateness Criteria endorse ultrasound as first-line
for RUQ pain and for initial imaging in suspected pancreatitis within 48–72 hours
when the diagnosis is clinically secure.[2][4]
CT or MRI/MRCP are reserved for cases with diagnostic uncertainty, severe
disease beyond 48–72 hours, suspected complications, or inconclusive
ultrasound.[2][9][10] MRCP or EUS can be used to evaluate occult
choledocholithiasis when ultrasound and liver chemistries do not demonstrate
obstruction.[9] ERCP is not an initial diagnostic test and should be reserved for
cholangitis or persistent biliary obstruction/progressive cholestasis; the
American College of Gastroenterology recommends urgent ERCP only in those
scenarios.[11][12]
Early RUQ ultrasound in this presentation best balances accuracy, speed, and
impact on subsequent management (e.g., need for cholecystectomy and selective
CBD evaluation).[1][2][4][5][6][7][8][13]
references: ACR Appropriateness Criteria Right Upper Quadrant Pain. Yarmish GM,
Multimodality Imaging, Including Dual-Energy CT, in the Evaluation of
Management of Gallstone Pancreatitis: A Review. McDermott J, Kao LS,
Urgent and Emergency Endoscopic Retrograde Cholangiopancreatography
Early Prediction of Acute Biliary Pancreatitis Using Clinical and Abdominal

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 63-year-old man with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease comes to
the urgent treatment center because of a 4-
day history of worsening shortness of breath. Use of his albuterol rescue
inhaler has provided no improvement in his
symptoms. Medical history otherwise is unremarkable. Additional
medications are formoterol, budesonide, and
tiotropium. The patient smoked two packs of cigarettes daily for 45 years
but currently smokes only three to four
cigarettes daily and says he is trying hard to quit. He appears anxious. He is
175 cm (5 ft 9 in) tall and weighs 100
kg (220 lb); BMI is 33 kg/m². Temperature is 37.2°C (99.0°F), pulse is 96/
min, respirations are 26/min, and blood
pressure is 128/76 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air discloses an oxygen
saturation of 85%. Mild cyanosis is
noted, but there is no clubbing or edema of the extremities. Auscultation of
the lungs discloses diffuse expiratory
wheezes. Cardiac examination discloses distant but regular heart sounds.
Results of arterial blood gas analysis on
room air are shown:
PO2: 48 mm Hg
PCO2: 55 mm Hg
pH: 7.36
After an albuterol nebulizer treatment is administered, the patient reports
feeling somewhat better but says he is
feeling "jittery" and is starting to have a mild tremor. In addition to
providing smoking cessation counseling and
prescribing antibiotic and corticosteroid therapy, which of the following is
the most appropriate next step in
management?

options.B: CT scan of the chest without contrast

explanation:
Titrated oxygen therapy.
This patient has an acute COPD exacerbation with severe hypoxemia (SpO2 85%
on room air; PaO2 48 mm Hg) and chronic hypercapnia (PaCO2 55 mm Hg) with
near-normal pH, consistent with acute on chronic respiratory failure. The
appropriate immediate step is to initiate supplemental oxygen and titrate to a
target SpO2 of 88–92% (goal PaO2 ≈60–65 mm Hg) to correct life-threatening
hypoxemia while minimizing oxygen-induced hypercapnia.[1][2][3][4] Continuous
monitoring with pulse oximetry and reassessment of ABGs after initiation is
appropriate. If ventilation worsens (eg, rising PaCO2 with pH <7.35) despite
optimal medical therapy, consider noninvasive ventilation.[2]
Why other options are not appropriate now:
Continuous nebulized albuterol: intermittent short-acting bronchodilators
are standard; continuous therapy adds side effects (tremor, tachycardia),
• 
which he is already experiencing, without proven superiority in COPD
exacerbations.[1][5]
Chest CT without contrast: not indicated in a typical exacerbation without
red flags (eg, suspected PE, mass, pneumothorax).
Intravenous aminophylline: randomized data show no meaningful clinical
benefit and increased adverse effects when added to standard therapy; not
recommended.[6]
Pulmonary function testing: not performed during an acute exacerbation;
defer to stability.
Pulmonary rehabilitation: beneficial after hospitalization for an
exacerbation, but not an acute ED intervention; the American Thoracic
Society recommends PR after discharge to reduce readmissions and
improve outcomes.[7]
Long-term oxygen improves survival in chronic severe resting hypoxemia;
acutely, targeted O2 improves outcomes and should be started promptly with
the 88–92% target.[1][2][3][4]

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 49-year-old man returns to the office for follow-up 2 weeks after he was
evaluated for a 4-month history of
intermittent diarrhea associated with cramping abdominal pain, urgency,
and fecal incontinence. Since the onset of
these symptoms, he has had four to five bowel movements daily that
contain blood and mucus. Endoscopy done 1
week ago showed erythematous, inflamed mucosa of the rectum and
sigmoid colon; there were no abnormalities of
the ileum. He also had an episode of uveitis treated with intraocular
corticosteroids 3 months ago. He takes no
routine medications. He is 185 cm (6 ft 1 in) tall and weighs 77 kg (170 lb);
BMI is 22 kg/m². Vital signs are
temperature 37.3°C (99.2°F), pulse 88/min, respirations 18/min, and blood
pressure 128/80 mm Hg. Skin
examination shows raised, tender nodules that are 2 to 3 cm in diameter on
the anterior surfaces of both lower
extremities. Palpation of the abdomen discloses mild tenderness. Rectal
examination discloses no fissures or ulcers.
Test of the stool for occult blood is trace positive. This patient should be
counseled that he is at greatest risk for
which of the following?
explanation:
This presentation—chronic bloody diarrhea with urgency, continuous
rectosigmoid inflammation on endoscopy, and extraintestinal manifestations
(uveitis, erythema nodosum)—is most consistent with ulcerative colitis limited to
the rectum/sigmoid (proctosigmoiditis).[1][2][3][4] Among the options, the key
counseling point is risk of colorectal neoplasia with colonic UC; risk rises with
disease duration, extent beyond the rectum, and cumulative inflammatory
burden, and surveillance colonoscopy is recommended for colonic disease
extending beyond the rectum starting at 8 years, earlier if primary sclerosing
cholangitis is present (per the American College of Gastroenterology and
American Gastroenterological Association quality guidance).[5][6] In contrast,
cholelithiasis and nephrolithiasis are more characteristic of Crohn disease,
particularly with ileal disease or resection; primary biliary cholangitis and
spontaneous bacterial peritonitis are not typical UC risks.[7][8][9]
Therefore, the best answer is: Bold the specific choice.
Surveillance counseling: initiate and maintain guideline-based dysplasia
surveillance for colonic UC, tailored by extent, duration, and inflammation
severity.[5][6]
To illustrate risk determinants for colorectal cancer in IBD, the following table
summarizes established risk factors, highlighting why counseling on colorectal
adenocarcinoma is appropriate in UC with colonic involvement.[5]
Answer: (A) Adenocarcinoma of the colon
references: ACG Clinical Guideline: Ulcerative Colitis in Adults. Rubin DT,
ACG Clinical Guideline Update: Ulcerative Colitis in Adults. Rubin DT,
Ulcerative Colitis: Epidemiology, Diagnosis, and Management. Feuerstein

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 27-year-old man is examined in the intensive care unit 1 day after he
sustained a closed head injury, liver
laceration, and pelvic fracture in a motor vehicle collision. He is intubated
and mechanically ventilated. He is
receiving intravenous midazolam and 5% dextrose in lactated Ringer
solution. Temperature is 37.8°C (100.0°F),
pulse is 95/min, and blood pressure is 100/70 mm Hg. Cardiopulmonary
examination shows no abnormalities. The
abdomen is soft. He withdraws appropriately to painful stimuli. Laboratory
studies show:
Hematocrit: 28%
Leukocyte count: 13,000/mm³
Serum
Na⁺: 138 mEq/L
K⁺: 3.5 mEq/L
Cl⁻: 102 mEq/L
HCO₃⁻: 24 mEq/L
Urea nitrogen: 20 mg/dL
Glucose: 220 mg/dL
Creatinine: 1 mg/dL
Which of the following is the most likely mechanism of these laboratory
findings?

options.A: Decreased uptake of glucose by insulin-sensitive tissues
options.B: Exogenous production of glucose by bacteria
options.C: Impaired excretion of glucose by the kidneys

explanation:
Decreased uptake of glucose by insulin-sensitive tissues
This patient’s moderate hyperglycemia one day after major trauma while
receiving a dextrose-containing infusion is most consistent with stress
hyperglycemia driven by acute insulin resistance. Counterregulatory hormones
(catecholamines, cortisol) and inflammatory cytokines in critical illness increase
hepatic glucose output and induce peripheral insulin resistance, reducing
insulin-mediated glucose uptake in skeletal muscle and adipose tissue; hepatic
insulin resistance further augments gluconeogenesis and glycogenolysis.[1][2][3]
[4][5][6] Traumatic brain injury also independently associates with stress
hyperglycemia via similar pathways.[7]
Although 5% dextrose infusion contributes exogenous glucose, the predominant
mechanism in this setting is stress-induced impairment of peripheral glucose
uptake with increased endogenous production, rather than excessive infusion
alone.[1][2][4][6] Normal creatinine argues against impaired renal glucose
excretion as a cause; there is no evidence of absolute insulin deficiency (no DKA,
normal bicarbonate).[2][3][5]
Key mechanisms:
Surge in catecholamines/cortisol and cytokines → decreased insulin
signaling in muscle/adipose; increased hepatic glucose production.[1][2][3]
[4][5][6]
• 
Adipocyte lipolysis and mediators (e.g., resistin) contribute to acute insulin
resistance after injury.[8]
Clinical implication: manage with moderate glycemic targets in ICU (avoiding
overly aggressive normalization due to hypoglycemia risk), and review dextrose
load; but the underlying driver is the stress-response–mediated decrease in
insulin-sensitive tissue glucose uptake.[1][2][4][7]
references: Stress Hyperglycaemia Following Trauma - A Survival Benefit or an

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 72-year-old woman is evaluated in the acute rehabilitation facility where
she has been receiving physical therapy
since sustaining a stroke 2 weeks ago that resulted in moderate left
hemiparesis. A swallowing study obtained on
admission to the facility showed no abnormalities and she has been
progressing well with her rehabilitation. She is
able to walk with the aid of a walker and assistant, has no speech
impairment, and tolerates a regular diet. Medical
history also is notable for well-controlled hypertension and hyperlipidemia.
Medications are lisinopril, simvastatin,
and daily aspirin. Temperature is 37.0°C (98.6°F), pulse is 72/min and
regular, respirations are 12/min, and blood
pressure is 130/80 mm Hg. Cardiopulmonary examination shows no
abnormalities. Cranial nerves are intact. Muscle
strength in the extremities is 4/5 on the left and 5/5 on the right.
Sensation is intact bilaterally. Standardized
assessment of cognitive function shows no abnormalities. For the past 2
days, the patient has insisted that she is well
enough to go home despite not being fully cleared to do so by her physical
therapist and physiatrist, who want her to
stay for 1 more week to try to advance from the walker to a cane. The
patient lives with her daughter, son-in-law,
and grandson, and has a bedroom on the main floor of the house with no
need to use stairs. The daughter works as a
stay-at-home mother. Which of the following is the most appropriate next
step in management?

options.A: Discharge the patient with a plan for home health care and physical
options.B: Inform the patient that she would need to sign out against medical
options.C: Inform the patient that transfer to a long-term care facility is most
options.D: Request psychiatric evaluation of the patient's decision-making

explanation:
Discharge the patient with a plan for home health care and physical therapy.
Rationale: She is medically stable, has intact cognition and decision-making
capacity, is ambulating with a walker, tolerates a regular diet, and has robust
caregiver support and a home setup without stairs. The American Heart
Association/American Stroke Association (AHA/ASA) emphasizes individualized
discharge planning that integrates residual deficits, functional mobility, cognitive
status, home environment, and caregiver capacity; for patients with adequate
support and ability to participate in therapy, discharge home with ongoing
rehabilitation is appropriate.[1][2][3][4][5] Patients living with family often can be
safely discharged home at lower ADL thresholds than those living alone,
supporting home discharge with services in this scenario.[6] Home-based PT can
continue progression from walker to cane while reducing institutional stay,
provided safety supports are in place.[2][7]
There is no indication for psychiatric capacity evaluation or an AMA discharge:
capacity assessments are decision-specific, and in the absence of cognitive
impairment or concerning features (eg, refusal of clearly beneficial life-saving
care without understanding), treating clinicians can establish capacity without
psychiatry consultation.[8][9][10] Long-term care placement is reserved for
patients with greater dependence or insufficient support, which is not the case
here.[2][5]
references: From Hospital to Home to Participation: A Position Paper on Transition
American Geriatrics Society Position Statement: Making Medical
Evaluating Medical Decision-Making Capacity in Practice. Barstow C,

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 46-year-old woman comes to the office for an annual health maintenance
examination. She reports a 6-week
history of fatigue, which she attributes to working full time and caring for
her three children. Her blood pressure has
been consistently high as measured at home with her husband's automated
cuff, with many readings greater than
150/90 mm Hg. Medical history is unremarkable and routine preventive
screenings, including mammography and
Pap smear, have shown no abnormalities. Her only medications are a daily
multivitamin and ibuprofen as needed.
She is 165 cm (5 ft 5 in) tall and weighs 72 kg (158 lb); BMI is 26 kg/m². Pulse
is 84/min, and blood pressure is
152/98 mm Hg. Physical examination shows trace edema of the ankles but
no other abnormalities. Results of serum
studies are shown:
Urea nitrogen: 12 mg/dL
Creatinine: 0.9 mg/dL
Na⁺: 141 mEq/L
K⁺: 3.1 mEq/L
Cl⁻: 100 mEq/L
HCO₃⁻: 23 mEq/L
Which of the following is the most likely explanation for this patient's
increased blood pressure?
explanation:
Hyperaldosteronism
Hypertension with spontaneous hypokalemia (K+ 3.1 mEq/L) most strongly
indicates primary aldosteronism. Excess aldosterone causes renal sodium
retention, potassium wasting, and hypertension; ankle edema may be mild. The
American College of Cardiology/American Heart Association guideline
recommends screening hypertensive patients with hypokalemia using the
plasma aldosterone–renin ratio under standardized conditions, with
confirmatory testing (oral sodium loading or IV saline infusion) if positive,
followed by subtype evaluation (adrenal CT ± adrenal vein sampling).[1]
Other options are less consistent with the presentation:
Cushing syndrome: uncommon; typically accompanied by characteristic
features (central adiposity, violaceous striae, proximal myopathy, glucose
intolerance) and is an infrequent cause among patients with resistant
hypertension.[2][3]
Fibromuscular dysplasia: renovascular hypertension classically in younger
to middle-aged women with abdominal bruit or abrupt/worsening
hypertension; does not explain hypokalemia unless there is concomitant
RAAS blockade/diuretics.[1][4]
• 
• 
Hypothyroidism: can raise diastolic BP but is not associated with
hypokalemia; clinical features and thyroid dysfunction are absent here.[5]
[6]
Obstructive sleep apnea: prevalent in resistant hypertension and obesity,
but not associated with hypokalemia; presentation lacks typical OSA
symptoms/signs.[1][7][8][9]
Given new-onset hypertension plus spontaneous hypokalemia and no offending
medications, primary aldosteronism is the most likely etiology and should be
specifically evaluated per guideline-directed screening.[1]

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 50-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department because of
a 3-day history of severely painful lesions
over her right leg and temperatures to 38.7°C (101.7°F). She has a 12-year
history of type 2 diabetes mellitus. Her
only medication is insulin. On arrival, temperature is 39.3°C (102.8°F).
Examination of the right lower extremity
shows exquisite tenderness to palpation, edema, and warmth. The
extremity appears shiny and diffusely
erythematous without sharp margins. The subcutaneous tissue is firm, and
there are areas of notable skin breakdown
with bullae containing thick purple fluid. Examination of the left lower
extremity shows no abnormalities. Which of
the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
The most likely diagnosis is Necrotizing fasciitis.
The presentation favors necrotizing fasciitis over cellulitis or erysipelas: rapidly
progressive unilateral lower-extremity involvement in a patient with diabetes;
severe, out-of-proportion pain; high fever; indistinct erythema with shiny tense
skin; “wooden-hard” induration of subcutaneous tissue; and hemorrhagic/purple
bullae with areas of breakdown—classic distinguishing features from non-
necrotizing SSTIs.[1][2][3] The IDSA (Infectious Diseases Society of America)
emphasizes the firm, wooden induration and systemic toxicity as differentiating
signs from cellulitis/erysipelas.[2] Dermatophytosis, erythema nodosum, and
herpes zoster have distinct morphologies (annular scaly plaques; tender
subcutaneous nodules; dermatomal vesicles, respectively) that do not match.[3]
[4][5]
According to a review published in The New England Journal of Medicine:
"Classic manifestations of necrotizing fasciitis include soft-tissue
edema (in 75% of cases), erythema (72%), severe pain (72%),
tenderness (68%), fever (60%), and skin bullae or necrosis (38%). In a
recent case–control study, factors that differentiated necrotizing
fasciitis from cellulitis were recent surgery, pain out of proportion to
clinical signs, hypotension, skin necrosis, and hemorrhagic bullae."
—Dennis L. Stevens, Ph.D., M.D. et al., Veterans Affairs Medical Center
and other institutions. Necrotizing Soft-Tissue Infections. N Engl J
Med. December 07, 2017.
Used under license from The New England Journal of Medicine.
These data align with the patient’s hemorrhagic/purple bullae, severe pain, fever,
and firm subcutaneous tissues, all supporting necrotizing fasciitis over cellulitis.
Clinical implications: immediate broad-spectrum antimicrobials and urgent
surgical exploration/debridement are standard; delay increases mortality.[1][2]
[6] While antibiotic dosing is beyond the scope of this identification question,
IDSA-endorsed initial regimens typically include agents active against
streptococci, staphylococci (including MRSA), gram-negatives, and anaerobes,
often with clindamycin for toxin suppression, coupled with emergent operative
management.[2]
To visualize key distinguishing features of necrotizing fasciitis versus cellulitis/
erysipelas, the following figure highlights clinical presentations that mimic and
those typical of cellulitis, underscoring when to suspect necrotizing infection
requiring operative management.
