question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 3
A 67-year-old man, who has been recovering in the hospital following
surgical repair of a contained ruptured abdominal
aortic aneurysm 10 days ago, suddenly develops severe abdominal pain. He
does not have chest pain or shortness of breath.
The patient's postoperative course had been uncomplicated until 4 days
ago, when he developed a low-grade fever. He has
been unable to tolerate a full liquid diet during the past 4 days. Medical
history is remarkable for hypertension,
hypercholesterolemia, and a myocardial infarction 6 years ago. Regular
medications include metoprolol, lovastatin, and an
81-mg aspirin daily. He has smoked one pack of cigarettes daily for the past
40 years, and he typically drinks three to five
beers daily. Vital signs now are temperature 38.1°C (100.6°F), pulse 68/min
and regular, respirations 16/min, and blood
pressure 150/90 mm Hg. Physical examination shows bilateral cataracts.
Auscultation of the chest discloses diffuse mild
wheezes and a grade 1/6 soft systolic ejection murmur at the cardiac apex.
Palpation of the abdomen discloses mild to
moderate mid and right upper quadrant abdominal tenderness. There is no
guarding, rebound, or palpable mass. Rectal
examination shows no abnormalities. Peripheral pulses are normal.
Neurologic examination discloses no abnormalities.
Serum electrolyte concentrations are within the reference ranges.
Leukocyte count is 15,800/mm³ with mild left shift.
Urinalysis shows no abnormalities. ECG shows an old inferior myocardial
infarction. Chest x-ray shows scarring at the left
and right cardiac bases; no masses or infiltrates are present. Which of the
following is the most appropriate additional
diagnostic study?

options.D: This postoperative patient 10 days after open AAA repair presents with new

explanation:
Ultrasonography of the abdomen (D)
This postoperative patient 10 days after open AAA repair presents with new
severe abdominal pain, persistent low-grade fever, leukocytosis, intolerance of
oral intake, and focal RUQ tenderness without peritonitis. The differential in this
context includes postoperative acalculous cholecystitis, ischemic colitis, graft
infection/mycotic aneurysm, and aortoenteric fistula.[1][2][3][4]
Given the RUQ localization and systemic inflammation, acalculous cholecystitis
is a common postoperative complication after major vascular surgery and is best
evaluated initially with RUQ ultrasonography due to its rapid availability and
good diagnostic performance for gallbladder pathology; when equivocal, HIDA is
the confirmatory test.[5][6][7] While contrast-enhanced CT angiography is the
first-line modality for undifferentiated acute abdomen and for suspected
vascular or graft complications, the clinical picture here most strongly suggests
biliary pathology, making ultrasound the most appropriate next study.[1][2]
Arteriography is not an initial diagnostic test in this setting.[8] MRI is slower and
not first-line for acute RUQ pain.[5][6] Blood cultures may be obtained if sepsis is
suspected but will not localize the source and are not a substitute for imaging.
If ultrasound is nondiagnostic or suggests alternative pathology, escalate to
contrast-enhanced CT angiography of the abdomen/pelvis to evaluate for
ischemic colitis or graft-related complications.[1][2][4]
references: Vascular Graft Infections, Mycotic Aneurysms, and Endovascular

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 3
A 31-year-old woman, gravida 3, para 2, who is at 32 weeks' gestation, is
admitted to the hospital because of a 1-week history
of progressive dyspnea and wheezing. She says her heart is "racing" and she
is coughing up a small amount of blood-streaked
sputum. Medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she
takes levothyroxine. An intravenous catheter is
placed. Vital signs are temperature 36.9°C (98.4°F), pulse 132/min,
respirations 32/min, and blood pressure 135/78 mm Hg.
Pulse oximetry on 100% oxygen via nasal cannula shows an oxygen
saturation of 92%. Auscultation of the lungs discloses
decreased breath sounds at the bases with expiratory crackles bilaterally.
Cardiac examination discloses an irregularly
irregular rhythm, an indistinct point of maximal impulse, and a loud S1. A
grade 3/6, low-pitched, diastolic, rumbling
murmur is audible at the apex; a distinct snapping sound precedes the
murmur. Fetal heart rate is 144/min. Which of the
following is the most likely cause of the findings in this patient?

options.A: Atrial septal defect with development of pulmonary hypertension
options.B: Chronic mitral regurgitation secondary to rheumatic heart disease
options.E: Mitral stenosis complicated by atrial fibrillation

explanation:
Mitral stenosis complicated by atrial fibrillation
The presentation is classic for severe rheumatic mitral stenosis decompensating
in late pregnancy: loud S1, diastolic low-pitched rumble at the apex with an
opening snap, irregularly irregular rhythm (atrial fibrillation), pulmonary edema
(crackles, hypoxemia despite oxygen), and hemoptysis. Pregnancy-induced
increases in blood volume and heart rate exacerbate transmitral gradients and
left atrial pressure, precipitating pulmonary venous hypertension, atrial
arrhythmias, and heart failure in previously compensated MS.[1][2][3][4] The
American Heart Association emphasizes left-sided stenotic lesions—especially
rheumatic MS—as among the highest-risk valvular pathologies in pregnancy,
with AF and pulmonary edema as typical complications.[1][4] Echocardiography-
based pathophysiology in rheumatic MS explains dyspnea and hemoptysis via
elevated left atrial and pulmonary venous pressures, worsened by tachycardia/
AF that shortens diastolic filling time.[2]
Alternatives are less likely:
ASD with pulmonary hypertension typically causes right-sided chamber
enlargement/murmurs rather than a classic apical diastolic rumble with
opening snap; pregnancy is generally tolerated without severe hypoxemia
unless advanced pulmonary vascular disease is present.[5][6][7][8]
Chronic MR produces a holosystolic apical murmur and is relatively better
tolerated in pregnancy compared with stenosis.[1][3]
Coarctation and congenital aortic stenosis do not explain the apical
diastolic rumble with opening snap or hemoptysis/pulmonary edema
pattern.
This scenario aligns most closely with severe rheumatic MS with AF in
pregnancy, a high-risk state associated with heart failure and maternal
morbidity.[1][3][4]
• 
• 
•
references: Recommendations for the Use of Echocardiography in the Evaluation of

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 3
A 70-year-old man is admitted to the hospital through the emergency
department because of a 12-hour history of severe
nausea and four episodes of vomiting of undigested food. During the past
week, he has had worsening nausea, heartburn
following meals, and early satiety. Medical history is significant for type 2
diabetes mellitus diagnosed 20 years ago,
hyperlipidemia, and hypertension. Medications are intermediate-acting
insulin twice daily and insulin lispro before meals. He
also takes enalapril and atorvastatin. On admission, the patient appears
uncomfortable. Vital signs are temperature 36.2°C
(97.1°F), pulse 102/min, respirations 16/min, and blood pressure 105/78 mm
Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an
oxygen saturation of 98%. Physical examination discloses mild epigastric
tenderness to palpation. The remainder of the
examination discloses no abnormalities. Results of laboratory studies
ordered in the emergency department are shown:
Serum Blood ALT 23 U/L
AST 26 U/L
Alkaline phosphatase 85 U/L
Amylase 104 U/L
Urea nitrogen 42 mg/dL
Creatinine 1.32 mg/dL
Hemoglobin A1c 8.2%
X-rays of the chest and abdomen disclose no abnormalities. Which of the
following will best explain the patient's condition?

options.A: Describes choledocholithiasis/pigment or mixed gallstones; this does
options.C: Decreased gastric pH is not a primary mechanism of gastroparesis and
options.D: Increased cholecystokinin inhibits gastric emptying by reducing antral

explanation:
The best explanation is Decreased gastric myoelectrical activity (B).
This presentation is classic for diabetic gastroparesis: subacute worsening of
early satiety, postprandial fullness/heartburn, and vomiting of undigested food
in a patient with long-standing diabetes and no mechanical obstruction on
imaging. Gastroparesis is defined as delayed gastric emptying without
obstruction and is strongly associated with these symptoms and poor glycemic
control.[1][2][3][4][5] The American College of Gastroenterology and the
American Diabetes Association emphasize diagnosis with gastric emptying
studies after excluding obstruction.[1][6]
Pathophysiology in diabetes centers on gastric neuromuscular dysfunction with
impaired myoelectrical activity and antral hypomotility due to autonomic (vagal)
neuropathy, injury/dysfunction of interstitial cells of Cajal, and smooth muscle
abnormalities, often exacerbated by hyperglycemia; these mechanisms slow
gastric emptying and produce the symptom constellation described.[2][3][4][7]
Why other options are incorrect:
(A) Describes choledocholithiasis/pigment or mixed gallstones; this does
not explain vomiting of undigested food and early satiety with normal
radiographs.[5]
(C) Decreased gastric pH is not a primary mechanism of gastroparesis and
would not account for delayed emptying.[3][4]
(D) Increased cholecystokinin inhibits gastric emptying by reducing antral
motility, but the patient’s syndrome is best attributed to diabetes-related
• 
• 
• 
neuromuscular dysfunction rather than isolated hormonal excess;
moreover, CCK’s effect is inhibitory rather than causative of the underlying
neuropathic disorder.[8][9][10]

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 3
A 70-year-old woman comes to the office for an annual health maintenance
examination. She describes a 1-year history of
slowly progressive fatigue, diffuse muscle aches, and generalized pain.
Medical history is significant for osteoporosis
diagnosed 5 years ago. Medications include alendronate and calcium
supplementation. Vital signs are normal. Physical
examination is unremarkable for her age, with no point tenderness on
palpation. Results of complete blood count, serum
electrolyte and thyroid-stimulating hormone concentrations, liver function
tests, and erythrocyte sedimentation rate are all
within the reference ranges. Screening mammography and colonoscopy
done 1 year ago disclosed no abnormalities. Which
of the following is the most appropriate next step in evaluation?

options.E: . Vitamin D deficiency is prevalent in older adults, can present with

explanation:
The most appropriate next step is to obtain a serum 25-hydroxyvitamin D assay
(E). Vitamin D deficiency is prevalent in older adults, can present with
nonspecific fatigue and diffuse myalgias, and is particularly relevant in a patient
with osteoporosis on bisphosphonate therapy; guidelines from the American
Association of Clinical Endocrinology recommend measuring 25(OH)D as part of
the laboratory evaluation in women with osteoporosis, and major reviews
emphasize checking 25(OH)D in those with bone disease and musculoskeletal
symptoms.[1][2][3][4][5] Low 25(OH)D may contribute to osteomalacic pain and
suboptimal response to alendronate; repletion is indicated if deficient.[3][5]
Other options are lower yield here: repeat DXA is not necessary for symptom
evaluation; EMG/NCS and cervical MRI are not indicated without focal
neurologic deficits; ANA is low yield with normal exam and ESR; “no further
evaluation” risks missing a common, treatable cause.[6][7][8][9]
If deficient, typical repletion regimens (per Endocrine-focused guidance) use
cholecalciferol to achieve ≥20–30 ng/mL, with many osteoporosis guidelines
targeting ≥30 ng/mL; dosing should be individualized to baseline level and
comorbidities.[4][6]
This diagnostic algorithm figure highlights incorporation of 25(OH)D testing in
the evaluation of postmenopausal osteoporosis and consideration of
osteomalacia among alternative diagnoses.
Figure 1. Diagnostic Algorithm for the Evaluation of Postmenopausal Osteoporosis.

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 3
A 22-year-old man comes to the emergency department because of a 4‐day
history of cough productive of green sputum as well as fever, chills, and
rigors. He also has had a 3‐day history of progressive shortness of breath;
he now has shortness of breath at rest. He was evaluated in an urgent care
center 1 day ago and was prescribed azithromycin, but his symptoms have
worsened. He is using accessory muscles of respiration. He is diaphoretic
and is able to give only one- to two-word answers to questions. Vital signs
are temperature 39.1°C (102.3°F), pulse 138/min, respirations 40/min, and
blood pressure 103/56 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an
oxygen saturation of 80%, and 100% oxygen is administered via
nonrebreather face mask. Skin is mottled and there is cyanosis of the
fingers and toes. Pupils measure 4 mm in diameter and are reactive to light.
Mucous membranes are dry. Auscultation of the lungs discloses bilateral
crackles. The remainder of the physical examination discloses no
abnormalities. Laboratory studies are ordered; results of arterial blood gas
analysis on 100% oxygen via nonrebreather face mask are shown:
PO2: 50 mm Hg
PCO2: 44 mm Hg
pH: 7.34
Oxygen saturation: 85%
Chest x-ray shows bilateral infiltrates with a normal cardiac silhouette and
no effusions.
Which of the following is the most likely underlying cause of this patient's
condition?

options.D: The presentation is consistent with severe acute hypoxemic respiratory failure

explanation:
Ventilation-perfusion mismatch and shunt (D)
The presentation is consistent with severe acute hypoxemic respiratory failure
from pneumonia complicated by ARDS: acute onset, bilateral infiltrates with
normal cardiac silhouette, profound hypoxemia refractory to 100% O2 (PaO2 50
mm Hg; SaO2 85% on NRB), tachypnea with near-normal PaCO2, and no signs of
cardiogenic edema. In ARDS, the predominant mechanisms of hypoxemia are
true intrapulmonary shunt (perfused, nonventilated alveoli from alveolar
flooding/collapse) and extensive low V/Q regions; these are characteristically
poorly responsive to increased FiO2, unlike hypoventilation, obstructive disease,
or isolated diffusion limitation.[1][2][3][4][5][6]
Key points:
Shunt/low V/Q cause large A–a gradients and refractory hypoxemia; near-
normal PaCO2 is common early due to high minute ventilation.[2][3][4]
Breathing 100% O2 often fails to normalize PaO2 in shunt-dominant states
and may worsen atelectasis in acute lung injury, further increasing shunt.
[5]
Hypoventilation would produce hypercapnia with alveolar hypoxemia that
corrects with oxygen; obstructive disease alone would not explain bilateral
infiltrates and such severe refractory hypoxemia; pure diffusion limitation
rarely causes this degree of hypoxemia at rest and typically improves with
high FiO2.[2][3][6][7]
• 
• 
• 
Overall, the gas exchange pattern and imaging most strongly indicate shunt-
dominant V/Q mismatch as seen in ARDS secondary to severe pneumonia.[1][3]
[4][5][6]
references: Gas Exchange in the Respiratory Distress Syndromes. Albert RK, Jobe A..
Gas Exchange in the Lung. Petersson J, Glenny RW.. Seminars in

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 3
A 33-year-old male physician reports for a shift in the emergency
department. A nurse alerts you that he noticed a faint odor
of alcohol near the physician. When approached, the physician appears
tired and more disheveled than usual. There is an
odor of alcohol on his breath. He is a skilled and talented physician with no
known history of substance or alcohol use
disorder. He is married with three children, and his wife is pregnant with
twins. The physician was recently hired and has had
no actions on his license by any state medical board. Which of the
following is the most appropriate next step?

options.A: Self-report is unreliable and does not satisfy reporting obligations;
options.B: Colleague-initiated BAC testing is not the first step; institutions have
options.D: Informal coverage without reporting circumvents required safety
options.E: Sending home without formal notification fails ethical/legal duties and

explanation:
Relieve the physician of duty and alert the hospital's patient safety officer (C).
Suspected on-duty impairment requires immediate removal from clinical duties
and notification through institutional safety channels to protect patients and
initiate appropriate evaluation. Ethical and, in many jurisdictions, legal duties
obligate physicians to report potentially impaired colleagues; reliance on self-
attestation, informal coverage, or sending the colleague home without formal
reporting fails to meet these obligations and exposes patients and the institution
to risk.[1][2][3][4] Immediate reporting also aligns with Joint Commission–driven
safety programs and just culture processes that ensure nonpunitive pathways for
assessment, treatment, and, when appropriate, monitored return to practice.[5]
[6]
Why other options are inappropriate:
(A) Self-report is unreliable and does not satisfy reporting obligations;
patient safety remains at risk.[1][2][4]
(B) Colleague-initiated BAC testing is not the first step; institutions have
defined protocols via occupational health/employee health and patient
safety channels.[3][5]
(D) Informal coverage without reporting circumvents required safety
processes and documentation.[4][5]
(E) Sending home without formal notification fails ethical/legal duties and
delays proper evaluation and support.[2][4][5]
This approach prioritizes patient safety while enabling a nonpunitive evaluation
and access to physician health resources and rehabilitation pathways.[2][3][6]
references: Physician Impairment and Rehabilitation: Reintegration Into Medical
A Call to Restore Your Calling: Self-Care of the Emergency Physician in the
Impaired Healthcare Professional. Baldisseri MR.. Critical Care Medicine.

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 3
A 60-year-old man comes to the office because of a 1-year history of
progressive left groin pain. The pain is worse with
exercise and is relieved with rest. He says the pain is beginning to affect his
ability to carry out his daily activities. He says,
"It even hurts when I'm turning over in bed." He does not recall any trauma
to the area. He also has had generalized mild
stiffness of his joints during the past 3 years. He takes atorvastatin for
hyperlipidemia and lisinopril for hypertension.
Medical history is otherwise unremarkable. He does not smoke cigarettes.
He drinks one alcoholic cocktail daily. He is 178
cm (5 ft 10 in) tall and weighs 68 kg (150 lb); BMI is 22 kg/m². Vital signs are
temperature 37.4°C (99.3°F), pulse 82/min,
respirations 18/min, and blood pressure 120/78 mm Hg. The patient walks
with a slight limp and has difficulty changing
position. Abduction of the left hip elicits pain. Physical examination of the
patient's other joints discloses no abnormalities.
Which of the following is the most appropriate imaging study at this time?
explanation:
Bold answer: Plain x-ray of the left hip (D).
Rationale: The presentation—chronic, progressive anterior groin pain
exacerbated by activity with mechanical features and pain on hip abduction—
suggests intra-articular pathology, most likely osteoarthritis. For chronic hip
pain in adults, multiple guidelines and reviews recommend plain radiography (AP
pelvis and lateral of the symptomatic hip) as the first-line study to assess for
joint space narrowing, osteophytes, subchondral sclerosis/cysts, and to exclude
alternative bony pathology. Cross-sectional imaging (MRI or CT) is reserved for
nondiagnostic radiographs or specific alternate concerns (eg, occult fracture,
osteonecrosis, tumor, infection).[1][2][3][4][5][6][7] Bone scan and skeletal survey
are not appropriate initial tests in this scenario.[2][3][8][9][10]
Visual context: The figure below illustrates radiographic features typical of hip
osteoarthritis that plain x-ray can detect and that are relevant to this patient’s
presentation.[4]
Figure 2. A Plain Radiograph and Illustration Showing Features of Right Hip Osteoarthritis

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 3
A 2-stage screening program for ovarian cancer is being designed. Which
of the following sets of sensitivity and specificity is
most likely preferred for Stage 1 and Stage 2 of the program?

options.A: Stage 1: High Sensitivity, Low Specificity; Stage 2: High Sensitivity, Low
options.C: Stage 1: Low Sensitivity, High Specificity; Stage 2: High Sensitivity, Low
options.D: Stage 1: Low Sensitivity, High Specificity; Stage 2: Low Sensitivity, Low

explanation:
(B)
Rationale:
In two-stage screening, the first-stage test should cast a wide net to
minimize missed cases, prioritizing high sensitivity even at the expense of
• 
specificity. For ovarian cancer, stage shift and mortality benefit depend on
capturing early disease; biomarker-led first steps (for example, CA125 or
multi-marker panels interpreted longitudinally) are designed to be sensitive
screens that trigger second-stage evaluation.[1][2][3][4]
The second-stage test should be highly specific to limit false positives and
unnecessary invasive procedures. In ovarian cancer programs,
confirmatory transvaginal sonography and specialist assessment are
employed to achieve extremely high specificity and acceptable PPV, as
demonstrated by ROCA-based two-step strategies with very high
specificity and low downstream intervention rates. The American College of
Radiology notes that such two-step designs achieve specificity near 99.9%
with few false positives when TVS is reserved for screen-positive
individuals.[1][3][4][5][6]
This configuration—Stage 1 high sensitivity/low specificity followed by Stage 2
low sensitivity/high specificity—best balances early detection with minimization
of harm, consistent with contemporary trial data and methodological principles
for two-stage screening in ovarian cancer.[1][2][3][4][5][6]
references: Normal Risk Ovarian Screening Study: 21-Year Update. Han CY, Lu KH,

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 3
A 35-year-old woman comes to the office for follow-up of chronic pelvic
pain. The patient has had deep pelvic pain since
undergoing total abdominal hysterectomy and radiation therapy for
treatment of cervical cancer 5 years ago. She reports constant,
severe aching pain; she rates the pain at its maximum as a 10 on a 10‐point
scale. The pain worsens during intercourse. All
subsequent cancer follow-up has been negative. Medical history otherwise
is unremarkable. The patient has taken oxycodone for
her pain for 3 years, but she now reports that the oxycodone only partially
relieves her pain. She requests either an increased dose of
oxycodone or a change in her prescription to controlled-release morphine,
which was recently given to her by a friend. She has
smoked one pack of cigarettes daily for the past 15 years. She does not
drink alcoholic beverages. BMI is 18 kg/m². She appears
lethargic. She is oriented to person, place, and time but speaks slowly with
slurred speech. Vital signs are normal. Examination of
the skin shows no abnormalities. Pupils are pinpoint and react sluggishly.
Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.B: Maintain the dose of oxycodone and add naproxen
options.D: is generally not appropriate given confidentiality

explanation:
The most appropriate next step is to order a urine toxicology screening (option
C) to assess for opioid misuse and ensure patient safety before making any
changes to her pain management, as supported by current guidelines and best
practices.
This patient presents with signs consistent with opioid intoxication (lethargy,
slurred speech, miosis) and reports nonmedical use of controlled-release
morphine. The CDC (United States Centers for Disease Control and Prevention)
recommends reassessing benefits/harms at follow-up and evaluating for misuse
when early warning signs such as sedation or slurred speech are present; urine
testing, pill counts, and PDMP checks are reasonable components of this
assessment.[1][2] ASCO emphasizes structured responses to aberrant behaviors,
beginning with reassessment and adherence/toxicology monitoring, rather than
dose escalation or dismissal.[3] Escalating or switching opioids now (options B/
E) is inappropriate and unsafe. Dismissing from care (A) is discouraged;
suspected opioid use disorder warrants more care, not less.[2][4] Reporting to
law enforcement (D) is generally not appropriate given confidentiality
constraints absent specific legal obligations.[5][6]
After urine toxicology and reassessment, management should include: safety
planning (overdose education, consider naloxone), risk stratification and
monitoring (PDMP, pill counts), optimizing nonopioid and nonpharmacologic
therapies, and evaluation for opioid use disorder with consideration of addiction
or pain specialty referral; buprenorphine may be suitable if OUD is diagnosed
and analgesia is needed.[1][3][4][7]
To illustrate a structured approach to aberrant behaviors and next steps, the
following table summarizes differential diagnosis and management strategies.

Taking Behaviorsa<|END VISUAL|>

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 3
A 35-year-old woman comes to the office for follow-up of chronic pelvic
pain. The patient has had deep pelvic pain since
undergoing total abdominal hysterectomy and radiation therapy for
treatment of cervical cancer 5 years ago. She reports constant,
severe aching pain; she rates the pain at its maximum as a 10 on a 10‐point
scale. The pain worsens during intercourse. All
subsequent cancer follow-up has been negative. Medical history otherwise
is unremarkable. The patient has taken oxycodone for
her pain for 3 years, but she now reports that the oxycodone only partially
relieves her pain. She requests either an increased dose of
oxycodone or a change in her prescription to controlled-release morphine,
which was recently given to her by a friend. She has
smoked one pack of cigarettes daily for the past 15 years. She does not
drink alcoholic beverages. BMI is 18 kg/m2. She appears
lethargic. She is oriented to person, place, and time but speaks slowly with
slurred speech. Vital signs are normal. Examination of
the skin shows no abnormalities. Pupils are pinpoint and react sluggishly.
Urine toxicology screening is positive for diazepam, methadone, and
oxycodone. The patient returns to the office 1 week later
to discuss these results. Which of the following is the most appropriate
opening remark?

options.B: Dismissal is discouraged; it increases harm and misses an opportunity to
options.C: Leads with an accusatory, investigative tone rather than concern and
options.D: Threatening police involvement is punitive, stigmatizing, and not

explanation:
(A)
Option (A) is correct because it opens with nonjudgmental concern, frames the
abnormal toxicology as a safety issue, and immediately orients to evidence-
based treatment for a likely opioid use disorder, consistent with best practice.
The CDC Clinical Practice Guideline (United States Centers for Disease Control
and Prevention) emphasizes assessing for opioid use disorder with DSM-5
criteria, avoiding dismissal from care, and offering/arranging medications for
opioid use disorder and harm reduction, not punitive actions.[1][2] NEJM reviews
similarly advise against dismissal and endorse engagement in treatment,
monitoring, and safety planning; a supportive tone improves disclosure and
outcomes.[3][4] Expert consensus on urine drug monitoring recommends
nonjudgmental, nonpunitive communication, eliciting the patient’s perspective,
and collaboratively planning next steps when unexpected results arise.[5] ASCO
guidance for cancer survivors with aberrant drug-related behaviors stresses
reassessment, considering addiction, and involving addiction specialists rather
than immediate termination or legal threats.[6] Motivational interviewing
principles—expressing empathy, supporting autonomy, and eliciting change talk
—align with (A) and improve engagement.[7]
Why others are inappropriate:
(B) Dismissal is discouraged; it increases harm and misses an opportunity to
initiate life-saving care.[1][2][3]
• 
(C) Leads with an accusatory, investigative tone rather than concern and
treatment engagement; details can be explored after establishing safety and
support.[5][7]
(D) Threatening police involvement is punitive, stigmatizing, and not
recommended absent clear evidence of diversion or imminent risk.[1][5][6]
references: Dowell D, Ragan KR, Jones CM, Baldwin GT, Chou R.. MMWR.

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 3
A 24-year-old woman, gravida 1, para 1, comes to the office because she and
her husband have been trying to conceive for
the past year without success. She gave birth to her first child at age 18
years by cesarean delivery following fetal distress.
She then developed postpartum endometritis that resolved with
intravenous antibiotic therapy. She used an intrauterine
device (IUD) for 1 year following her first pregnancy but had the device
removed because of intermenstrual bleeding.
Medical history is also remarkable for chlamydial cervicitis treated with
antibiotic therapy as an outpatient at age 21 years,
and recurrent bacterial vaginosis that had been treated with metronidazole
therapy during her pregnancy and on two other
occasions. Vital signs today are within normal limits. Breast and pelvic
examinations disclose no abnormalities.
Hysterosalpingography shows bilateral hydrosalpinx. Which of the
following factors in this patient's history most increased
her risk for development of her condition?
explanation:
The historical factor that most increased this patient’s risk of bilateral
hydrosalpinx is prior sexually transmitted chlamydial infection. Chlamydia
trachomatis commonly ascends from the cervix to the upper genital tract,
causing PID, salpingitis, and subsequent tubal scarring that predisposes to
hydrosalpinx and tubal factor infertility; risks increase with repeat infection and
are stronger than for BV, postpartum endometritis, cesarean delivery, or IUD use.
[1][2][3][4] BV is associated with incident PID but is a weaker and less specific
driver of tubal damage than chlamydia.[2][5][6] IUD-associated PID risk is largely
limited to the first 20 days post-insertion and long-term tubal factor infertility
risk from IUDs is low.[7][8] Postpartum endometritis primarily involves the
endometrium rather than the tubes; its linkage to hydrosalpinx is less robust
than that of chlamydia.[9][10]
Answer: Bolded below.
The correct choice: Chlamydial cervicitis
The following figure illustrates the mechanism by which chlamydia ascends to
the upper genital tract, leading to PID and tubal damage that underlies
hydrosalpinx and infertility, aligning with why chlamydial cervicitis most strongly
confers risk in this case.[1]
• 
Figure 1. Chlamydia trachomatis and Ascension to the Upper Genital Tract in Women.

explanation:
The presentation is classic intermittent claudication from peripheral artery
disease (PAD). The most appropriate initial test is the resting ankle brachial
index (A). The American College of Cardiology/American Heart Association and
the Society for Vascular Surgery recommend ABI as first-line, noninvasive
confirmation of PAD in symptomatic patients; it is reasonably sensitive and

ECG, echocardiography, and MUGA do not diagnose PAD in this context.[1][2][3]
[4]
In patients with diabetes, noncompressible arteries can yield falsely elevated
ABIs (>1.40); in that scenario, toe-brachial index is recommended.[2][3][4]

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 3
In determining the usefulness of a D-dimer assay in ruling out pulmonary
embolism as a diagnosis in a 27-year-old woman
with pleuritic chest pain and nonproductive cough, a recent study in the
medical literature is reviewed. The study evaluates
the use of D-dimer assay in 1500 patients (705 female and 795 male)
ranging in age from 19 years to 57 years (mean age is
36 years), whose examining physicians had a low clinical suspicion that the
patients had pulmonary embolism. All patients in
the study underwent a D-dimer assay, followed by CT angiography of the
chest to confirm or exclude the diagnosis of
pulmonary embolism. Study results are shown:
D-Dimer Positive and CT Angiography Positive: 88
D-Dimer Negative and CT Angiography Positive: 0
D-Dimer Positive and CT Angiography Negative: 312
D-Dimer Negative and CT Angiography Negative: 1100
Total CT Angiography Positive: 88
Total CT Angiography Negative: 1412
Total D-Dimer Positive: 400
Total D-Dimer Negative: 1100
Which of the following is the most appropriate conclusion about this data
in regard to patients with low clinical suspicion for
pulmonary embolism?

options.A: D-Dimer assay results should be used to determine the need for
options.B: False-negative D-dimer assay results are common in patients with
options.C: False-positive D-dimer assay results are rarely encountered
options.D: A negative D-dimer assay has a high negative predictive value for
options.E: A positive D-Dimer assay has a high positive predictive value for

explanation:
The study shows sensitivity 100% (88/88) and specificity 77.9% (1100/1412).
Negative predictive value (NPV) is 100% (1100/1100), and positive predictive value
(PPV) is 22% (88/400). In a low pretest probability population, this supports
using a negative D-dimer to exclude PE while recognizing frequent false
positives. Therefore, the best conclusion is: A negative D-dimer assay has a high
negative predictive value for excluding the diagnosis of pulmonary embolism.
This aligns with contemporary diagnostic strategies: in low-risk patients, a
normal D-dimer (standard or age-adjusted) safely obviates imaging, whereas a
positive D-dimer warrants imaging because PPV is low and false positives are
common.[1][2][3][4][5]
This approach is reflected in the diagnostic pathways that incorporate
structured clinical probability (e.g., Wells or Geneva), PERC for very-low risk, and
D-dimer strategies (standard, age-adjusted, or YEARS/PEGeD) to safely avoid
imaging when negative.[1][2][3][4][5]
To visualize the modern workflow emphasizing D-dimer’s rule-out role in low-
risk patients, see the following figure.
Figure 1. Overview of Testing for Pulmonary Embolism in Outpatients or Patients in the Emergency
Department.
Used under license from The New England Journal of Medicine.
Key implications for the presented data and current practice:
Do not use D-dimer to confirm PE; PPV is low with many false positives
(312/1412 CT-negative patients were D-dimer–positive).[2][3][5]
• 
In low-risk patients, a normal D-dimer result obviates CT imaging,
consistent with the American College of Physicians best practice advice and
contemporary reviews.[1][2][3]
Age-adjusted and clinical-probability–adjusted D-dimer thresholds further
improve specificity while maintaining high sensitivity in appropriate
settings.[2][4][5]
references: Overview of Testing for Pulmonary Embolism in Outpatients or Patients in the Emergency

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 3
A 45-year-old man is admitted to the hospital because of a 6-hour history
of bilateral vision loss. He is otherwise
asymptomatic. He has no history of serious illness and takes no
medications. Vital signs are within normal limits. Physical
examination findings are consistent with psychogenic blindness. A medical
student on the patient's treatment team suggests
to the attending physician that they administer a placebo 0.9% saline
injection to the patient but tell the patient it is a new
medication that will "confirm a diagnosis of blindness by temporarily
recovering your vision." Which of the following best
describes the appropriateness of the medical student's suggestion?

options.A: Incorrect: A deceptive placebo cannot “prove” non-organic disease and
options.B: Incorrect: Beneficent intent does not excuse deception.[2][3]
options.C: Incomplete: Even if minimally invasive, the core problem is deception,
options.D: It is inappropriate because the physician would not be stating the truth
options.E: Incorrect: Ethics committee approval does not legitimize deceiving a

explanation:
D
Administering an inert saline injection and falsely describing it as a “new
medication” to diagnose psychogenic blindness is ethically inappropriate
because it requires intentional deception, violating honesty, informed consent,
and respect for patient autonomy. Contemporary analyses emphasize that
undisclosed or deceptive placebo use undermines trust and cannot be justified
by potential benefit or practitioner intent; beneficence does not override
autonomy in routine clinical practice.[1][2][3] Diagnostic placebo “induction”
procedures using deception have been specifically criticized as ethically
problematic and of dubious necessity, with recommendations to avoid deception
and to consider alternatives and/or transparent debriefing if ever contemplated.
[4] Emerging consensus favors open-label placebos—when used at all—precisely
because they avoid deception; undisclosed placebo use risks ethical and even
legal concerns around patient rights and medical fraud in some jurisdictions.[1]
[3][5][6]
(A) Incorrect: A deceptive placebo cannot “prove” non-organic disease and
lacks sufficient diagnostic validity to justify deception.[4]
(B) Incorrect: Beneficent intent does not excuse deception.[2][3]
(C) Incomplete: Even if minimally invasive, the core problem is deception,
not needle pain.[2][4]
(E) Incorrect: Ethics committee approval does not legitimize deceiving a
patient in routine clinical care.
In summary, the student’s proposal is inappropriate because it entails lying to
the patient about the nature and purpose of the injection, breaching ethical
duties of truth-telling and informed consent.[1][2][3][4]
references: Deception and the Ethics of Placebo. Stoessl AJ.. International Review of

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 3
A 5-week-old infant is brought to the office by his mother for an initial
well-child visit. He was born at 37 weeks' gestation
via cesarean delivery because of chorioamnionitis. The infant had fetal
distress that was documented by fetal monitoring
during labor. The mother became jaundiced in the immediate postpartum
period and was found to have hepatitis C (HCV)
and HIV infection. She is unaware of her hepatitis B (HBV) status but knows
that her son received the hepatitis B
vaccination on the second day of life. The infant's weight today is 3912 g (8
lb 10 oz); physical examination shows no
abnormalities. The mother wants to know what chance her child has of
having contracted HIV or HCV infection from her.
The risk for vertical transmission of HIV is approximately 20%. The mother
is advised and appropriate management is
planned for the infant regarding HIV. To determine the risk of HCV
transmission, a recently published study of risk factors
for perinatal transmission of HCV in 155 mothers coinfected with HIV and
HCV is reviewed. Results of the study are shown:
Association of Gestational and Infant Factors With Mother-to-Infant HCV
Transmission
155 Mothers with HIV and HCV Infection
Factor: Gestational Age <37 weeks - HCV Transmission 8.4%, RR 1.0, 95% CI
-
Factor: Gestational Age ≥37 weeks - HCV Transmission 8.3%, RR 0.99, 95%
CI 0.32 to 3.06, p Value 0.99
Factor: No Cesarean Delivery - HCV Transmission 6.0%, RR 1.0, 95% CI -
Factor: With Cesarean Delivery - HCV Transmission 13.3%, RR 2.21, 95% CI
0.69 to 7.06, p Value0.24
Factor: No Chorioamnionitis - HCV Transmission 7.0%, RR 1.0, 95% CI -
Factor: With Chorioamnionitis - HCV Transmission 33.3%, RR 4.77, 95% CI
0.86 to 26.3, p Value 0.21
Factor: Did not use Fetal Electrode - HCV Transmission 7.0%, RR 1.0, 95%
CI —
Factor: Used of Fetal Electrode - HCV Transmission 3.3%, RR 0.66, 95% CI
0.09 to 4.89, p Value 0.99
Factor: Infant Not HIV-infected - HCV Transmission 5.4%, RR 1.0 95% CI —
Factor: Infant HIV-infected - HCV Transmission 17.1%, RR 3.19, 95% CI 1.14
to 8.93, p Value 0.04
RR=relative risk; CI=confidence interval
Based on these data, which of the following is the most appropriate
conclusion regarding the five potential risk factors for
transmission of HCV?

options.A: All factors appeared to have an effect on HCV transmission
options.B: Fetal electrode monitoring was protective against HCV
options.C: Infant human immunodeficiency virus infection was the only significantly
options.D: Maternal chorioamnionitis was the most significantly associated factor

explanation:
(C) Infant human immunodeficiency virus infection was the only significantly
associated factor
Maternal HIV coinfection is consistently and significantly associated with
increased HCV mother-to-child transmission (approximately twofold higher
than HCV alone), supported by meta-analyses and contemporary cohort
syntheses.[1][2][3][4][5] The American Association for the Study of Liver Diseases
and the Society for Maternal-Fetal Medicine both highlight HIV coinfection as a
key risk factor for HCV MTCT, with absolute risks rising from roughly 5%–6% to
about 9%–12% in the setting of HIV, particularly with higher HCV viremia.[1][2][3]
[4][5]
Fetal scalp/internal monitoring is not protective; invasive intrapartum
procedures, including fetal scalp electrodes, have been associated with higher
transmission risk and are discouraged.[2][6][7][8] Maternal chorioamnionitis has
not shown a significant association with HCV MTCT in the medical literature.[7]
While prolonged rupture of membranes and other intrapartum factors may
increase risk, these associations are less consistent and context-dependent, and
none among the remaining listed factors demonstrate the robust, consistent
statistical significance seen with HIV coinfection.[2][6][7][8]
Therefore, among the five proposed factors, infant (maternal) HIV infection is the
only one that is consistently and significantly associated with increased HCV
transmission risk.[1][2][3][4][5]
references: Dotters-Katz SK, Kuller JA, Hughes BL.. American Journal of
Recommendations for the Use of Antiretroviral Drugs During Pregnancy

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 3
A 45-year-old man comes to the community health center because of a 1-
week history of fever, chills, sweating, mild
shortness of breath, and nonproductive cough. Medical history is
remarkable for AIDS and pneumonia 3 years ago that
required hospitalization. Today, he says that he lost his job 6 months ago
and cannot afford to buy his antiretroviral
medications. Vital signs are temperature 38.6°C (101.5°F), pulse 82/min,
respirations 20/min, and blood pressure
116/72 mmHg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of
97%. The health center is very busy with several
patients waiting to be evaluated. Which of the following is the most
appropriate next step?

options.A: Arrange a direct admission to the hospital for this patient
options.B: Continue with obtaining a thorough history and examining this patient
options.C: Place masks on the patient and
options.D: Send this patient for chest x-rays, and while they are being obtained

explanation:
The clinical priority is infection control for suspected pulmonary tuberculosis in
an immunocompromised patient with cough, fever, and constitutional symptoms
in a crowded clinic. The most appropriate immediate action is to institute source
control and respiratory protection before proceeding with evaluation and
testing. Therefore, the correct choice is: (C) Place masks on the patient and
yourself and then continue to evaluate him.
Rationale:
People with HIV and respiratory symptoms have a higher pretest
probability of TB and atypical presentations; early TB precautions are
recommended to reduce nosocomial transmission. [1][2]
The Infectious Diseases Society of America and CDC emphasize masking
patients with suspected infectious TB and using appropriate respiratory
protection for healthcare workers in community and healthcare settings;
surgical masks reduce exhaled aerosols, and N95/KN95 for providers offer
better protection. [3]
Evaluation for TB should follow promptly (chest radiograph, sputum AFB
smear/culture and NAAT) in HIV patients with cough and fever, but not
before instituting precautions. [4]
Direct admission is not indicated given current stability and normal oxygen
saturation; sending for imaging before masking risks exposure to others. [5]
[6]
Figure context: The figure underscores that CAP/TB evaluations proceed with
imaging and labs after initial infection control and triage, reinforcing that
precautions precede diagnostics in settings with transmission risk.

•

Laboratory, and Imaging Abnormalities.

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 3
A 45-year-old man comes to the office for a follow-up examination 2 days
after he was seen in the emergency department for
a generalized tonic-clonic seizure. He was prescribed chlordiazepoxide
upon being discharged but did not fill the
prescription. The patient says that this was his third seizure in the past 2
years; prior to that he had no history of seizures.
Medical history is also remarkable for a 10-year history of daily ingestion of
two six-packs of beer. He has not had any
alcoholic beverages for the past 5 days. He takes no medications. The
patient says that his seizures usually occur after he
abstains from alcohol for a period of time. Vital signs are normal. Physical
examination shows scattered spider angiomata on
his chest and abdomen. Liver is enlarged, smooth, and nontender. CT scan
of the head with contrast done in the emergency
department 2 days ago showed no abnormalities. Which of the following is
the most appropriate next step?

options.A: Lumbar puncture for examination of cerebrospinal fluid
options.C: Repeat CT scan of the head in 1 week

explanation:
No further evaluation is necessary
This patient’s recurrent generalized tonic–clonic seizures occur exclusively in
the setting of alcohol cessation, with return to neurologic baseline, normal
contrast head CT, and no fever, meningismus, focal deficits, or persistent altered
mental status—features consistent with acute symptomatic alcohol withdrawal
seizures. In such cases, further neurodiagnostic testing (LP, MRI, EEG) is not
routinely indicated unless alternative etiologies are suspected by history, exam,
or abnormal initial imaging.[1][2] Emergency EEG is reserved for patients not
returning to baseline or with focal deficits; nonemergent EEG/MRI can be
considered if there are atypical features (e.g., focal onset, head trauma,
prolonged confusion), which are absent here.[2]
Management priority is treatment of alcohol use disorder and prevention of
withdrawal; chronic antiseizure medication is not indicated for withdrawal-only
seizures and abstinence reduces recurrence risk.[3] Contemporary observational
data emphasize identifying atypical features predicting epilepsy (focal semiology,
epileptiform EEG, structural lesions, status epilepticus); none are present here.
[4][5]
references: New-Onset Seizure in Adults and Adolescents: A Review. Gavvala JR,
Long-Term Outcome of Alcohol Withdrawal Seizures. Sansone G,

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 3
A case-control study is conducted to assess risk factors predicting
inpatient mortality among geriatric patients with
community-acquired pneumonia. Results of the study include the odds
ratios shown below, which were calculated from a
multivariable logistic regression equation:
Predictor Variables:
Hypotension (systolic blood pressure ≤100 mm Hg) - Odds Ratio: 3.32, 95%
CI (1.65 to 5.11)
Hypoxemia (PO2 ≤ 50 mm Hg) - Odds Ratio: 2.43, 95% CI (1.55 to 3.32)
Lung infiltrate present on chest x-ray at time of admission - Odds Ratio:
1.35, 95% CI (0.76 to 2.24)
When the other covariates are controlled, which of the following is the
most appropriate conclusion regarding these data?

options.A: The risk for inpatient mortality is greater for patients with hypotension
options.B: The risk for inpatient mortality is increased more by hypoxemia than by
options.C: The risk for inpatient mortality is increased when there is a pulmonary
options.D: The risk for inpatient mortality is significantly affected by all of these

explanation:
(A) The risk for inpatient mortality is greater for patients with hypotension
than for those without hypotension
Interpretation:
Adjusted ORs >1 with 95% CIs excluding 1 indicate independent associations
with higher odds of mortality. Hypotension (OR 3.32, 95% CI 1.65–5.11) and
hypoxemia (OR 2.43, 95% CI 1.55–3.32) are both statistically significant;
pulmonary infiltrate (OR 1.35, 95% CI 0.76–2.24) is not, as its CI includes 1,
so there is no clear independent effect in this model.[1][2][3][4]
Comparing magnitudes, hypotension confers a greater increase in adjusted
odds of inpatient mortality than hypoxemia (3.32 vs 2.43), supporting option
A over B.
Option C is not supported (non-significant CI), and D is incorrect because
not all predictors are significant.
Clinical context:
Low systolic blood pressure is a strong predictor of adverse outcomes in
CAP and features prominently in severity tools (e.g., CURB-65), with
multiple cohorts showing hypotension as a high-impact prognostic factor
in hospitalized pneumonia, including older adults.[5][6][7][8][9]

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 3
A 38-year-old man comes to the office because of a 2-month history of
intermittent episodes of hot flushes, chest discomfort,
and excessive sweating, with associated panic. The episodes have been
increasing in frequency and now are occurring daily.
The patient says there is no specific precipitating cause of the episodes,
but he has noticed that the episodes occur most
frequently in the afternoon. He does not have any new stressors in his life.
Medical history is significant for hypertension and
type 2 diabetes mellitus. Medications are lisinopril and metformin. Family
history is significant for hyperparathyroidism in
an aunt and the death of an uncle from medullary thyroid cancer. The
patient's BMI is 33 kg/m². Vital signs are temperature
36.9°C (98.4°F), pulse 74/min, respirations 18/min, and blood pressure
138/92 mm Hg. Physical examination discloses no
abnormalities. Results of serum laboratory studies are shown:
Calcium 9.8 mg/dL
Urea nitrogen 15 mg/dL
Creatinine 1.0 mg/dL
Na+ 140 mEq/L
K+ 3.8 mEq/L
Cl− 102 mEq/L
HCO3 −28 mEq/L
ECG shows no abnormalities.
Which of the following is the most appropriate diagnostic study at this
time?
explanation:
Serum free metanephrine concentration (option C)
The paroxysmal flushing, diaphoresis, chest discomfort, and panic in a
hypertensive patient with a family history suggestive of MEN2 (medullary thyroid
carcinoma, hyperparathyroidism) warrant evaluation for pheochromocytoma.
The American Heart Association notes plasma or 24-hour urinary metanephrines
are effective screening tests when pheochromocytoma/paraganglioma is
suspected, and comprehensive reviews and NCCN guidance support plasma free
metanephrines as the preferred initial test due to high sensitivity in symptomatic
patients.[1][2][3][4] ACE inhibitors such as lisinopril do not meaningfully interfere
with LC-MS/MS plasma metanephrine measurements.[5] Aldosteronism
screening (renin/aldosterone) is less indicated here given the clinical picture and
normal potassium.[1][6][7] PTH testing is unnecessary with normal calcium and
no specific symptoms of hyperparathyroidism.
Practical notes for test accuracy:
Collect plasma metanephrines with the patient fully supine after rest;
review medications that may cause false positives (e.g., tricyclics, SNRIs,
MAOIs).[2][3][4]
Marked elevations (e.g., ≥3× ULN) are highly specific and should prompt
anatomic imaging (CT/MRI).[3][4]
Neumann et al.’s consensus approach illustrates initiating evaluation with
metanephrines in symptomatic patients and MEN-associated scenarios.[2]
• 
• 
This table summarizes the recommended diagnostic approach to suspected
pheochromocytoma, aligning with selecting plasma free metanephrines first in
this patient.
Table 1. Approach to Pheochromocytoma and Associated Syndromes According to the Clinical
Scenario.*

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 3
A 19-year-old woman is referred to the office by her counselor for
evaluation of possible depression. The counselor describes
the patient as polite, conscientious, cooperative, open to therapy, and
always present and on time for her weekly sessions.
The patient's relationship with her high school boyfriend ended 6 months
ago. She had dated him through high school, and he
had "looked out for her as a friend" since the 6th grade. She says, "I feel
helpless and don't know what to do without him."
She admits that she often cannot make decisions, such as whether to go to
college. She says, "I always mess things up." She
is still sad and often tearful about her ex-boyfriend, but she has not had
alterations in her sleep habits, appetite, weight, or
energy level. She has not had any thoughts about death. Medical history is
unremarkable and she takes no medications. BMI
is 24 kg/m². Vital signs are normal and physical examination discloses no
abnormalities. Which of the following is the most
appropriate initial response to this patient's self-deprecating description?

options.A: is overly directive and risks undermining autonomy, especially salient
options.C: promotes dependence and defers the work, potentially reinforcing
options.D: invalidates and minimizes affect, which can damage alliance and reduce

explanation:
(B)
Option (B) is correct because it provides empathic validation and gently
challenges the patient’s global, negative self-appraisal without minimizing her
feelings, being directive, or fostering dependence. This aligns with core
strategies in CBT and IPT for adolescents—identifying and reframing distorted
cognitions while supporting autonomy and agency.[1][2] Therapist responses
that acknowledge emotion and offer partnership improve alliance and
engagement; validating while offering a more balanced reframe is preferred over
reassurance alone.[3][4][5][6]
(A) is overly directive and risks undermining autonomy, especially salient
given dependent traits and decision-making difficulty; directive advice can
reinforce externalization of control.[2][7][8]
(C) promotes dependence and defers the work, potentially reinforcing
reassurance-seeking rather than fostering problem-solving and cognitive
restructuring.[2][4][7][8]
(D) invalidates and minimizes affect, which can damage alliance and reduce
engagement.[5][6]
Evidence-based early responses emphasize validating emotion, collaboratively
examining evidence for negative beliefs, and supporting patient agency—
precisely the stance reflected in (B).[1][2][3][4][5][6]

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 3
A 35-year-old man comes to the office because of 1-week history of mid
low back pain that radiates down his right leg. The
pain began after the patient lifted a heavy box onto his truck. He rates his
current pain as an 8 on a 10-point scale. He has
been unable to find a comfortable position and has been sleeping in a
recliner. Medical history is unremarkable and he takes
no medications. He has smoked one pack of cigarettes daily for the past 25
years, and he drinks a six-pack of beer on Friday
and Saturday nights. BMI is 27 kg/m². He appears uncomfortable and
stands during the physical examination. Vital signs are
normal. Straight-leg raise test is positive on the right, with loss of right
ankle reflex. The remainder of the physical
examination discloses no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most
likely explanation for this patient’s symptoms?
explanation:
The most likely explanation is a lumbar radiculopathy from acute disk herniation
after lifting, with S1 involvement suggested by unilateral sciatica, positive
straight-leg raise, and absent ankle (Achilles) reflex. In this age group and
presentation, the typical pathology is displacement of disk material, most
commonly nucleus pulposus, compressing the traversing nerve root in the lateral
recess/foramen.[1][2][3][4] Degenerative causes such as facet hypertrophy,
osteophytes, ligamentum flavum thickening, or spondylolisthesis are classically
seen in older adults and more often present with chronic stenotic symptoms and
neurogenic claudication rather than abrupt severe radicular pain.[5][6] The
straight-leg raise has high sensitivity for disk herniation, and a focal reflex deficit
increases the post-test probability of root compression.[2][7]
Answer: Displacement of the nucleus pulposus
To visually contrast the acute herniation mechanism with degenerative stenosis
mechanisms (facet/osteophytes/ligamentum flavum hypertrophy/
spondylolisthesis), the following figure shows how the latter typically narrow the
canal/foramen in older patients, in contrast to this patient’s acute herniated
nucleus pulposus presentation.
Citations: NEJM Clinical Practice: Herniated Lumbar Intervertebral Disk; NEJM
Nonspecific Low Back Pain (diagnostic accuracy of straight-leg raise); JAMA
Review on Lumbar Spinal Stenosis (degenerative etiologies); NEJM Review:
Sciatica (S1 loss of ankle reflex); Am J Med Review: Lumbar Disc Herniation;
Cochrane Review: Physical examination performance.[1][2][3][4][5][7]
references: Sciatica. Ropper AH, Zafonte RD.. The New England Journal of Medicine.

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 3
A 71-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department by her
daughter for evaluation of her mental status. The
daughter says, "I visited mom today for the first time in 6 months; her
memory has worsened, her bills are unpaid, and her
house is unusually messy." The patient says, "I'm perfectly healthy." Medical
history is significant for hypertension
diagnosed more than 25 years ago, and two small strokes occurring 3 years
and 7 months ago. The daughter gives you three
empty medicine bottles from her mother's home: metoprolol,
hydrochlorothiazide, and aspirin. The patient is 165 cm
(5 ft 5 in) tall and weighs 59 kg (130 lb); BMI is 22 kg/m². Vital signs are
temperature 36.4°C (97.6°F), pulse 76/min,
respirations 16/min, and blood pressure 196/112 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on
room air shows an oxygen saturation of 93%.
Muscle strength in her left hand is 1/5. Strength in the right extremities is
4/5. The patient is oriented only to self; she does
not know the day of the week or today's date. She recalls 0 of 3 words at 5
minutes, and she is unable to name 2 objects,
correctly draw interlocking pentagons, or count backwards by serial
sevens. Complete blood count, renal function studies,
liver function studies, and urinalysis are all within the reference ranges.
ECG shows no abnormalities. Which of the
following is the most likely underlying cause of the patient's symptoms?
explanation:
The most likely underlying cause is Vascular dementia (E).
Rationale:
Prominent risk factors and history: longstanding hypertension and prior
strokes strongly support vascular cognitive impairment/dementia (VCI/
VaD). The American College of Radiology and the American Stroke
Association emphasize that cerebrovascular disease from atherosclerosis/
arteriolosclerosis underlies VaD and is associated with stepwise or
fluctuation in decline, executive dysfunction, and focal neurologic signs.[1]
[2][3]
Phenotype: impaired executive function (serial 7s), visuoconstruction
(drawing), language/naming, and loss of instrumental ADLs with focal
deficit (left hand weakness) align with VaD more than Alzheimer disease,
which more typically lacks focal deficits early and shows more insidious,
amnestic-dominant onset.[2][4][5]
Alternatives are less consistent: ALS is a motor neuron disorder with
possible FTD spectrum but would not explain prior strokes and focal
weakness in this pattern. Corticobasal degeneration features asymmetric
limb apraxia/alien limb and parkinsonism; focal stroke history and
hypertension favor VaD. Neurosyphilis requires compatible risk profile and
serology/CSF findings and often presents with other specific signs (e.g.,
Argyll Robertson pupils, tabes); none are described.[6][7][8]
Contextual visual: Neurosyphilis can cause dementia (general paresis), but it is
typically accompanied by characteristic exam and CSF/serologic findings,
making it less likely here than stroke-related VaD.
• 
• 
• 
Caption: Stages of Neurosyphilis According to Clinical Features and Associated
Laboratory Test Results.
Description: *The fluorescent treponemal-antibody absorption (FTA-ABS) test is
not specific. CSF denotes cerebrospinal fluid, and VDRL Venereal Disease
Research Laboratory.†The serum VDRL test may not become reactive in the

chorioretinitis or retinitis (ocular syphilis).§Loss of deep sensation indicates the
absence of a response to pressure on visceral structures such as muscles,
tendons, testicles (Pitres’s sign), or eyeballs. Charcot joints are also known as
neuropathic arthropathy.
<|END VISUAL|>
Key literature: The American Stroke Association statement underscores the high
prevalence of vascular cognitive disorders after stroke and the need to
differentiate them from AD, noting common mixed pathology but strong
association with vascular risk factors and focal deficits.[3] Hypertension-driven
small-vessel disease is a major driver of VaD.[1] ACR 2024 highlights stroke/
stepwise decline with focal signs as classic for VaD/VCI.[2]
