question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 3
A 3-week-old male infant is brought to the office by his mother because of
a 2-day history of white lesions in the mouth. The infant was born at term
via uncomplicated spontaneous vaginal delivery; he has been breast-
feeding well and is urinating and having bowel movements normally. At
birth, he weighed 3289 g (7 lb 4 oz; 30th percentile). Today, he weighs 3629
kg (8 lb; 25th percentile). He appears adequately hydrated. Vital signs are
normal. Physical examination discloses white patches on the tongue and
buccal mucosa that do not come off when gently scraped with a tongue
blade. Which of the following is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy?
explanation:
Nystatin
Findings are classic for neonatal oral candidiasis (thrush): adherent white plaques
on the tongue and buccal mucosa in a well 3-week-old. The Infectious Diseases
Society of America recommends topical therapy as first line for uncomplicated
oropharyngeal candidiasis; nystatin oral suspension is effective, safe,
inexpensive, and minimizes systemic exposure in otherwise healthy neonates.[1]
[2] Typical dosing used in practice for infants is nystatin oral suspension 100,000
units/mL, 1 mL applied to the oral mucosa four times daily for 7–14 days,
continuing at least 48 hours after lesion resolution.[1][2]
Fluconazole is an alternative, with randomized data showing higher clinical cure
than nystatin in infants and strong efficacy in broader populations, and may be
preferred for refractory disease, poor adherence to frequent dosing, or more
extensive disease; however, topical nystatin remains an appropriate first-line
choice in an otherwise healthy term neonate.[1][2][3][4]
Acyclovir and valacyclovir treat herpes simplex virus and are not indicated for
candidiasis; HSV oral lesions in neonates are typically vesicular/ulcerative and
often accompanied by systemic signs, warranting systemic acyclovir when
suspected.[5][6][7] No-pharmacologic management alone is inappropriate given
symptomatic thrush in this age group.
references: Common Oral Conditions: A Review. Stoopler ET, Villa A, Bindakhil M, Díaz
Randomised Controlled Trial of Prophylactic Fluconazole Versus Nystatin

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 3
A 55-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by his wife
because he told her he did not want to live anymore. During the past 6
weeks, he has experienced fatigue, loss of interest in usual activities, a 7-kg
(15-lb) weight loss, and insomnia. He has no history of serious medical or
psychiatric illness. Vital signs are temperature 37.0°C (98.6°F), pulse 80/
min, respirations 16/min, and blood pressure 140/82 mm Hg. Physical
examination discloses no abnormalities. Beck Depression Inventory score
is 35 (severely depressed). He says he feels guilty that his investments have
not done well and that he has ruined his family finances. He reports he
hears voices at night telling him he has sinned. In addition to olanzapine,
which of the following is the best treatment option for this patient?
explanation:
Fluoxetine is the best agent to add. This presentation is consistent with unipolar
major depressive episode with psychotic features. For psychotic depression,
antipsychotic plus antidepressant combinations outperform monotherapy; the
olanzapine–fluoxetine combination shows the most robust efficacy signal versus
placebo and versus olanzapine alone, with comparable acceptability in
randomized trials and network meta-analyses.[1][2] Other listed agents
(divalproex, lamotrigine, lithium) are primarily for bipolar disorder or
augmentation in nonpsychotic, treatment-resistant depression and are not first-
line for unipolar psychotic depression; divalproex may also lower oral olanzapine
concentrations, risking subtherapeutic exposure.[3][4][5][6]
Recommended option: start an SSRI—specifically, add fluoxetine to olanzapine.
Typical studied dose ranges for the combination are olanzapine 6–18 mg/day
with fluoxetine 25–50 mg/day, titrated to response and tolerability.[7] Monitor
suicidality, metabolic effects, and consider ECT if rapid response is required or if
inadequate response.
Answer: B. Fluoxetine
To visualize where olanzapine–fluoxetine stands among acute depressive
treatments (noting its regulatory status in bipolar depression and its strong
evidence base when paired with an antipsychotic), see the summary table below.
Caption: Pharmacological Treatments of Bipolar Disorder<|END VISUAL|>
Rationale highlights:
SSRI + second-generation antipsychotic (especially fluoxetine + olanzapine)
shows superior response vs placebo and vs antipsychotic monotherapy in
psychotic depression.[1][2]
Antidepressant or antipsychotic monotherapy is less effective than the
combination.[1][2]
Divalproex, lamotrigine, and lithium are not first-line for unipolar psychotic
depression; valproate can reduce oral olanzapine levels.[3][4][5][6]

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 3
A 78-year-old man, who underwent coronary angiography with stent
placement 8 years ago, comes to the office for a routine health
maintenance examination. The patient reports no new symptoms except
for shortness of breath and fatigue during exercise that began 3 months
ago when he tried to increase the distance of his daily walk from 1 mile to 3
miles. He has not had orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, or chest
pain. Additional medical history is significant for hypertension, coronary
artery disease, dyslipidemia, and polymyalgia rheumatica. The patient also
has a heart murmur that was discovered at an armed forces screening
physical examination at age 18 years, but it did not prevent him from
serving in the military. Medications are metoprolol, atorvastatin, lisinopril,
omeprazole, and a low dose of prednisone every other day. The patient is
active and manages a small farm. BMI is 26 kg/m². Vital signs are
temperature 37.0°C (98.6°F), pulse 60/min, respirations 18/min, and blood
pressure 124/61 mm Hg. Cardiac examination discloses a normal S1,
pronounced S2, and a grade 2/6 late systolic murmur heard at the right
upper parasternal border. Pulses are normal in contour. Results of
laboratory studies are within the reference ranges. ECG shows no
abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in
management?
explanation:
The most appropriate next step is to evaluate the new exertional dyspnea and
longstanding systolic murmur with imaging for structural heart disease. Physical
examination alone cannot reliably characterize etiology or severity of a systolic
murmur; transthoracic echocardiography (TTE) is the recommended initial test
to assess valve anatomy and function, ventricular size and function, diastolic
parameters, and pulmonary pressures in symptomatic patients.[1][2][3] This is
particularly important here given the late systolic murmur at the right upper
sternal border and a loud S2, raising concern for valvular pathology and/or
pulmonary hypertension; TTE is appropriate per American College of
Cardiology/American Heart Association and multisociety imaging criteria.[3][4]
[5] Adjusting metoprolol dose or increasing prednisone is not indicated without
evidence of drug adverse effects or PMR flare, and vitamin D testing is unrelated
to the presenting complaint.[6][7][8]
Therefore, the best next step is: (D) Order a transthoracic echocardiography.
The diagnostic pathway for unexplained dyspnea in older adults includes early
echocardiography to identify HF phenotypes and valvular disease; the figure
below illustrates the role of TTE in evaluating symptoms potentially due to
HFpEF or other structural causes.[3][9][10]
Figure 2. Diagnostic Algorithm for Heart Failure With Preserved Ejection Fraction
Used under license from JAMA Network.
references: Diagnostic Algorithm for Heart Failure With Preserved Ejection Fraction
Polymyalgia Rheumatica. Espígol-Frigolé G, Dejaco C, Mackie SL, et al..
Heart Failure With Preserved Ejection Fraction: A Review. Redfield MM,

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 3
A 28-year-old man comes to the office because he would like to quit
smoking cigarettes. He says, "I cannot go more than a day without
cigarettes because I get so anxious and irritable. I even wake up at night to
smoke." The patient began smoking cigarettes at age 12 years; he was
smoking two packs of cigarettes daily by age 17 years, and he has not
curtailed his use since that time. He does not drink alcoholic beverages or
use illicit drugs. Medical history is remarkable for childhood febrile
seizures. He takes no medications. Vital signs are normal. The patient is
interested in using a nicotine patch and bupropion therapy because he says
his friend found the treatments to be helpful. In combining these two
therapies, this patient is at greatest risk for which of the following?

options.B: , and not (E) as indicated by here by the USMLE.
options.E: The patient is at no increased risk

explanation:
The correct answer is: Hypertension.
Combining bupropion with nicotine replacement therapy (NRT) increases the
risk of treatment-emergent hypertension compared with either agent alone. FDA
labeling based on a comparative trial reports hypertension in 6.1% with
bupropion SR + nicotine patch versus 2.5% with bupropion SR alone, 1.6% with
nicotine patch alone, and 3.1% with placebo; some cases required
discontinuation of therapy.[1][2][3] The American College of Cardiology also
notes that bupropion can raise blood pressure and that the combination with
nicotine patch is more effective but requires attention to blood pressure
monitoring.[4]
Other listed options are not characteristic harms of this combination:
Cold intolerance, polyuria, and renal insufficiency are not recognized
adverse effects of bupropion/NRT in the cessation literature.[5][6][7]
Although bupropion lowers seizure threshold, a remote history of
childhood febrile seizures is not an absolute contraindication; seizure risk
at recommended doses is low (~0.1% at 300 mg/day) and the primary
incremental risk with the combination is hypertension rather than seizures.
[2][4][5][8]
Practical considerations:
Typical bupropion SR dosing for smoking cessation is 150 mg once daily for
3 days, then 150 mg twice daily (maximum 300 mg/day), with periodic
blood pressure assessment; the nicotine patch is commonly started on the
quit date.[2][3][4]

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 3
A 25-year-old woman comes to the office because of a 24-hour history of
right-sided facial weakness. Two days ago she developed mild aching
behind the right ear, which resolved spontaneously. This morning,
however, the entire right side of her face was drooping and she was unable
to close her right eye. When she tried to drink coffee this morning, the
liquid ran out the right side of her mouth. She has been otherwise healthy
and she takes no medications. Vital signs are normal. Examination of the
ears and oral mucosa shows no abnormalities. Cranial nerve examination
shows moderate weakness of the right orbicularis oculi and oris, frontalis,
buccinator, and platysma muscles. Sound is perceived as louder in the right
ear. The remainder of the neurologic examination shows no abnormalities.
Which of the following is the most accurate statement regarding this
patient's prognosis for neurologic recovery?

options.B: Prognosis cannot be determined without results of MRI of the brain and
options.C: She has an 80% chance of complete recovery within the next 12 months
options.D: With oral corticosteroid therapy she has a 20% chance of complete
options.E: are inconsistent with the typical presentation and natural history; (D)

explanation:
(C) She has an 80% chance of complete recovery within the next 12 months
without treatment.
This presentation is classic for Bell’s palsy: acute unilateral LMN facial paresis
involving the forehead with retroauricular pain and ipsilateral hyperacusis,
without other neurologic deficits or vesicular eruption. In typical cases,
diagnostic imaging or CSF analysis is not required to determine prognosis.[1][2]
[3]
Spontaneous prognosis is favorable: approximately 70–80% achieve complete
recovery within 6–12 months without treatment, with higher rates among those
with incomplete weakness at onset, as in this case.[1][3][4][5][6] Early oral
corticosteroids (e.g., prednisone 50–60 mg daily for 5 days with a 5-day taper if
started within 72 hours) improve the likelihood of complete recovery and reduce
synkinesis, but most untreated patients still recover fully; antivirals add little or
no additional benefit for functional recovery in most trials, though they may
reduce late sequelae when combined with steroids.[1][4][6][7][8] Options (A), (B),
and (E) are inconsistent with the typical presentation and natural history; (D)
understates the benefit of steroids and misstates recovery probabilities.[6][7][8]

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 3
A 20-year-old woman comes to the office because of a 2-day history of
nasal congestion, runny nose, and nonproductive cough that has made it
difficult for her to sleep. She has not had fever. Her brother with whom she
resides also has similar symptoms. The patient's medical history is
significant for mild persistent asthma for which she takes inhaled
fluticasone- salmeterol twice daily and albuterol as needed. She has been
using her albuterol more frequently during the past 2 days without much
effect. During this time, her peak expiratory flow rates have been 70% to
80% of her baseline. Vital signs today are temperature 37.2°C (99.0°F), pulse
70/min, respirations 20/min, and blood pressure 110/70 mm Hg. Physical
examination discloses inflamed nasal mucosa with clear discharge. The
posterior oral pharynx is erythematous. Auscultation of the lungs discloses
rare expiratory wheezes. Which of the following is the most appropriate
pharmacotherapy for this patient's cough?
explanation:
The presentation is most consistent with a viral URI triggering mild asthma
symptoms. For cough due to uncomplicated URI, antitussives (e.g., codeine),
first-generation antihistamines (diphenhydramine), and expectorants
(guaifenesin) lack demonstrated benefit and may cause harm, and inhaled
ipratropium is not supported for routine use in this setting.[1][2][3][4] Systemic
corticosteroids are reserved for moderate–severe asthma exacerbations or poor
response after 48 hours; her PEF 70–80% baseline with stable vitals and rare
wheeze does not meet guideline thresholds for oral steroids.[1][5] Therefore,
none of the listed agents is routinely appropriate for her URI-related cough. If
symptoms progress to a moderate/severe exacerbation or fail to respond to
guideline-directed reliever/ICS intensification, the most appropriate
pharmacotherapy among the options would be a short course of oral prednisone
as per Global Initiative for Asthma (GINA, Global Initiative for Asthma).[5]
Answer: Prednisone
To contextualize the threshold for adding oral corticosteroids when control
worsens (step-up/exacerbation management), the following table summarizes
step therapy for adolescents/adults from the National Asthma Education and
Prevention Program (NAEPP) update; while not depicting cough-specific therapy,
it frames when systemic steroids are considered during exacerbations alongside
controller/reliever adjustments.[6]
Table 1. Recommendations for Pharmacologic Step Therapy for Managing Asthma in Adolescents
(Aged 12-17 Years) and Adults (Aged 18 Years or Older)[[3]]a
Used under license from JAMA Network.
Key evidence points:
Avoid routine codeine, diphenhydramine, guaifenesin, or inhaled
anticholinergics for acute bronchitis/common cold cough; consider
differentials like asthma exacerbation before initiating other therapy.[1][2]
• 
Add oral corticosteroids for severe exacerbations (e.g., PEF or FEV1 <60%
personal best or failure to respond after 48 hours); typical adult dosing
prednisolone/prednisone 40–50 mg daily for 5–7 days without taper if <2
weeks.[5]
Ipratropium may have signal in post-infectious cough or as adjunct in
severe exacerbations, but not recommended for routine URI cough
management.[3][4]
references: Recommendations for Pharmacologic Step Therapy for Managing Asthma in Adolescents

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 3
A 34-year-old woman, gravida 2, para 1, comes to the office at 26 weeks'
gestation to establish prenatal care after obtaining health insurance
coverage. She has gained 14 kg (31 lb) during this pregnancy. Her last
pregnancy 4 years ago resulted in delivery of a 4564-g (10-lb 1-oz) male
newborn. Today, the patient is 162 cm (5 ft 4 in) tall and weighs 99 kg (218
lb). Vital signs are normal. Physical examination discloses a fundal height of
30 cm. Fetal heart rate is 146/min. Prenatal laboratory studies and
ultrasonography are ordered. This patient is most at risk for which of the
following?

options.A: This patient’s risk is highest for gestational diabetes given:

explanation:
Gestational diabetes (A)
This patient’s risk is highest for gestational diabetes given:
Obesity: BMI ≈ 37.7 kg/m² (162 cm, 99 kg) substantially elevates GDM risk
independent of other factors.[1][2][3][4][5]
Excessive gestational weight gain by 26 weeks further increases GDM risk.
[1][2][4]
Prior macrosomic infant (4564 g) is a classic high-risk feature for GDM in
the current pregnancy; maternal obesity, excessive GWG, and diabetes are
independent predictors of macrosomia.[6][7]
Fundal height > dates (30 cm at 26 weeks) suggests accelerated fetal
growth; fetal overgrowth at 28 weeks often precedes or accompanies GDM
and is additive with maternal obesity.[8]
Although obesity and excessive GWG also increase preeclampsia risk, the
constellation of prior macrosomia plus current size>dates most strongly points
to GDM as the principal risk in this scenario.[1][2][3][9][10] Multiple gestation and
placenta previa are not suggested by history; polyhydramnios can result from
GDM but is a downstream finding rather than the primary risk.
Screen at 24–28 weeks per standard practice; many experts advocate earlier
testing in high-risk patients, with repeat testing if initial results are normal.[3] If
GDM is diagnosed, initial management is medical nutrition therapy and
moderate exercise; pharmacotherapy is added if glycemic targets are not met.[3]
[5] Low-dose aspirin (at least 75 mg daily from 12 weeks until delivery) may be
considered in obesity to reduce preeclampsia risk.[3]
references: 7 kg/m² (162 cm, 99 kg) substantially elevates GDM risk
Excessive Gestational Weight Gain Is an Independent Risk Factor for
Preeclampsia. Magee LA, Nicolaides KH, von Dadelszen P.. The New
Cluster Analysis to Estimate the Risk of Preeclampsia in the High-Risk

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 3
A 39-year-old woman, gravida 6, para 5, is admitted to the hospital at 39
weeks' gestation for a scheduled induction of labor for delivery of twins.
Her prior pregnancies were uncomplicated. Her current pregnancy has
been complicated by well- controlled gestational diabetes. Vital signs are
temperature 36.7°C (98.1°F), pulse 80/min, respirations 16/min, and blood
pressure 134/84 mm Hg. Uterine size is consistent with a 36-week
gestation. The fetuses are estimated to weigh 1814 g (4 lb) each. The patient
delivers healthy twins. After the delivery of the placenta, examination
discloses decreased uterine tone, increased bleeding, and increased
hypotension. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial
pharmacotherapy?
explanation:
The presentation (uterine atony with brisk bleeding immediately after placental
delivery) calls for first-line uterotonic therapy. The most appropriate initial agent
is Oxytocin. Oxytocin is recommended as first-line for treatment of postpartum
hemorrhage (PPH) due to uterine atony given superior effectiveness and safety
versus misoprostol and fewer contraindications than ergot alkaloids or
prostaglandin F2α analogs.[1][2][3][4] Typical dosing for PPH control is 10–40
units in IV fluids titrated to sustained uterine tone, or 10 units IM after placental
delivery.[2][3] Methylergonovine (ergonovine) is second-line and contraindicated
in hypertension/preeclampsia; carboprost (15-methyl PGF2α) is second-line and
avoided in asthma.[1][5][6][7][8] Misoprostol is less effective and has more
adverse effects than oxytocin and is reserved for settings where oxytocin is
unavailable or as adjunctive therapy.[1][4][8][9]
To contextualize the recommendation and dosing hierarchy, the following table
from a comprehensive review summarizes first- and second-line uterotonics,
dosing, and key contraindications.
Table 1. Medical Therapy for Postpartum Hemorrhage.*
Used under license from The New England Journal of Medicine.
Answer: Oxytocin
references: Pitocin. Food and Drug Administration. Updated date: 2024-08-12
Prostaglandins for Postpartum Hemorrhage: Pharmacology, Application,

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 3
A 32-year-old woman comes to the emergency department because of a 3-
day history of worsening fever, dry cough, and shortness of breath. She
also has had abdominal discomfort, diarrhea, and nausea, but she has not
vomited. She returned home from a business trip to a midwestern city 2
days ago. She has had no known contact with any ill individuals. She had
been generally healthy and medical history is unremarkable. She takes no
medications. Her last menstrual period was 6 weeks ago and she thinks she
might be pregnant. She does not smoke cigarettes, drink alcoholic
beverages, or use illicit drugs. She is married and uses no contraception.
She appears well developed and well nourished but is short of breath and
lethargic. Vital signs are temperature 38.3°C (101.0°F), pulse 104/min,
respirations 28/min, and blood pressure 100/60 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on
room air shows an oxygen saturation of 92%. Auscultation of the chest
discloses crackles at the right lung base, tachycardia, a normal S1 and S2,
and no other abnormalities. Abdominal examination discloses no
abnormalities. Petechiae are present over both lower extremities. Results
of laboratory studies are shown:
Serum
ALT - 80 U/L
AST - 63 U/L
Urea nitrogen - 14 mg/dL
Creatinine - 1.7 mg/dL
Na⁺ - 130 mEq/L
K⁺ - 3.9 mEq/L
Cl⁻ - 104 mEq/L
HCO3⁻ - 16 mEq/L
Iron, total - 18 μg/dL
Iron binding capacity, total - 428 μg/dL (N=250–350)
Iron saturation - 5% (N=20–50)
β-hCG - Positive
Blood
Hematocrit - 30.3%
Hemoglobin - 10.2 g/dL
WBC - 11,300/mm³
MCV - 76 μm³
Platelet count - 104,000/mm³
Red cell distribution width - 16% (N=11.5–14.5)
Which of the following is the most appropriate intravenous
pharmacotherapy at this time?
explanation:
The presentation is consistent with severe community-acquired pneumonia
(CAP) with sepsis and early pregnancy, without clear MRSA/Pseudomonas risk
factors. Guideline-concordant therapy for hospitalized severe CAP is a beta-
lactam plus a macrolide; in pregnancy, this avoids routine fluoroquinolone use
while providing atypical coverage. Both cefuroxime (a parenteral second-
generation cephalosporin) and azithromycin have reassuring pregnancy safety
data and are appropriate empiric choices.[1][2][3][4][5][6][7] Therefore, the most
appropriate intravenous therapy is: Cefuroxime and azithromycin (option B).
Practical notes:
Dosing consistent with the CAP literature: azithromycin 500 mg IV daily
plus a parenteral beta-lactam; while many guidelines list ceftriaxone,
cefuroxime is a reasonable cephalosporin alternative with pregnancy safety
(adjust for renal dysfunction).[2][5][6][7]
Avoid fluoroquinolone monotherapy in early pregnancy when alternatives
exist, despite meta-analyses not showing clear teratogenic signals; they are
not first line for pregnant patients and have other safety concerns.[1][2][8]
[9][10]
No routine antipseudomonal or anti-MRSA coverage absent risk factors;
reassess with microbiology and clinical trajectory. Initiate sepsis bundle
promptly per obstetric sepsis guidance.[2][3][4]
The following figure summarizes the empirical regimen framework for
hospitalized CAP, supporting a beta-lactam plus macrolide approach and
selective use of antipseudomonal/MRSA agents when risk factors are present.

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 3
A 72-year-old man is admitted to the intensive care unit for pain
management and observation of ventricular ectopy 4 hours after
undergoing uncomplicated left total knee replacement. He is receiving
fentanyl via systemic patient-controlled analgesic pump for pain
management. Medical history is remarkable for coronary artery disease, for
which he takes atorvastatin, furosemide, carvedilol, lisinopril, and 81-mg
aspirin. Baseline echocardiogram showed an ejection fraction of 0.20. Vital
signs now are temperature 38.0°C (100.4°F), pulse 90/min, respirations 16/
min, and blood pressure 130/90 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows
an oxygen saturation of 96%. Physical examination discloses no
unexpected abnormalities. Which of the following regimens for prophylaxis
of deep venous thrombosis will likely be most beneficial in this patient?

options.A: Continuous application of bilateral lower extremity pneumatic
options.B: Continuous intravenous infusion of heparin titrated to a PTT of 1.5 to

explanation:
Subcutaneous enoxaparin
Low–molecular-weight heparin (LMWH) is preferred for VTE prophylaxis after
total knee arthroplasty. The American College of Chest Physicians (ACCP)
recommends LMWH over unfractionated heparin (UFH), adjusted-dose warfarin,
aspirin, or intermittent pneumatic compression device (IPCD) alone for TKA,
with dosing initiated ≥12 hours postoperatively (or ≥12 hours preoperatively)
rather than within 4 hours of surgery.[1] Subsequent comparative literature and
multi-society guidance continue to support LMWH as first-line, balancing
superior efficacy against DVT/PE with acceptable bleeding risk relative to UFH,
warfarin, or mechanical prophylaxis alone.[2][3] Network meta-analytic data
indicate UFH and warfarin are less effective than LMWH, while DOACs may
further reduce asymptomatic VTE but are not listed among the options here.[4]
Mechanical prophylaxis alone (IPCD) is an alternative when bleeding risk is
prohibitive but is generally less effective than chemoprophylaxis; combined
IPCD plus anticoagulant can further reduce VTE compared with either
alone, at the cost of more bleeding versus IPCD alone.[1][5][6]
Continuous IV heparin is not recommended for routine prophylaxis post-
arthroplasty.
Warfarin is less effective and slower to achieve therapeutic anticoagulation
postoperatively, with more management burden.[1][2]
UFH SC is acceptable but less effective than LMWH.[1][4]
If dosing is requested: enoxaparin 30 mg SC every 12 hours (common in North
America) or 40 mg SC daily, initiated ≥12 hours postoperatively, continued for at
least 10–14 days (many extend to 35 days based on risk).[1][2]
references: Francis CW, Johanson NA, et al.. Chest. 2012;141(2 Suppl):e278S-e325S.
Pharmacotherapy for Venous Thromboprophylaxis Following Total Hip or

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 3
A 45-year-old man comes to the emergency department because of a 1-
month history of fatigue, generalized muscle weakness, and a 4-kg (8-lb)
weight loss. He also reports a loss of appetite and nausea without vomiting
during the past 2 weeks. Medical history is unremarkable and he takes no
medications. He does not smoke cigarettes or drink alcoholic beverages. He
is 180 cm (5 ft 11 in) tall and weighs 70 kg (155 lb); BMI is 22 kg/m². Vital
signs are temperature 36.4°C (97.5°F), pulse 100/min, respirations 20/min,
and blood pressure 88/50 mm Hg. Physical examination shows bronze
darkening of the elbows, on the creases of his hands, and around the
areolae of his nipples. Muscle strength is 4.5/5 in all extremities. The
remainder of the physical examination shows no abnormalities. Results of
laboratory studies are shown:
Serum
Na⁺ 128 mEq/L
K⁺ 5.2 mEq/L
Cl⁻ 95 mEq/L
HCO3⁻ 20 mEq/L
Blood
Hemoglobin 12 g/dL
WBC 3500/mm³
In addition to administering intravenous fluids, which of the following is
the most appropriate pharmacotherapy?
explanation:
The presentation (weight loss, fatigue, orthostatic hypotension,
hyperpigmentation, hyponatremia, hyperkalemia) is classic for primary adrenal
insufficiency with impending adrenal crisis. Immediate glucocorticoid
replacement is indicated in addition to isotonic saline. The correct choice is 
Hydrocortisone.
Rationale and dosing:
In suspected adrenal crisis, give hydrocortisone 100 mg IV bolus, then 200
mg per 24 hours (continuous infusion or 50 mg IV/IM q6h), with volume
resuscitation; taper to oral doses over 2–3 days as stabilized.
Hydrocortisone provides both glucocorticoid and mineralocorticoid
activity at stress doses, addressing hypotension and electrolyte
abnormalities. For chronic replacement after stabilization, typical
outpatient dosing is hydrocortisone 15–25 mg/day divided; add
fludrocortisone 0.05–0.3 mg/day for mineralocorticoid replacement in
primary disease.[1][2][3][4]
Other options are inappropriate: calcitonin treats hypercalcemia; DHEA is
only considered in select chronic cases (mainly women) with persistent
symptoms; levothyroxine can precipitate adrenal crisis if given before
glucocorticoids in unrecognized adrenal insufficiency; mitotane is for
adrenocortical carcinoma, not adrenal insufficiency.[4][5][6][7]
• 
• 
To underscore acute management, the following table summarizes
recommended hydrocortisone therapy in adrenal crisis.
The table below (from NEJM) shows first-line dosing and fluid strategy for
adrenal crisis, highlighting the 100-mg IV bolus followed by 200 mg/24 h
regimen.
Table 2. Management of Adrenal Crisis.
Used under license from The New England Journal of Medicine.
Strong concordance exists across contemporary reviews and guidelines
(Endocrine Society; European Society of Endocrinology) supporting immediate
stress-dose hydrocortisone in suspected crisis without delaying for testing.[2][3]
[4]
Correct answer: Hydrocortisone.

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 3
A 45-year-old man comes to the office because of severe pain of the right
foot that awoke him from sleep last night. He says the pain kept him awake
for the rest of the night. Walking has been difficult due to the pain, which
he rates as a 9 on a 10- point scale. He reports no recent trauma. Medical
history is remarkable for hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus, and
asthma. Medications include hydrochlorothiazide, metformin, atorvastatin,
and an albuterol inhaler. He is allergic to penicillin. He does not smoke
cigarettes, but he has drunk three to four glasses of red wine daily for the
past 5 years. Family history is significant for arthritis in his father. He is 168
cm (5 ft 6 in) tall and weighs 111 kg (245 lb); BMI is 40 kg/m². Vital signs are
temperature 37.9°C (100.2°F), pulse 100/min, respirations 16/min, and
blood pressure 160/100 mm Hg. Examination of the right foot discloses
erythema and increased warmth over the first toe extending over the
dorsum of the foot. The involved area is very tender and the patient resists
movement of his toes. There is moderate swelling of the first
metatarsophalangeal joint. The remainder of the physical examination
discloses no abnormalities. Results of laboratory studies are shown:
Serum
Urea nitrogen - 15 mg/dL
Creatinine - 1.0 mg/dL
Blood
Hemoglobin - 16 mg/dL
WBC - 12,500/mm³
Examination of joint aspirate shows a leukocyte count of 50,000/mm³; no
organisms are seen on Gram stain. Which of the following is the most
appropriate pharmacotherapy to treat this patient's acute condition?

options.D: The clinical picture is classic podagra with inflammatory synovial fluid and

explanation:
Indomethacin (D)
The clinical picture is classic podagra with inflammatory synovial fluid and
negative Gram stain, favoring an acute gout flare over septic arthritis. First-line
therapy for acute flares includes an NSAID, colchicine, or a glucocorticoid per
the American College of Physicians and the American College of Rheumatology;
indomethacin is a commonly used NSAID for rapid symptom relief in this setting.
[1][2][3][4][5] Typical dosing is 50 mg three times daily until pain is tolerable,
then taper/stop, using the lowest effective dose for the shortest duration.[6][7]
Acetaminophen provides analgesia but is not an effective anti-inflammatory
agent for gout flares. Allopurinol should not be initiated for acute flare
treatment; it is for urate lowering between flares or can be continued if already
on therapy.[2][3] Antibiotics (ceftriaxone, vancomycin) are not indicated with a
negative Gram stain and no clinical concern for septic arthritis; empiric
antibiotics are reserved for suspected infection.[8][9][10]
Given comorbid hypertension, diabetes, thiazide use, and asthma, NSAID risks
(BP elevation, fluid retention, renal effects; bronchospasm in aspirin-sensitive
asthma) warrant short-course use with monitoring; alternatives include low-
dose colchicine or glucocorticoids if NSAIDs are contraindicated.[1][2][3][6][7]
references: Approach to Septic Arthritis. Horowitz DL, Katzap E, Horowitz S, Barilla-
Management of Gout: A 57-Year-Old Man With a History of Podagra,

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 3
A 2-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department by his babysitter
because of a 30-minute history of respiratory distress. The babysitter
reports that she has cared for the patient on numerous occasions during
the past year. The child's parents are currently out of town for business but
will return later tonight. The babysitter says, "He has had a runny nose
since I started babysitting yesterday, but this afternoon he awoke from a
nap with a barking sound and he was breathing real heavy." She does not
know the child's prior health history, nor does she know if the boy takes
any medications on a regular basis. The child looks tired and sits upright on
the babysitter's lap. He is obviously comfortable with his caregiver. Vital
signs are temperature 38.7°C (101.7°F), pulse 110/min, respirations 28/min
and labored, and blood pressure 85/50 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air
shows an oxygen saturation of 89%. Physical examination discloses
inspiratory stridor. Neck is supple, tympanic membranes are normal, and
there is a profuse nasal discharge. Examination of the pharynx discloses no
abnormalities. Auscultation of the chest discloses equal air entry on both
sides and no crackles. The remainder of the examination shows no
abnormalities. Treatment with 40% oxygen via venturi mask and
intravenous fluids is initiated. Despite continued supplemental oxygen and
hydration, the child remains tachypneic and stridorous at rest. Oxygen
saturation is now 93%. In addition to administering racemic epinephrine by
nebulizer, which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

options.D: Practical dosing: dexamethasone 0.15–0.6 mg/kg PO once (often 0.6 mg/kg),

explanation:
Oral glucocorticoids are essential in moderate-to-severe croup for sustained
improvement beyond the transient benefit of nebulized epinephrine. Compared
with placebo, dexamethasone reduces croup scores within 2 hours, shortens
ED/hospital stay, and decreases return visits and need for additional treatments;
effective single-dose regimens range from 0.15–0.6 mg/kg (commonly 0.6 mg/
kg) given orally, with similar efficacy to nebulized budesonide.[1][2][3][4][5][6][7]
Aminophylline has no role in viral croup; antibiotics are not indicated unless
bacterial tracheitis is suspected; acetaminophen does not address airway
obstruction; and intubation is reserved for impending respiratory failure or
severe hypoxemia unresponsive to medical therapy.[1][2][3][6][8] Therefore, the
next best step is to administer a single dose of dexamethasone.
The following summary table from JAMA Pediatrics highlights the benefits of
glucocorticoids versus placebo in croup, supporting dexamethasone use for
symptom improvement and reduced return visits.
Table 1. Summary of Findings for Any Glucocorticoid Compared With Placebo for Croup
Used under license from JAMA Network.
Answer: Oral dexamethasone therapy (D)
Practical dosing: dexamethasone 0.15–0.6 mg/kg PO once (often 0.6 mg/kg),
with close observation after racemic epinephrine given its short duration of
effect.[1][2][4][5][6][7] Intubate only if worsening work of breathing, exhaustion,
altered mental status, or refractory hypoxemia develops despite optimal medical
management.[1][6][8]

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 3
A 30-year-old man comes to the office because of an itchy and painful
facial rash that has worsened since he first noticed it 1 day ago. He has no
other symptoms and says he otherwise has been generally healthy. Medical
history is unremarkable and he takes no medications. He does not use any
substances. He is unmarried and is not currently in a romantic relationship
but has been sexually active with multiple female partners during the past
year. Vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination shows an
erythematous macular rash with several 1- to 2-mm raised vesicles that do
not cross the midline over the left forehead and periorbital area. There are
no other abnormalities. Acyclovir therapy is initiated. Which of the
following is the most appropriate next step?
explanation:
Serum human immunodeficiency virus antibody test[1][2][3]
Unilateral vesicular eruption in the V1 dermatome with lesions not crossing the
midline is classic for herpes zoster ophthalmicus (HZO). In otherwise healthy
adults under 50, HZO should prompt evaluation for underlying
immunosuppression, particularly HIV. Observational data from regions with high
HIV prevalence demonstrate a strong association between HZO in younger
adults and unrecognized HIV infection, leading many experts to recommend
routine HIV screening in this context; early diagnosis alters prognosis and
management.[1][2][3] This patient’s sexual history further supports screening.
Dermatology referral, skin biopsy, or Tzanck testing are not the next best steps.
HZO is a clinical diagnosis; antiviral therapy has already been appropriately
initiated, and laboratory confirmation is rarely necessary unless the diagnosis is
uncertain. When testing is pursued, PCR/DFA outperform Tzanck for VZV, and
Tzanck has limited sensitivity and specificity for zoster.[1][2][4][5]
Prompt ophthalmic evaluation should also be arranged given the periorbital
involvement and risk of ocular complications, but among the listed options, HIV
testing is the most appropriate next step.

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 3
A 31-year-old man with a 5-year history of HIV infection comes to the
office because of anal pain, particularly on defecation, for the past 4
months. He says he has seen spots of blood on the toilet tissue but has not
had any other noticeable bleeding. He reports no change in bowel habits
and has not had recent fever, chills, or rectal drainage. He says he and his
partner engage in anal-receptive intercourse. His most recent CD4+ T-
lymphocyte count 2 months ago was 350/mm³; HIV viral load at that time
was undetectable. He currently is being treated with antiretroviral therapy.
He has had no opportunistic infections. Medical history is also significant
for syphilis and genital herpes treated with penicillin and acyclovir,
respectively. He does not smoke cigarettes or drink alcoholic beverages.
Vital signs are normal. Physical examination shows small bilateral inguinal
lymph nodes, but respiratory, cardiac, and abdominal examinations
disclose no abnormalities. There are several tender fleshy lesions around
the perianal area. Rectal examination produces tenderness, but there is no
rectal discharge. Test of the stool for occult blood is trace positive. Which
of the following is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy at this time?

options.B: is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy for this HIV-positive

explanation:
Imiquimod (B) is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy for this HIV-positive
patient with perianal warts, as it is effective, safe, and guideline-
recommended for the treatment of external anogenital warts in this
population.
Presentation with tender, fleshy perianal lesions, anal pain with defecation,
minor bleeding, and small inguinal nodes in a man with HIV who has receptive
anal intercourse is most consistent with perianal condylomata acuminata (HPV).
Anal warts are common in HIV and can be symptomatic.[1][2] The United States
Centers for Disease Control and Prevention recommends patient-applied
imiquimod for external anogenital warts, including perianal warts, and clinical
trials in HIV-positive men on ART demonstrate efficacy and acceptable safety
without increasing HIV replication or causing significant systemic effects.[2][3]
[4][5][6] Typical dosing is imiquimod 5% cream applied three times weekly at
bedtime, washed off after 6–10 hours, for up to 16 weeks; local skin reactions are
common and expected.[2][4][6]
Why other options are incorrect:
Acyclovir treats HSV; this exam lacks vesicles/ulcers typical of active
herpes.[7]
Levofloxacin and metronidazole target bacterial/anaerobic etiologies, not
HPV warts.
Penicillin treats syphilis; no chancre/condylomata lata or systemic signs.
For context, HSV antivirals (e.g., acyclovir) are indicated for genital herpes, not
HPV warts; the table below illustrates HSV regimens to contrast with the current
HPV diagnosis.[7]

underscore why acyclovir is not the right choice for HPV warts:
